Vervolgens wordt een 3 (conditie) x 2 (meetmoment) factorial mixed ANOVA gerapporteerd met significante hoofdeffecten en een significante interactie, wat consistent is met een differentiële verandering over tijd per conditie. Er worden post-hoc resultaten en Holm-correctie genoemd, maar de gerapporteerde p-waarden zijn intern deels onduidelijk/tegenspraak-gevoelig en niet volledig gerelateerd aan het model (bv. “geen significante verschillen” terwijl p=.007 wordt genoemd). In de rapportage ontbreekt ook essentiële uitkomstdetails zoals effectgroottes en volledige F/df-structuur voor de post-hoc/vergelijkingen, waardoor de statistische onderbouwing incompleet blijft.
results_style_notes: De taal is overwegend duidelijk en de structuur is logisch (assumpties → ANOVA → post-hoc → figuur). Er zijn echter meerdere inconsistenties in formulering van significantie en p-waarden (bijv. “geen significante verschillen” met p=.007), en sommige notaties zijn onnauwkeurig (p =< .001). APA/typografie voor tabelfiguurverwijzingen en consistentie van afkortingen/termen is beperkt: tabellen/figuren worden genoemd zonder verdere toelichting en er ontbreken enkele typische statistische elementen (effectgroottes/df’s voor post-hocs). Verder bevatten de tekstpassages enkele formuleringen die vaag of dubbelzinnig zijn (“duidt op”/“aanname… bevestigd”), zonder kwantitatieve ondersteuning zoals effectgroottes.
results_rubric_feedback: De rubric voor Results beoordeelt zowel correctheid/completeness als “in-depth knowledge” maar geeft weinig concrete, toetsbare criteria voor wat telt als minimaal noodzakelijke statistische rapportage (bv. effect sizes, df’s, type correctie, onderbouwing van post-hocs). De overgang tussen de prestatiebanden (50%, 75%, correct/complete) is daardoor lastig te onderscheiden omdat er niet expliciet is welke onderdelen minimaal moeten ontbreken om van band te wisselen. Overweeg een subrubriek of checklist op te nemen voor: (1) modelkeuze en consistentie met hypotheses, (2) volledige inferentiële rapportage (F/df/t/χ², p-notatie), (3) effectgroottes en betrouwbaarheidsintervallen, en (4) post-hoc methodiek (welke comparisons, welke correctie, binnen/buiten-sujets). Daarnaast zou de rubric kunnen verduidelijken hoe om te gaan met interne inconsistenties in significantie vs p-waarden: is dat automatisch een lagere band of afhankelijk van mate van impact?
discussion_content_summary: De discussie herformuleert het onderzoeksdoel en vergelijkt de resultaten met de verwachtingen: VRET laat wel een significante afname van sociale angstsymptomen zien, iVET niet, en iVET verschilt niet van de wachtlijst. De auteur koppelt de gevonden VRET-effecten aan plausibele exposuremechanismen (habituatie en inhibitory learning) en bespreekt vervolgens waarom iVET in deze studie minder effectief kan zijn bij adolescenten (intensiteit/veiligheidsgedrag). Daarnaast worden meerdere beperkingen genoemd (individualisering en meetmethode) en worden concrete suggesties voor vervolgonderzoek gedaan (scenario-aanpassing, aanvullende metingen, follow-up). Ten slotte wordt de klinische relevantie voor toegankelijkheid van zorg benadrukt, maar er is weinig reflectie op de mate waarin causale gevolgtrekkingen exact passen bij de (vermoedelijk) beperkte/gebrekkige effectrapportage uit de Results-sectie.
discussion_style_notes: De tekst is overwegend academisch, helder en logisch opgebouwd met duidelijke alinea-focus (doel/verwachtingen → resultaten → verklaring → beperkingen → implicaties). Er zijn enkele formuleringen die grammaticaal of typografisch stroef zijn (o.a. “zelfgerapporteerde”/spaties, “Indiidual” in de literatuur, en enkele lange of omslachtige zinnen), en consistentie in terminologie (VRET/iVET/wachtlijst) is goed maar niet altijd strak. APA-opmaak lijkt grotendeels aanwezig in de literatuurlijst, maar er zijn opvallende fouten in referenties (spelfouten, inconsistenties in informatietypen zoals “British Psychological Society (UK).”) die later de APA-score kunnen beïnvloeden. Er is daarnaast soms lichte redundantie (herhalen van habituatie/inhibitory learning in meerdere contexten) en enkele claims zijn relatief algemeen zonder kwantitatieve onderbouwing in de discussie.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De rubric voor Discussion beoordeelt zowel inhoudelijke correctheid (redenering naar conclusie) als “kritische reflectie” en “outstanding insight in limitations/future directions”, maar de bandbeschrijvingen blijven relatief breed zonder concrete indicatoren. Het criterium “all references are used responsibly” is lastig te operationaliseren: er staat niet expliciet of alleen bronnen die relevant zijn voor specifieke claims worden meegewogen, of dat ook de mate van voorzichtig taalgebruik (bv. effectclaims vs. hypotheses) telt. Ook is het onderscheid tussen Developing/Adequate/Good en Good/Excellent deels gevoelsmatig: er wordt geen expliciete drempel gegeven voor wat telt als “75% complete/coherent” of als “convincingly argued for”. Tot slot zou het helpen als er meer nadruk kwam op het matchen van discussieclaims aan de (statistische) resultaten: een concreet subcriterium hierover kan de beoordeling consistenter maken.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De taal- en stijleisen zijn nuttig, maar de drempels “vage/ambiguë statements” en het tellen van fouten (bv. “<5” of “>10”) zijn moeilijk consistent toe te passen zonder duidelijke definitie van wat als fout telt (typo vs. grammatica vs. APA-opmaak) en zonder voorbeeldbeslisregels. De criteria mixen daarnaast verschillende aspecten (structuur, grammatica, APA, ambiguïteit) in één band; het zou helpen om per subaspect (structuur vs. grammatica vs. APA vs. ambiguïteit) aparte, afzonderlijk schaalbare niveaus te gebruiken. Tot slot is het criterium “overall use of scientific language is at least 60/80% correct” relatief subjectief; concretere ankerpunten (bv. wat precies onder “scientific language” valt) zouden de betrouwbaarheid verhogen.

intro_content_summary: De introductie geeft een duidelijke achtergrond over sociale angststoornis bij adolescenten, motiveert relevantie (problemen in functioneren, zorgdrempels en vroege behandeling) en beschrijft het mechanistische kader van vermijdings-/veiligheidsgedrag en exposure. De lijn naar de onderzoeksvraag en testbare hypotheses is grotendeels coherent: exposure als behandeling, iVET als intensief, en VRET als mogelijk laagdrempeliger/efficiënter, waarna eerdere (volwassen) evidentie en de lacune bij adolescenten worden geschetst. De studieopzet en verwachtingen worden helder samengevat (VRET vs. iVET vs. wachtlijst; pre/post; randomisatie; primaire verwachting dat beide actief effectiever zijn dan wachtlijst en dat VRET iVET overtreft zonder baselineverschillen). Referenties lijken overwegend relevant en (meestal) actueel, al is het patroon van “meer up-to-date alternatieven” niet volledig te beoordelen zonder de gebruikte literatuurset te verifiëren. Overall is de inleiding wetenschappelijk onderbouwd en logisch, met een duidelijke focus en impliciete RQ/verwachtingen.
intro_style_notes: De taal is overwegend academisch en precies, met meestal correcte terminologie (SAS, iVET, VRET, DSM-criteria) en heldere opbouw per alinea (achtergrond → theorie → behandeling → lacune → doel/hypotheses). Er zijn wel enkele kleine formulering- en grammatica/afleidingsproblemen (o.a. “noodzaak van behandelvormen die beter aansluiten bij de behoeften adolescenten” zonder lidwoord; “zoals afwijzing of negatieve evaluatie” is inhoudelijk oké maar zinsstructuur kan strakker; meerdere lange zinnen die de leesbaarheid iets verminderen). APA-stijl lijkt grotendeels consistent met auteur-jaar, maar er is een inconsistentie/typografische afwijking in “American Psychiatric Association (APA), 2013” (dubbeling/verwijzingsvorm) en mogelijk ontbreekt een uniforme consistentie voor organisatie-auteurs in de hele tekst. Paragraphing en signposting zijn goed; redundantie is beperkt, maar sommige hypothesezinnen zijn wat lang en bevatten meerdere claims tegelijk.
intro_rubric_feedback: De rubric onderscheidt goed tussen incoherente vs. deels/volledig aanwezige redeneringslijn naar RQ en tussen correct/volledig vs. onvoldoende methodische/inhoudelijke beschrijvingen, maar de bandbreedtes zijn in de praktijk lastig omdat “scientific relevance/hypothesis present” en “mostly coherent” niet verder operationaliseren. Vooral bij Introduction zou een explicieter criterium kunnen helpen: bv. of de RQ expliciet geformuleerd is als vraag/claim met populatie, interventie/conditie en uitkomst. Ook is de drempel “meer dan de helft” en “alle references up to date” moeilijk te beoordelen zonder rubric-anker (hoeveel references telt mee, en wat is ‘outdated’). Tot slot zou een voorbeeld van wat als “80% conclusies overtuigend” telt in een introductiecontext helpen (introducties bevatten meestal geen ‘conclusies’ maar argumentstappen naar hypotheses).
methods_content_summary: De methodes beschrijven helder wie aan de studie deelnamen (N=120, leeftijd 13–16, inclusie/exclusiecriteria) en hoe zij werden geworven en toegewezen aan drie condities. De meetmethode (LSAS-CA) en het behandelkader voor VRET en iVET worden relatief concreet uitgewerkt, inclusief sessieaantallen, duur, opbouw via angsthiërarchieën en gebruikte VR-hardware/software. De procedure bevat de kernstappen (screening, randomisatie, pre/postmeting, behandelperiode, debriefing en wachtlijst-afhandeling). Er ontbreken echter meerdere methodologische details die nodig zijn om het design volledig verifieerbaar te maken (o.a. randomisatieprocedure, operationalisering van blinding/uitkomstbeoordeling, behandelfidelity/therapeut-tracking, en precieze timing/interval van metingen).
methods_style_notes: De tekst is overwegend duidelijk en specifiek, met passende technische termen voor het type VR-interventies en de meetinstrumenten. Kleine issues: inconsistent gebruik van opsomming/inspringing en enkele lange zinnen die preciezer gesplitst hadden kunnen worden. APA-formattering lijkt beperkt gecontroleerd (o.a. ontbrekende consistentie in afkortingen/italics en niet-gestandaardiseerde kopjes zoals ‘Participanten’ met variërende tab/indentatie), maar er zijn geen flagrante grammaticale fouten zichtbaar. Redundantie is beperkt, maar sommige herhalingen (bv. exposure op basis van angsthiërarchie in meerdere delen) kunnen strakker worden geformuleerd.
methods_rubric_feedback: De rubriek voor ‘Method’ is grotendeels passend, maar de bandbreedtes lijken wat gevoelig voor interpretatie van wat precies “75% complete” versus “complete” betekent. Het onderscheid tussen ‘lacks multiple important details’ en ‘lacks one or two important details’ blijft vrij subjectief zonder voorbeelden. Het criterium ‘no unnecessary details anywhere’ is lastig te beoordelen zonder expliciete richtlijnen voor wat als noodzakelijk geldt in PT:PR (bv. hardware-specificaties wel/niet). Overweeg daarom concrete voorbeelditems (zoals randomisatieprocedure, blindering, uitkomstbeoordeling, fidelity) en een expliciet wegingsmodel voor welke ontbrekende details het zwaarst tellen.
results_content_summary: De sectie beschrijft dat alle 120 deelnemers zijn meegenomen en geeft onderbouwde checks voor normaliteit en variantiehomogeniteit. Vervolgens wordt een 3×2 factorial mixed ANOVA op LSAS-CA-scores gerapporteerd met relevante F-waarden en significante hoofdeffecten en interactie. De post-hoc informatie is echter niet volledig consistent en mist cruciale koppelingen aan de mixed design (bv. verwarring in de gerapporteerde p-waarden bij “post-hoc” versus binnen-conditie verandering). Er wordt wel een Holm-correctie genoemd, maar de daadwerkelijke post-hoc vergelijkingen/structuur zijn onvoldoende transparant, waardoor de volledigheid en controleerbaarheid van de analyses beperkt is.
results_style_notes: De taal is overwegend duidelijk in het Nederlands en grammatica/spelling zijn grotendeels acceptabel, al komen er enkele onnauwkeurigheden voor (bv. “p =< .001”, en formuleringen die niet precies de statistische structuur volgen). De paragrafen zijn logisch gegroepeerd rond beschrijvende statistiek, assumptietests, ANOVA en post-hoc/interpretatie, maar sommige zinnen zijn dubbelzinnig door onduidelijke post-hoc specificatie (welke vergelijkingen exact). APA-lay-out is deels aanwezig met tabellen/figuurverwijzingen, maar de tabellen worden inhoudelijk niet gespecificeerd en er ontbreekt info over bv. effectgroottes en exacte presentatie van df/parameters consistent over alle analyses.
results_rubric_feedback: De rubriek voor Results onderscheidt vooral tussen “minstens 50% correct” en “75% correct/compleet”, maar geeft weinig concrete voorbeelden van wat telt als correct/incompleet (bijv. effectgroottes, df-consistentie, juiste post-hoc structuur, alignment met mixed ANOVA). Het criterium “in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses” is gevoelsmatig, maar mist duidelijke signalen/indicatoren (welke typen fouten of welke extra analyses leveren automatisch hogere band?). Ook is het onduidelijk hoe om te gaan met situaties waarin de globale ANOVA klopt maar de post-hoc/interpretatie niet goed aansluit; een explicieter weging per type rapportage zou de bandonderscheiding verbeteren. Tot slot zouden thresholds rond het rapporteren van effectgroottes (ηp²/Cohen’s d) en multiple comparisons transparanter kunnen zijn.
discussion_content_summary: De discussie herneemt het doel en koppelt de bevindingen aan de twee kernhypotheses: VRET toont een significante daling in sociale-angstsymptomen van pre- naar post, terwijl iVET niet verschilt van de wachtlijst. De lijn in de argumentatie is overwegend consistent: resultaten worden gekoppeld aan eerdere studies (vooral bij volwassenen) en aan mechanistische verklaringen (habituatie en inhibitory learning). Vervolgens formuleert de auteur plausibele verklaringen voor het uitblijven van effect bij iVET (intensiteit/confrontatie en mogelijk minder goed op gang komen van leerprocessen) en benoemt twee beperkingen (individualisering en zelfrapportage) met concrete implicaties voor vervolgonderzoek. De discussie sluit af met klinische relevantie en toegankelijkheidsargumenten voor VRET, maar de statistische claims (zoals “effectiever” en “niet significant”) blijven wat algemeen zonder expliciete herleiding aan de interventie-contrastlogica (bv. effectgroottes/vergelijkingen tussen condities).
discussion_style_notes: De taal is academisch en doorgaans helder, met een logische opbouw in alinea’s (doel/hypotheses → aansluiting literatuur → mechanismen → verklaring iVET → beperkingen → implicaties). Er zijn wel enkele formuleringen die net iets te stellig of algemeen zijn (“toont aan dat VRET effectiever was”) zonder exacte statistische onderbouwing in deze sectie, en af en toe lange zinnen die de leesbaarheid iets verminderen. APA-achtige referentieconsistentie lijkt redelijk, maar er zijn duidelijke typografische/taalfouten in de literatuurlijst (bv. ‘Yound’, ‘selfreportage’, ‘Indiidual’, ‘intens’ inconsistent gespeld/gezet) die niet direct in de discussie zelf staan maar wel de compliance voor het geheel beïnvloeden. Paragraafstructuur is over het algemeen duidelijk en er is weinig redundantie op sectieniveau.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De rubric voor Results/Discussion vraagt wel om correctheid en “in-depth knowledge”, maar definieert niet scherp welke elementen tellen als ‘correct’ of ‘complete’ rapportage in een Discussion (bv. moeten inferentiële toetsen, effectgroottes en specifieke contrasten expliciet terugkomen?). De bandgrenzen tussen “sound”, “mostly coherent” en “coherent” zijn relatief subjectief; het zou helpen om voorbeeldcriteria te formuleren (bijv. ‘hypothese-toetsen worden expliciet verbonden met condities en de RQ-antwoordlogica’). Ook is “all references used responsibly” een nuttige eis, maar zonder duidelijke drempel (hoeveel referenties mogen conceptueel zijn i.p.v. direct ondersteunend?) kan het beoordelen variëren tussen beoordelaars. Tot slot zou een korte checklist per sectie (Discussion: recap resultaten, verklaring, beperkingen, implicaties) de betrouwbaarheid van de grading verhogen.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De Language & Style criteria zijn nuttig, maar de drempels (“<5”, “<10”) zijn lastig om consequent toe te passen omdat tellen van fouten (bv. APA/typografie vs. grammatica) niet strak gespecificeerd is. Ook is “vage of onduidelijke statements” niet operationeel genoeg: voorbeelden of een korte definitielijst (wat telt wel/niet als cruciaal) zouden de beoordeling consistenter maken. Verder zou “APA style referencing or layout” baat hebben bij concretere subcategorieën (o.a. auteur-jaar, italics/afkortingen, tab/figuurverwijzingen) zodat beoordelaars dezelfde dingen meetellen.

intro_content_summary: De introductie geeft een begrijpelijke achtergrond over de prevalentie en klinische relevantie van sociale angststoornis bij adolescenten, en leidt vervolgens naar exposuretherapie en mechanismen (vermijding/veiligheidsgedrag, habituatie en inhibitory learning). De onderzoeksvraag en hypotheses zijn uiteindelijk wel aanwezig en grotendeels testbaar (VRET/iVET versus wachtlijst; VRET beter dan iVET), maar de logische afbakening richting een concrete RQ blijft impliciet en is niet strak geformuleerd als één centrale vraag. De theoretische onderbouwing voor waarom VRET mogelijk iVET overtreft is plausibel, maar sommige argumenten missen nog duidelijke koppeling naar precies welke uitkomstmaten en welke verwachtingen in welke richting (o.a. effectgrootte/sterkte) verwacht worden. Qua actualiteit en relevantie van de literatuur lijkt het merendeel passend, maar er zijn meerdere citaties waarvan niet geheel duidelijk is of ze direct aan de kernredenering bijdragen (waardoor het “nagenoeg alles up-to-date en relevant” niveau niet volledig gehaald wordt).
intro_style_notes: De taal is over het algemeen academisch en leesbaar, met goedlopende alinea-opbouw (probleem → theorie → behandeloptie → studies → doel/hypothesen). Er is lichte inconsistente formulering (“behoeften adolescenten”; “beter aansluit bij de behoeften adolescenten”) en enkele formuleringen die net wat onnauwkeurig of grammaticaal stroef zijn (bijv. “twee meetmomenten zijn: een prebehandelingsmeting en een postbehandelingsmeting”). APA-stijl lijkt grotendeels correct met auteur-jaar, maar er is een mogelijk formatprobleem met “American Psychiatric Association (APA), 2013” (dubbele aanduiding) en de tekst bevat niet consequent dezelfde naamgeving voor varianten/afkortingen. Redundantie is beperkt, maar sommige alinea’s (mechanismen exposure, VRET-iVET verschil) herhalen conceptueel dezelfde kern zonder telkens de onderzoeksvraag specifieker te verankeren.
intro_rubric_feedback: De rubric voor de Introductie bandbreedtes gebruikt termen als “duidelijk”, “coherent”, “meest/slechts deels sound” en percentages zoals “40/60/75/100% van de conclusies overtuigend” en “meer dan half van de references irrelevant of outdated”, maar het is niet volledig concreet hoe je “conclusies” telt in een introductie (vs. discussie). Ook is het onderscheid tussen “RQ duidelijk maar mogelijk vaag” en “RQ duidelijk en passend” deels subjectief zonder voorbeelden van wat als voldoende-specifiek geldt (bv. wel/niet opnemen van populatie, interventie, vergelijking, uitkomst). Tot slot zou het helpen om thresholds voor “up-to-date en relevant” specifieker te maken (bv. binnen een bepaald aantal jaren) of om een aparte subcriterium te maken voor “directe relevantie voor de RQ” versus “algemene theoretische achtergrond”.
methods_content_summary: De Methods beschrijft deelnemers (leeftijd, werving, inclusie/exclusie) en de drie condities met een duidelijke basisstructuur voor random toewijzing en meetmomenten (pre/post). De operationalisatie van sociale angst via de LSAS-CA en het gebruik van een cut-offscore worden genoemd, en er is een vrij gedetailleerde beschrijving van VRET (sessies, opbouw, software/hardware en scenario’s) en in algemene zin van iVET. De procedure is logisch opgebouwd rond inclusie, randomisatie, interventieperiode en follow-up/informatie. Wel blijven enkele methodologische kernpunten impliciet (o.a. randomisatieprocedure/allocatieverberging, behandelfidelity, exacte implementatie van iVET en details van meetmomenten zoals tijdsinterval en scoring/psychometrie in de steekproef), waardoor het ontwerp niet volledig toetsbaar wordt.
methods_style_notes: De tekst is grotendeels helder en concreet, met duidelijke kopjes en goedlopende zinnen. Er zijn enkele inconsistenties/typografische elementen (o.a. tabs/inspringing bij Materialen, en herhaling van protocol-achtige formuleringen), en enkele formuleringen zijn wat breed (bv. ‘goede psychometrische eigenschappen’ zonder specificatie). APA-stijl lijkt overwegend gevolgd (auteurs jaartal), maar de rubricering mist formele precisie zoals consequentie in afkortingen, en er zijn kleine onduidelijkheden rond termen zoals “DS﻿M-5 Childhood Disorders (SCID-5 Junior)” en “cut-offscore van 29,5” zonder context. Verder bevat de sectie relatief weinig grammaticale/spellingfouten.
methods_rubric_feedback: De rubric bij Methods onderscheidt bandbreedtes vooral op “coherent en (minstens) 75% compleet,” maar dat criterium is moeilijk operationaliseerbaar zonder concrete checklist (bv. randomisatie, interventietrouw/fidelity, maatinterval, scoring, analysetype). De descriptoren “incompleet” en “lacks multiple important details” overlappen sterk tussen aangrenzende bands; het zou helpen om specifieke voorbeelden van ‘belangrijke details’ te noemen (allocatie, blindering, trainings/monitoring therapeuten, exacte iVET-interventiecomponenten, timing van metingen). Ook is “er zijn geen onnodige details” scherp, maar zonder voorbeelden kunnen studenten/ beoordelaars daar verschillend mee omgaan. Tot slot zou een aparte subcriterium voor ‘controle van bias’ (bv. randomisatieprocedure, blinding van assessoren) de onderscheidbaarheid tussen bands vergroten.
results_content_summary: De resultaten rapporteren dat alle 120 participanten zijn meegenomen, inclusief een check op outliers en basisdescriptieven (Tabel 1). Vervolgens worden assumpties voor normaliteit en homogene varianties besproken en wordt een 3 (conditie) × 2 (meetmoment) mixed ANOVA gerapporteerd met F-waarden en p-waarden voor hoofdeffecten en de interactie. Ook worden (Holm-gecorrigeerde) post-hoc vergelijkingen beschreven en worden pre- vs post veranderingen samengevat per conditie. De statistische logica (interactie → differentiële verandering) is in grote lijnen consistent met de beschrijving van de opzet en de uitkomstmaat (LSAS-CA).
results_style_notes: De tekst is grotendeels helder en volgt een logisch verslagformat (datacleaning → assumpties → primaire analyse → post-hoc → interpretatie). Er zijn echter enkele taal- en precisieproblemen in statistische notatie (bijv. “p =< .001” en “p = .007” als significantieresultaat waar het ook niet significant verklaard wordt), wat kan leiden tot ambiguïteit. APA-niveau lijkt wisselend door het gebruik van decimal/p-waarderegels en onvolledige labels voor tabellen/figuren; ook ontbreken waar nodig effectgroottes of covariaten/structuurdetails voor de mixed ANOVA. Paragrafen zijn goed geordend, maar de post-hoc sectie bevat inhoudelijke inconsistenties die de interpretatie verzwakken.
results_rubric_feedback: De rubric voor Results bevat twee onderdelen: (1) correctheid/compleetheid/coherentie en (2) “in-depth knowledge van statistische analyses”. Dat tweede criterium is vrij breed en kan uiteenlopen; het zou helpen om concreet te maken welke elementen tellen (bv. effectgroottes, df-interpretatie, sphericity/ε bij mixed ANOVA, checks voor missing data, en correcte post-hoc/contraststructuur). Ook is er een bandgrens tussen “50% correct” en “75% correct” zonder duidelijke voorbeelden: een expliciete checklist (wel/geen effect sizes, juiste koppeling tussen design en df, consistentie van significantieconclusies) zou de beoordeling consistenter maken. Tot slot kan de rubric verduidelijken hoe om te gaan met kleine maar cruciale statistische inconsistenties (zoals p-waarden die niet overeenkomen met de uitspraak “geen significant verschil”).
discussion_content_summary: De discussie vat de belangrijkste resultaten samen in relatie tot de verwachtingen: VRET leidt tot een significante afname van sociale angstsymptomen, terwijl iVET niet en iVET niet verschilt van de wachtlijst. De auteur verbindt de gevonden effecten vervolgens met habituatie en inhibitory learning en probeert de (verwachte) effectiviteit van iVET bij volwassenen te vergelijken met de huidige adolescentenuitkomsten. Er worden meerdere beperkingen en suggesties voor toekomstig onderzoek genoemd (individualisering en meetmethode; follow-up). De sectie blijft echter op sommige punten te weinig analytisch/onderbouwend in het koppelen aan de statistische bevindingen (bv. exacte uitkomstpatronen en effectgrootte) en bevat formuleringen die de resultaten mogelijk te stellig interpreteren.
discussion_style_notes: De taal is grotendeels academisch en begrijpelijk, met duidelijke alinea’s (doel → resultaten → mechanistische interpretatie → beperkingen → implicaties). Er zijn enkele grammaticale/typenmerken (o.a. ‘Familıy’, ‘Indiidual’, ‘virtuele scenarios/scenario’s’ inconsistentie) en opvallende inconsistenties/typfouten in termen en referenties (bijv. ontbrekende voornamen/plaatsing in bronnen en mogelijk onjuiste verwijzingen zoals ‘Vidanalage’/varianten). APA-lay-out lijkt niet consequent (kopjes en literatuurlijst; sommige referenties bevatten formatafwijkingen) en de discussie bevat af en toe redundante herhalingen van dezelfde kernpunten. Over het geheel zijn de APA/taalconsistentie en precisie in meerdere zinnen niet “near faultless”.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Voor de rubric bij Discussion is het onderscheid tussen de banden soms lastig: termen als ‘at least 75% complete’ en ‘mostly coherent’ zijn onvoldoende operationeel zonder voorbeelden (bv. wat telt precies als ontbrekend vs. ‘bijna compleet’?). Ook is ‘kritische reflectie… overtuigend’ relatief subjectief; het zou helpen om concretere criteria te vragen zoals: bespreekt de student de direction/interaction vs. hoofdeffecten, rapporteert mogelijke meet-/uitkomstbiases gericht op de gebruikte analyse, en gaat men in op waarom iVET uitblijft met plausibele alternatieven. Daarnaast mist de rubric een expliciete eis voor het correct terugkoppelen naar specifieke resultaten/statistiek (bijv. effectgroottes of exacte patronen), terwijl dat in deze toepassing wel cruciaal is voor “wetenschappelijke redenering en argumentatie”.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De Language & Style criteria gebruiken vrij brede bandbreedtes (bv. “at least 60% correct” vs. “near faultless”) zonder duidelijke operationalisering voor wat telt als een fout. Drempels zoals “less than five vague/ambiguous statements” zijn lastig toe te passen omdat niet gespecificeerd is hoe “crucial parts” en “vage” precies afgebakend worden. Verder zou het helpen om APA/typografie-fouten (bv. decimal/p-waarde notatie, tabel/figuur labels, consistentie in afkortingen) als aparte subcriteria te benoemen, zodat beoordelaars vergelijkbaarder kunnen scoren. Tot slot mist er een expliciete richtlijn hoe om te gaan met inconsistenties/typo’s die in meerdere secties terugkomen (consistency vs. sporadic errors) binnen dezelfde rubricband.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear clinical motivation for studying social anxiety and treatment access, leading to a plausible practical research focus on alternatives to traditional therapy. It builds an argument linking inhibitory learning theory (Craske et al., 2014) to variability and then to VR-based exposure (VRET), culminating in a rationale for comparing self-led versus therapist-led VRET. The literature review is extensive and generally relevant, but it sometimes relies on comparative claims (e.g., differences across studies) that are not tightly synthesized into a single, explicit research question. The hypotheses are present in a testable form, yet the exact operationalization of “better” is not specified (e.g., which outcome/time points), and the stated RQ is not explicitly formulated as a single question or set of hypotheses grounded in prior findings.
intro_style_notes: The language is generally understandable with mostly correct grammar and academic tone, though there are some clarity issues from long, multi-clause sentences (e.g., early prevalence-to-impact transition; multiple “Therefore/Given these reasons” statements). APA referencing is mostly used appropriately in-text, but there are minor punctuation/spacing inconsistencies (e.g., extra spaces before commas) and occasional awkward phrasing (e.g., “therapist-le d VRET” type issues; “social anxiety symptoms” vs “SAD”). Paragraph structure is mostly coherent with topical progression, but the middle sections become somewhat repetitive and list-like (review of studies and sampling criticisms). Overall, the introduction is well-structured at the section level, but more concise synthesis and a clearly stated research question would improve scientific precision.
intro_rubric_feedback: Strengthen the scientific reasoning by explicitly stating the research question (or hypotheses) as a clear, single focal line that the reader can extract verbatim; right now, the RQ is implied rather than stated. Improve the logical bridge from the inhibitory learning model to the specific comparison self-led vs therapist-led by specifying why the hypothesized difference follows from “safety signals” in this exact design and outcome. Ensure that all reported prior findings are synthesized into the argument (rather than primarily summarized) so the reader can see exactly what gap remains and why the present study directly addresses it. Finally, make the hypotheses outcome-specific (e.g., which measure(s), expected direction, and when), so they are unambiguously testable given the method section.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section describes a coherent randomized controlled design with three conditions (self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, waitlist) and clear timing of pre- and post-assessments. Participants are screened using SCID-5-CV-based criteria with explicit inclusion/exclusion rationale, and group allocation/attrition are reported. The section also specifies the main outcome measure (LSAS-SR) and provides a reasonable overview of the VR exposure materials and session structure. However, important procedural and analytic details are missing for full scientific completeness (e.g., randomization procedure details, adherence/compliance, therapist qualifications/standardization, outcome timing granularity, blinding, and—critically—data analysis strategy).
methods_style_notes: Language is generally clear and understandable, with mostly correct scientific phrasing. There are some grammar/style issues (e.g., repeated/awkward constructions like “followed the entirety,” “often glancing over,” and occasional imprecision such as “evaluated thoughts” and “therapist looked back”). APA referencing is mostly present but could be improved for consistency (e.g., clarifying whether citations are formatted for software/program names and ensuring capitalization/italics consistency). Paragraphing follows the expected subheadings (Participants/Materials/Procedure), with some redundancy and several statements that would benefit from more precise wording.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Methods criterion would benefit from more concrete anchors distinguishing what constitutes “complete and correct” versus “mostly correct.” In particular, data analysis/plan components are not mentioned in the Methods rubric, yet they are essential for evaluating whether the method matches the hypotheses (and the paper’s results/discussion depend on them). Clarifying whether the rubric expects explicit statistical plan elements (e.g., ANCOVA/mixed models, handling missing data, effect size reporting, pre-registration) would make scoring more objective. Finally, adding small, specific checklist items (randomization method, allocation concealment, blinding, adherence measures, therapist training/standardization, intervention fidelity, and exact assessment time points) would reduce subjectivity across graders and bands.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports a factorial mixed ANOVA with condition (3 levels) and time (2 levels) and gives overall F-tests for main effects and the interaction, aligned with a pre–post RCT structure. It also mentions descriptive statistics (Table 1/Table 2), tests of assumptions (normality, sphericity, homogeneity), and follow-up (post-hoc) comparisons. However, key reporting elements are incomplete or unclear: degrees of freedom appear inconsistent (unrealistic denominator df values), no effect sizes or confidence intervals are provided, and the post-hoc strategy lacks detail (e.g., correction method, whether simple effects were tested). Additionally, there is no clear link to the actual analytical model assumptions required for repeated-measures factors (e.g., how sphericity applies with two time points) and no explicit reporting of the central descriptive numbers to substantiate claims.
results_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and uses standard scientific phrasing, but several statements are vague or underspecified (e.g., outlier handling rationale, details of post-hoc testing). There are multiple APA/formatting issues: Table 1/Table 2 are described but not actually presented with required statistics, Figure/table numbering/placement is inconsistent, and some sentences are grammatically awkward (e.g., 'for the integrity of the data'). Statistical notation includes problematic df formatting and lacks effect sizes, which can undermine precision. Paragraph structure is mostly clear, but redundancy and ambiguity occur around assumptions and post-hoc interpretation.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion would benefit from more concrete anchors for what constitutes “complete” statistical reporting (e.g., requirement for effect sizes such as partial eta-squared/omega-squared, and minimal reporting of model terms and degrees of freedom consistency checks). The rubric should more explicitly distinguish between acceptable coverage of assumption checks versus required reporting of the specific outcomes for each assumption. It would also help to clarify expectations for post-hoc/simple-effects reporting (e.g., multiplicity correction and how comparisons were selected) to reduce subjectivity in judging “mostly correct” versus “correct.” Finally, the rubric could separate data-analysis correctness from reporting completeness, because a section may be statistically correct but still fail the completeness expectations (or vice versa).
discussion_content_summary: The discussion makes a generally plausible interpretation of the main finding that both active VRET conditions reduced social anxiety and that therapist-led VRET outperformed self-led VRET, linking these claims to inhibitory learning. It also attempts to address a surprising waitlist increase with potential explanations and proposes reasonable future directions (larger samples, more therapists, broader treatment comparisons). However, the argumentation is sometimes overconfident given missing details from the Results (e.g., magnitude/uncertainty, model-based interpretation), and several claims are vague or not tightly tied to the specific statistical outcomes described earlier. The conclusion about overall effectiveness is asserted without carefully distinguishing “within-group change,” “between-group differences,” and any time × condition interaction evidence.
discussion_style_notes: Language is mostly understandable and academically toned, but there are several imprecise or awkward phrases (e.g., repeated “negative effect,” “inspired by previously conducted studies regarding VRET,” and “time of day of the session could prove to have different effects on how the therapist conducted the sessions”). Grammar is mostly adequate but contains numerous long, run-on sentences and inconsistent capitalization/formatting (e.g., tabs/indentation, “therapist-led VRET” vs spacing, and stray formatting before the References). APA compliance is weak in places: the in-text citation for Wolitzky-Taylor & LeBeau (2023) is not properly formatted, and the reference list includes an obvious formatting error (Wolitzky-Taylor, K., & LeBeau, R. (2023) is written with parentheses embedded). Paragraph structure is uneven: the first paragraph mixes interpretation and theory; later paragraphs become list-like and speculative without clear signposting tied to specific results.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Several rubric criteria are currently under-specified and could use tighter anchors. For example, “reasoning culminating in the main conclusion is correct” would benefit from clearer guidance on what counts as “correct” given statistical reporting gaps (e.g., requiring alignment with specific effect types: main effects vs interactions, within- vs between-group claims). The Discussion criterion could also be more concrete about what “critical reflection” entails (e.g., requiring at least two specific, design-linked limitations plus at least one alternative explanation that could be falsified/checked). Finally, language & style descriptors could be made more objective by distinguishing citation-format errors and formatting/layout issues as separate anchors from general clarity and redundancy.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criterion is mostly clear, but the bands (e.g., “mostly correct” vs “near faultless”) could use more concrete behavioral anchors such as a checklist (APA formatting consistency, presence of tables/figures, citation formatting, and run-on sentence limits). Some descriptors are somewhat subjective (e.g., “unstructured,” “numerous vague statements,” “hardly any redundancy”), so adding measurable thresholds or examples per band would improve reliability. It may also help to explicitly separate APA/layout problems from writing-level issues (grammar/clarity) since they can affect graders differently.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows adequate-to-good scientific reasoning in the Introduction, with a coherent theoretical motivation and testable hypotheses. However, the Results section contains substantial statistical reporting problems (inconsistent df, missing effect sizes/CIs, and insufficient specification of the post-hoc approach), which weakens the evidential basis for the Discussion’s stronger claims. The Discussion is plausible and engages inhibitory learning with some reflection, but it does not carefully disentangle within-group change from between-group and interaction effects given the limitations in the Results reporting. In line with the rubric’s performance bands, these major weaknesses in statistical reasoning and the evidential link to conclusions keep the paper in the Developing/Adequate range rather than Good/Excellent.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a generally coherent background on social anxiety disorder, its clinical and societal importance, and a rationale for targeting avoidance via exposure therapy. It links exposure outcomes to Craske et al.’s inhibitory learning model and then argues that VR-based exposure can better operationalize variability, motivating a focus on VRET for social anxiety. The literature review is mostly logical and leads to a clear need: comparing self-led versus therapist-led VRET relative to waitlist. However, the research question/hypotheses are not stated with full precision (e.g., no explicit RQ format; expectation is described, but operational specificity and testable hypothesis statements are somewhat implicit), and several claims are somewhat overgeneral or not tightly tied to the exact comparison being made.
intro_style_notes: Language is mostly clear and scientific, with a generally logical flow across paragraphs. Grammar and phrasing are largely acceptable, but there are some awkward constructions (e.g., “among which waitlists,” “web-based VRET… download an app onto a handheld device of their choosing,” and repeated/long sentences) and occasional imprecision (“almost exclusively” without support). APA referencing is mostly in-text and consistent, though parentheses/formatting and some phrasing could be tightened for precision and to avoid ambiguity (e.g., ensure DSM-5 and capitalization/terminology consistency). Paragraph structure is broadly appropriate, but there is some redundancy (especially in summarizing multiple prior studies) without always culminating in a clear, explicit RQ statement each time.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from clearer anchors for what counts as an “explicitly clear RQ” versus a “mostly implicit” RQ, since many introductions can be conceptually clear but not formally stated. The bands could also specify how much hypothesis testability (e.g., explicit directional claims, group comparisons, mediators/moderators) is required to move from “adequate” to “good.” Finally, reference-responsibility is mentioned, but the rubric could give concrete examples of irresponsible use (e.g., unsupported causal claims, mismatch between study design and claim) versus acceptable but rough literature synthesis.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section describes a three-arm randomized controlled design (self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, waitlist) with pre- and post-assessment, which is broadly consistent with the stated comparison in the introduction. Participant recruitment, screening/exclusion criteria, and attrition are reported, and the main outcome measure (LSAS-SR) is identified with basic item structure and scoring. The VRET intervention is described at a high level (session timing, number of sessions, home vs clinic delivery), but key details needed to evaluate fidelity and internal validity (e.g., randomization/sequence details, therapist qualifications/session structure, adherence/monitoring, VR exposure hierarchy, and control for assessment/expectancy effects) are missing or vague. The analysis plan is not included in this excerpt, which limits evaluation against the study design.
methods_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and uses mostly scientific phrasing, but several wording issues reduce precision (e.g., “new technology” wording, and unclear phrasing around “cut-off score … followed the guidelines of” rather than citing specific decision rules). There are occasional APA/reference-format inconsistencies (e.g., missing full details for SCID-5-CV cut-off determination and some citation formatting). Paragraph structure follows the expected Methods subsections (Participants/Materials/Procedure), yet there is redundancy and some ambiguity (e.g., waitlist receiving VRET “after 8 weeks” but the nature/timing of that assignment is unclear). Grammar is mostly correct with minor errors (e.g., “being used for purposes of being used,” and “having owned a VR headset, by which limiting bias” reads ungrammatically).
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric could better specify what constitutes “complete and correct,” since several common methodological elements (randomization procedure details, therapist/exposure protocol fidelity, adherence/engagement monitoring, blinding, and the statistical analysis plan) are crucial yet not explicitly anchored. The bands are currently somewhat subjective—e.g., “somewhat comprehensibly” versus “very comprehensibly”—so adding concrete checklists or example items for each level would improve reliability. Consider separating “Methods completeness” into measurable subcomponents (design, participants, intervention, outcomes/assessments, and analysis plan) and clarifying whether the rubric expects the analysis plan within the Methods section. Finally, wording like “coherent and somewhat related” would benefit from a clearer anchor tied to the RQ/hypotheses operationalization.
results_content_summary: The section reports that there were no dropouts and that all participants were included in a mixed ANOVA, and it identifies LSAS-SR as the dependent variable. It provides F-tests for main effects and the condition × time interaction, and it summarizes post-hoc comparisons and where significant differences occurred. However, the statistical reporting is incomplete/unclear (e.g., implausible degrees of freedom, missing effect size estimates, and limited detail on the post-hoc method and correction for multiple comparisons). Overall, the results are broadly aligned with the described factorial mixed design, but the analysis reporting is not detailed enough to fully verify or interpret the effects rigorously.
results_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and uses appropriate scientific terms, but several statements are vague (e.g., “Table 1 shows assumptions were met”) without specifying test statistics or thresholds. There are APA-style issues: Table/Figure captions are present but the reporting is inconsistent (e.g., tables referenced without including values/units; “Table 1 Per condition...” formatting is unclear). Grammar is mostly correct, though there are minor precision problems (e.g., “integrity of the data” and some unclear phrasing). Some redundancy occurs between the ANOVA description and subsequent narrative interpretation.
results_rubric_feedback: Results should include correct and transparent statistical reporting that matches the stated design, including plausible degrees of freedom and sufficient information to evaluate the model (e.g., effect sizes and confidence intervals). The rubric could benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as “complete” reporting (e.g., requirement to report partial eta-squared, specify post-hoc procedure, and mention sphericity correction where relevant). The descriptors for “mostly correct” versus “correct, complete” are currently a bit subjective; adding explicit required elements would reduce ambiguity. Additionally, clarifying how to judge alignment to Methods when the Methods excerpt lacks an analysis plan would help raters apply the criterion more consistently.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion connects the observed pattern of symptom reduction and the differential effect between self-led and therapist-led VRET to the inhibitory learning model, and it reiterates the main comparative takeaways (both active conditions improve vs waitlist; therapist-led stronger). It also provides plausible explanations for the unexpected waitlist increase (small sample, external events, therapist effects) and suggests future directions (more therapists, larger samples, more active CBT-comparators). However, the discussion contains several overgeneral or under-specified claims (e.g., “VRET has a negative effect” rather than “reduces symptoms,” and limited alignment to precise hypotheses/RQ), and it does not clearly address the limitations that most directly follow from the study’s statistical reporting and measurement/control logic. Overall, it is coherent and relevant but not tightly reasoned or fully consistent in its scientific argumentation.
discussion_style_notes: Writing is generally understandable and scientific, with a logical section flow (main findings → limitations → future research). There are notable wording/precision problems (e.g., “negative effect on social anxiety symptoms” is semantically incorrect; several sentences are vague or speculative without tying to the design/statistics). Grammar is mostly acceptable but there are some awkward phrasings (e.g., “effectiveness of VR” and “none of the waitlist conditions… showed any substantial difference” without careful conditional framing). APA compliance is mixed: the reference list includes formatting inconsistencies and a likely erroneous citation in Wolitzky-Taylor & LeBeau (2023) entry plus repetition of reference style elements; the in-text citations are consistent but the section lacks clear subsections with topical sentences throughout.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Strengths include a generally coherent linkage from results to theory (inhibitory learning) and a relevant limitations section that proposes concrete future improvements. To reach higher bands, the discussion should more explicitly restate the research question/hypotheses from the Introduction in testable terms and ensure every conclusion matches those exact comparisons and effect directions. The “unexpected waitlist” argument would be stronger with design- and stats-consistent reasoning (e.g., attrition/assessment effects, regression to the mean, measurement timing), and with avoiding semantically incorrect wording. Finally, tighten writing precision (especially effect phrasing) and reduce unsupported speculation; improve APA formatting consistency in the reference list.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criterion is generally helpful, but the bands could use more concrete anchors (e.g., specify what counts as “few” vs “numerous” vague statements, or how many APA/reference-format issues trigger each band). Consider separating grammar/clarity from APA compliance and table/reporting clarity, since those are distinct sources of style loss. Adding examples of ambiguous scientific statements (e.g., vague test reporting) and specifying expectation thresholds for structure (topical sentences, subsection focus) would reduce subjectivity.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a generally coherent theoretical rationale and a plausible argument for comparing self-led vs therapist-led VRET, but the research question/hypotheses remain insufficiently operationalized and tightly linked to the exact contrasts. The Methods section is only partly complete for evaluating internal validity (notably intervention fidelity/monitoring, therapist details, and key analysis information), placing it in the “developing to adequate” range. Results reporting is too incomplete/unclear (e.g., problematic df, limited post-hoc/correction/effect size information), which prevents a rigorous interpretation of the statistical evidence. The Discussion is relevant and coherent but sometimes overgeneralizes and does not directly address limitations arising from the statistical reporting and measurement/control logic; language/style appears generally adequate, leading to an overall “developing” holistic performance.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear topic and rationale: social anxiety disorder is prevalent, treatment is hindered by waitlists, and virtual reality exposure therapy may mitigate constraints while aligning with inhibitory learning theory. It explains the theoretical mechanism (variability in inhibitory learning) and argues why VRET could be effective for SAD, then narrows to the specific gap that existing self-led VRET work does not directly compare against therapist-led VRET. The research aim is stated (compare self-led vs therapist-led VRET vs waitlist), and hypotheses are logically derived from the discussion of inhibitory learning and “safety signals,” yielding testable expectations. However, the explicit research question/hypotheses are not formatted as discrete statements, and some parts of the literature review rely on somewhat thin or loosely justified critiques (e.g., sampling/exclusion criteria) rather than clearly connecting to the study’s design implications.
intro_style_notes: Language is largely scientific and understandable, with mostly correct grammar and appropriate terminology (e.g., VRET, inhibitory learning). APA elements are mostly present (author-year citations; DSM reference), but there are minor issues such as inconsistent capitalization/wording (e.g., “among which waitlists,” “Therefore, Bouchard et al.”) and occasional awkward phrasing (“humanitarian reasons,” “astronomical benefits”). Paragraph structure generally follows a logical progression from background → mechanism → technology → literature gap → current study, though some sentences are long and could be split for readability. APA formatting compliance for headings/subsections is not shown, but the narrative is coherent enough that style issues likely won’t dominate the Language & Style grade.
intro_rubric_feedback: Several rubric bands could be made more concrete by explicitly describing what qualifies as a “clear and relevant” RQ vs merely “somewhat clear,” and what level of hypothesis explicitness is expected (e.g., hypotheses stated verbatim vs implied). The rubric currently blends coherence, correctness, and convincingness, which can be somewhat subjective; adding anchors like “RQ stated in one sentence and aligned with the final hypotheses/design” would improve reliability. For Method-related items, it would help to separate “hypotheses are testable” from “design is coherent,” because students may state hypotheses clearly but omit design details elsewhere. Overall, the rubric would benefit from clearer distinctions between (a) correct but not yet tightly reasoned and (b) tightly reasoned with strong theoretical linkage, especially in the Introduction criterion.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section is generally coherent with the stated aim to compare self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, and a waitlist, and it includes key elements such as sample recruitment, eligibility screening, intervention dose (8 weekly sessions), and pre/post measurement timing. However, important methodological specifics are missing or underspecified, especially around randomization implementation details, the exact therapist involvement (e.g., adherence, guidance structure), and the VR program/exposure procedures (e.g., how exposures are selected, progression rules, and fidelity checks). The screening and exclusions are described, but the cut-off rationale and how SCID-5-CV was scored/translated into “19 out of 40” are not sufficiently operationalized. Overall, the design and measures are recognizable, but it is not yet complete enough to be fully reproducible and fully aligned with the hypotheses about inhibitory learning mechanisms.
methods_style_notes: The language is mostly understandable and uses appropriate scientific terminology, with generally correct grammar and spelling. The paragraph structure is clear at a subsection level (Participants/Materials/Procedure), and APA-style citations are included, though the hyperlink embedded in the randomization description and some phrasing are not fully consistent with typical academic style. A few statements are vague (e.g., “evaluation of thoughts,” “inspired by… albeit slightly tweaked,” and details of the VR program and therapist oversight). Some long sentences reduce precision, and redundancy appears in repeatedly stating assessment timing across conditions.
methods_rubric_feedback: Several rubric criteria would benefit from clearer, more concrete reporting standards (e.g., specifying what “complete and correct” entails for randomization, therapist-led vs self-led operationalization, and intervention fidelity). The Methods rubric currently bundles many disparate components (sampling, materials, procedures) into broad bands; adding anchors/examples for what counts as “somewhat comprehensible” versus “comprehensible” (or “nearly complete” versus “complete”) would reduce subjectivity. It may also help to explicitly mention key reproducibility items as separate sub-criteria (e.g., randomization concealment/allocation, blinding, therapist training/adherence, session structure specifics, and outcome assessment scheduling). Finally, the rubric could clarify whether mechanism-relevant methodological details (e.g., how inhibitory learning/safety-signal variability is implemented in the VRET program) are expected for top scores in Methods when such mechanisms are central to the RQ/hypotheses.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports a basic factorial mixed ANOVA structure (condition × time) and includes key test statistics for main effects and the interaction, aligning broadly with a pre–post design. However, the reporting is incomplete and internally inconsistent in several places (e.g., degrees of freedom values and missing/unclear reference to the repeated-measures structure), and effect sizes and exact estimated means/SEs are not provided. Post-hoc comparisons are mentioned but not specified in a way that clearly indicates the correct multiplicity correction or the specific post-hoc test used. Overall, the results are thematically relevant to the intervention comparison, but the statistical reporting is not sufficiently complete or precise to fully support reproducible interpretation.
results_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and mostly uses appropriate scientific terms, but there are several imprecise or potentially confusing formulations (e.g., “post-assessment results” outlier handling without justification, “integrity of the data”). APA formatting for tables/figures is partially correct (Table/Figure labels), but Table 1 and Table 2 content is missing and figure/table references are not consistently integrated. There are some grammatical/wording issues (e.g., “There was an significant…”), and the section has redundancy in describing the ANOVA basics without giving the corresponding results details (effect sizes, df structure, post-hoc method).
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as “correct, complete, coherent,” especially for common reporting elements in psych research (e.g., reporting effect sizes like partial eta-squared, specifying post-hoc procedures and multiplicity correction, and clarifying repeated-measures df conventions). The Results criterion conflates analysis correctness with reporting quality; separating “statistical appropriateness/correctness” from “reporting completeness/transparency” could reduce ambiguity. The df-based judgment (“relevant in terms of stated design”) is hard to operationalize without guidance on expected df reporting conventions for mixed ANOVA; adding examples or checklists would make grading more consistent across tutors. The top band (“in depth knowledge”) could specify concrete indicators (e.g., assumptions reporting with methods, missing-data handling, sphericity corrections where applicable, and consistent df/error terms).
discussion_content_summary: The discussion interprets the main effects by claiming that both self-led and therapist-led VRET reduce social anxiety, with therapist-led showing a stronger effect, which is broadly consistent with the inhibitory-learning rationale from the introduction. It also frames the waitlist increase in symptoms as an unexpected finding and proposes plausible explanations (sample size, life events, therapist idiosyncrasies). The section provides a limitations-to-future-research narrative (single therapist, VRET-only intervention contrast) and ends with a general conclusion about VRET’s potential. However, several conclusions are phrased without clearly tying back to the specific statistical outcomes (e.g., the strength/extent of effects), and some statements are overconfident given the earlier incompleteness of reporting.
discussion_style_notes: The writing is generally understandable and maintains mostly scientific language, but there are frequent grammar and phrasing issues (e.g., awkward constructs like “have a negative effect,” “wholly unexpected,” “as such,” and inconsistent numerals/formatting). Paragraphing is present but sometimes the topic sentence focus drifts (e.g., broad conclusions followed by multiple “however” sections), and redundancies appear (re-stating that VRET works multiple times). APA elements are inconsistent: there are missing in-text citation consistency issues and at least one likely citation formatting problem in the reference list (e.g., Wolitzky-Taylor, K., & LeBeau, R. (2023) appears to have an extra comma/format inconsistency), and the discussion references “the therapist who administered the therapies” with unclear gender/role wording. Overall, the section lacks precise referencing to specific Results details (which would also support Language & Style by reducing ambiguity).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Some rubric bands for Discussion rely heavily on broad judgments (e.g., “very convincing”) and could be strengthened with concrete anchors such as: (a) explicit linkage of each hypothesis/RQ to specific reported results, (b) specificity of effect interpretation (direction, magnitude, uncertainty), and (c) number and relevance of limitations with clear methodological remedies. The rubric could also better distinguish between “limitations listed” and “critical reflection” by requiring at least one limitation to be shown as potentially biasing the interpretation of the primary findings. Finally, the rubric could clarify how to grade discussion statements that are plausible but not supported by the (possibly incomplete) statistical reporting—e.g., whether such overreach should cap the score even if reasoning is otherwise coherent.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria mix broad aspects (clarity, structure, APA compliance) with highly subjective judgments (e.g., “impedes proper understanding,” “graceful style”) without concrete anchors. The rubric would benefit from clearer operational definitions and examples for what counts as “vague or ambiguous statements” versus “minor” ambiguity. APA-related bands could be anchored to specific, observable elements (e.g., consistent in-text citations, correct table/figure captions/numbering, reference list formatting). Finally, the redundancy criterion could be made more actionable by distinguishing between acceptable scholarly repetition and excessive sentence-level duplication.
final_grade_justification: Holistically, the paper shows a generally coherent theoretical rationale and a clear study aim in the introduction, but the argument leading to the hypotheses/research question is only partially operationalized. Methods are largely aligned with the comparison of self-led vs therapist-led VRET and waitlist, yet key procedural and fidelity details are missing, limiting scientific replicability. Results are thematically relevant but fall into the “developing/insufficient” band due to incomplete and internally inconsistent statistical reporting (e.g., df, missing effect sizes/means, insufficiently specified post-hocs). The discussion offers plausible interpretations and limitations, but several conclusions are not tightly and precisely tethered to the reported statistics, placing overall performance around the developing-to-adequate transition; language/structure appear adequate overall.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear topic rationale linking social anxiety prevalence and treatment access barriers (e.g., waitlists) to the need for alternatives. It develops a coherent theoretical argument through inhibitory learning and variability as a mechanistic rationale for why VRET may work, and then narrows to self-led vs therapist-led VRET due to an identified gap in the literature. The section largely leads to a research aim to compare self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, and waitlist, and it ends with logically derived expectations about relative effectiveness. However, the RQ/hypotheses are not stated in a fully explicit, testable format (e.g., with clear directional hypotheses framed against the specific comparisons), and some claims are underspecified or potentially overstated (e.g., uniform “increased efficacy” generalizations across disorders; interpretation of safety signals without directly operationalizing them).
intro_style_notes: Language is mostly scientific and understandable, with generally strong paragraph focus and logical progression from problem → theory → gap → study rationale. Grammar is mostly correct, but there are a few issues such as awkward phrasing (e.g., “among which waitlists”), occasional imprecise wording (“humanitarian reasons, there are also great economical benefits”), and minor punctuation/spacing problems. APA-style referencing appears generally consistent with author–year and italics/formatting largely present, though there are some inconsistencies in capitalization and punctuation (e.g., “VR, almost exclusively,” and some comma spacing). The introduction is well-structured overall, but it contains redundancy and long chains of exposition where shorter topic sentences and clearer signaling of the RQ/hypotheses would improve precision and reduce ambiguity.
intro_rubric_feedback: Several rubric criteria for the introduction could be made more concrete to improve grading consistency. In particular, bands for “RQ clarity” could explicitly distinguish between (a) presence/absence of an explicit RQ statement and (b) whether hypotheses are stated as testable propositions with clear variables and comparisons. The criterion also mentions “irresponsible use of references,” which is somewhat subjective; adding anchors like “claims not supported by cited work,” “overgeneralizations from single studies,” and “missing citations for key numerical estimates” would help. Finally, the band descriptions blend RQ quality and hypothesis quality; separating these into distinct sub-criteria (RQ vs hypotheses) would make the rubric more diagnostic.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section presents a basic coherent structure: recruitment and screening, random assignment to self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, or waitlist, and a pre/post assessment schedule. However, key methodological details needed to evaluate validity and replicability are missing or underspecified, including the exact cut-off definition/SCID-5-CV operationalization, randomization procedures beyond allocation, and dropout handling. The procedure describes session timing and broad content differences between conditions, but it lacks critical information on therapist role (e.g., guidance scripts, fidelity), VR task protocol (e.g., hierarchy, exposure duration/intensity), adherence, and blinding. Overall, the design appears conceptually aligned with comparing self-led vs therapist-led VRET and a waitlist, but the absence of complete, testable operational details prevents the methods from being “complete and correct.”
methods_style_notes: Language is generally understandable with an appropriate scientific tone, but there are several precision issues (e.g., unclear phrasing like “new technology bias,” and ambiguous references such as “newspaper ads… and on-campus recruiting” without detail). Grammar is mostly acceptable, though there are minor inconsistencies and awkward constructions (e.g., “indicating at least a moderate severity,” “often glancing over to the participant”). APA formatting is inconsistent: some citations are embedded in parentheses without full APA conformity, and there is a raw URL in the text. Paragraphing is structured by headings (Participants/Materials/Procedure), but some sentences are long and information-dense, increasing ambiguity.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Methods criteria are helpful but could use clearer anchors that distinguish “nearly complete” from “complete and correct” (e.g., specifying what counts as adequate operationalization of inclusion criteria, randomization, and analytic plan). It would also benefit from explicit mention of common required methodological elements for intervention trials (e.g., therapist qualifications/role, intervention fidelity, adherence monitoring, and handling of missing data) so bands are not judged solely on overall completeness. The “coherent and related” wording is somewhat subjective; adding sub-criteria (study design alignment, replicability, and internal validity) with concrete checklists would reduce rater variability. Finally, considering that Methods often includes analysis details, the rubric could clarify whether the “Method” section should include a statistics plan or whether that belongs strictly under Results/Discussion.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports a factorial mixed ANOVA with condition (3 levels) and time (2 levels) using LSAS-SR as the outcome, and it states that relevant assumptions were tested. It provides F-statistics for main effects and the condition-by-time interaction and summarizes post-hoc comparisons in a general way. However, the statistical reporting is internally questionable (e.g., implausible degrees of freedom and no effect sizes, confidence intervals, or correction details), and the link to the described design is incomplete (e.g., no specification of how the mixed model handles repeated measures beyond labeling “mixed”). Tables and figures are referenced but not actually provided here, leaving reporting completeness and transparency difficult to evaluate.
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and uses appropriate scientific terms, with a mostly coherent paragraph structure (design → assumptions → main effects → post-hoc). There are some APA/layout issues: tables and figures are referenced but not properly formatted/presented, and numbering/indentation appears inconsistent (e.g., Table 1 line break and stray tab characters). A few vague formulations reduce precision (e.g., “Boxplots showed one outlier… not excluded,” but no criterion is given), and there is some redundancy (assumptions mentioned without quantitative results). Grammar and spelling are mostly correct.
results_rubric_feedback: Some rubric anchors for Results would benefit from more concrete, behavior-based indicators (e.g., explicit requirements for reporting effect sizes, multiple-comparison adjustments, and correct/consistent df). The current bands partly depend on “mostly correct” and “mostly complete,” which can be interpreted subjectively; adding checklist-style elements (model specification, df correctness, F/t statistics, p-precision, post-hoc method) would reduce ambiguity. “Demonstrating an in depth knowledge” is a bit broad—defining what qualifies as in-depth (e.g., partial eta-squared, estimated marginal means, planned contrasts) would make grading more consistent across tutors. Finally, the rubric could clarify whether referencing tables/figures without including them counts against “complete and correct,” since this strongly affects interpretability.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion links the observed condition-by-time pattern to the inhibitory learning model and provides a coherent interpretation that both VRET formats reduce social anxiety, with therapist-led VRET showing greater reduction. It reasonably addresses the study’s purpose and includes a clear, relevant account of limitations (waitlist change, single-therapist effects, and restricted treatment comparison), and it ends with an applied conclusion about accessibility. However, several claims are either too broad or not tightly grounded in the specific statistical results (e.g., stating “negative effect” and hypothesized patterns without noting directionality/effect size or exact contrasts). The waitlist explanation relies on plausible but speculative mechanisms without connecting them to specific measures or controls, and parts of the text would benefit from tighter alignment with the introduction’s stated hypotheses and constructs.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and mostly maintains a scientific tone, but there are frequent imprecisions (e.g., “negative effect” phrasing; “mirroring” without specifying what statistical comparison) and some awkward wording. Grammar and punctuation are sometimes problematic (notably a dangling phrase: “especially the group that, be it due to…”). APA-style is largely inconsistent within the text (e.g., inline reference punctuation and a repeated “References” list rather than discussion-only citations), and there is some redundancy (multiple “hypothesized” supports) and occasional overextension (“promises to be an excellent step forward for the field”). Paragraph structure is mostly clear (limitations listed in sequence), but transitions and topical focus could be sharper.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Strengthen the logical chain between results and conclusions by explicitly tying each main claim to the specific tests/contrasts reported (and, ideally, effect sizes/CI where appropriate). Improve hypothesis alignment: restate the study’s directional expectations from the introduction and show how each is supported or not, including what the interaction means substantively. For limitations, move from speculation to testable alternatives (e.g., suggest specific follow-up measures, procedural checks, or analytic remedies) rather than broad conjectures. On style, reduce vague/absolute phrasing and ensure every major statement is precise, grammatically clean, and consistently cited in APA format.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criterion is helpful but still a bit broad: “scientific language use impedes proper understanding” and “numerous errors” would benefit from more concrete thresholds (e.g., count of APA/reference/layout errors per page). The band descriptors (Developing/Adequate/Good/etc.) are not fully anchored to observable markers like the proportion of paragraphs with topical sentences or the frequency of vague/ambiguous statements in critical sections. Consider splitting “APA style referencing or layout” into separate sub-criteria (in-text citations vs. reference list vs. figures/tables formatting) to reduce subjectivity and improve reliability across tutors.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper demonstrates a coherent theoretical motivation and largely sensible interpretation of results in the discussion, which supports a band around Developing-to-Adequate scientific reasoning. However, the study aims/hypotheses are not fully operationalized in testable, comparison-specific form, and the Methods section lacks key details needed for validity and replicability (randomization, operational cut-offs, VR/task protocol, therapist fidelity, adherence/blinding, and dropout handling). The Results reporting is statistically questionable/incomplete relative to the claimed design (implausible degrees of freedom, limited effect information, and insufficient transparency), which weakens the evidential basis for the main conclusions. Language/style appears generally acceptable, but because the core scientific reasoning and statistical communication are not consistently at an Adequate/Good level across sections, the holistic grade is set at 5.5.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a coherent rationale for studying VRET for social anxiety, starting from prevalence, treatment barriers (waitlists), and the need for alternatives. It links exposure therapy to Craske et al.’s inhibitory learning model and identifies variability as a key mechanism that VRET may support. The section also builds a literature-based gap: most prior VRET studies for SAD are therapist-led, while self-led VRET evidence is limited and does not directly compare to therapist-led VRET. However, the research question and hypotheses are not stated as a single, clearly phrased RQ/Hypothesis set, and some claims (e.g., about what the safety-signal mechanism implies for outcome differences) would benefit from more precise operationalization and tighter logical steps.
intro_style_notes: Language is generally clear and mostly scientific, with a logical progression across paragraphs. Grammar/spelling issues and phrasing problems occur (e.g., repeated/awkward constructions like “waitlists” and “besides humanitarian reasons, there are also great economical benefits,” and a few tense/wording inconsistencies such as “does not go away completely, but in fact get accompanied”). APA elements are mostly present for citations, but section-level structure lacks an explicit RQ/hypothesis statement and some references are used in ways that are not always tightly anchored to specific claims. Redundancy appears in places (e.g., reiterating that VRET allows variability and listing multiple VRET study summaries before stating the specific gap).
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from clearer anchors for what counts as an “explicit, testable” RQ/hypothesis (e.g., requiring a statement like “RQ: …; Hypotheses: H1…, H2…”). The criteria also blend multiple constructs in a way that can be hard to judge consistently (e.g., “line of reasoning culminating in the RQ” vs. “scientific relevance and/or hypothesis is present”), so separating these into more observable sub-dimensions would improve reliability. Band descriptors could be made more concrete by specifying example features for each level (e.g., “Good: RQ in one sentence; hypotheses mapped to IV/DV; gap explicitly stated in last 1–2 paragraphs of intro”). Finally, the rubric might clarify how to treat responsible vs. irresponsible reference use (e.g., whether unsupported causal claims from correlational work are penalized).
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a mostly coherent overview of the study design, including setting, random allocation to self-led VRET, therapist-led VRET, and waitlist, and the timing of pre- and post-assessments. Participants, screening/exclusion criteria, sample size after dropout, and the primary symptom measure (LSAS-SR) are described with reasonable clarity. The VRET “program” and general session structure are outlined, and the procedure broadly matches a pretest–posttest comparison across conditions. However, important methodological specifics are missing or underspecified (e.g., therapist qualifications/role details, intervention fidelity, randomization/stratification/sequence generation details, handling of dropout, and key outcome/assessment timing for follow-up or baseline equivalence), which limits alignment with a hypothesis-driven scientific standard.
methods_style_notes: Language is generally clear and understandable, with mostly correct grammar and a scientific tone. APA compliance is partly inconsistent (e.g., capitalization/hyphenation in terminology; “Final participation spread” phrasing; missing some standard formatting details such as consistent reference style for web links and instrument descriptions). Paragraph structure is logical (Participants/Materials/Procedure), but there is some redundancy and occasional imprecision (e.g., “being used for exposure therapy,” “tweaked further,” “often glancing over,” and the inconsistent granularity of describing sessions). Overall, it is readable, but some claims would benefit from more precise, operational language to support later language-and-style grading.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Methods bands are generally well-calibrated to the presence of a coherent design and completeness of participant/material/procedure details, but the descriptors could be made more concrete by specifying which common methodological elements are expected at each level (e.g., randomization details, intervention fidelity, outcome timing, handling missing data). The scale for “complete and correct” could benefit from clearer anchors/examples, since “mostly correct” vs “complete” is still somewhat subjective without enumerated checklist items. “Participants, materials and procedures described comprehensibly” could be tightened to clarify whether measurement properties, inclusion/exclusion operationalization, and therapist/intervention specification count as “materials” vs “procedure.”
results_content_summary: The section reports descriptive information (no dropouts; one outlier retained) and states a 2×3 factorial mixed ANOVA using LSAS-SR mean scores with condition and time as factors.
