The outcomes and intervention components are described, but the data analysis plan is only briefly stated (“multiple regression”) without sufficient detail to confirm it matches the pre/post design. For the rubric, some band distinctions could be clearer by explicitly listing “essential method details” (e.g., randomization procedure, blinding, adherence, therapist qualifications, handling missing data) and by defining what counts as “one or two important details” vs “multiple important details.” Additionally, the Results criterion references statistical reasoning, but the Methods criterion could more directly separate “completeness” from “statistical alignment,” to make grading less subjective between adjacent bands.
results_content_summary: The section reports a mixed ANOVA on LSAS with a clear dependent variable and specified between- and within-participant factors, including tests of normality (Q-Q plots) and homogeneity (Levene’s test). It reports the key omnibus effects (main effects and the time × condition interaction) and summarizes post-hoc comparisons with Holm correction. However, the reporting is internally inconsistent with the ANOVA degrees of freedom (e.g., F(1, 99) despite N = 99) and the post-hoc results are not fully coherent (e.g., repeated/possibly misstated p-values and ambiguous SVRET vs TVRET comparisons). The results are also largely unlinked to the repeated weekly/session-level anxiety measures described in Methods (which are not reported here).
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and mostly uses appropriate scientific phrasing, with understandable variable naming (SVRET/TVRET, LSAS, pre/post). There are several APA/technical issues: incomplete/placeholder tables (Table 1/2, Figure 1 without contents), inconsistent punctuation/spacing in p-values, and at least one incorrect degrees-of-freedom pattern and some ambiguous comparison reporting. Paragraph structure is readable (assumptions → main ANOVA → post-hoc), but redundancy appears (multiple statements repeating “in line with the hypothesis” without adding new quantitative detail).
results_rubric_feedback: The “Results” banding could be clarified by specifying what counts as “≥75% correct” (e.g., correct test statistics/dfs, alignment with design, coherence between omnibus and post-hoc, correct multiplicity handling). The criterion currently mixes two dimensions—procedural reporting adequacy (matching the stated design) and statistical/knowledge depth—without separate thresholds, which makes intermediate judgments (like partial correctness) harder. It would also help to explicitly mention whether reporting of effect sizes (e.g., partial eta-squared) and assumption checks are required for the higher bands. Finally, the rubric could define how to evaluate inconsistencies (e.g., degrees of freedom or p-value formatting) and whether omission of secondary outcomes (weekly anxiety) should systematically lower the score.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion interprets the results as showing both SVRET and TVRET reduce social anxiety symptoms and that SVRET is superior to TVRET and waitlist, aligning this with a self-efficacy account (Bandura). It attempts to contrast the findings with prior work, raises plausible limitations (sample age range, lack of follow-up, absence of manipulation checks, and missing FNE outcomes), and proposes future studies to address these issues. However, the reasoning is often only loosely tied to the reported analyses and the paper’s stated hypotheses/RQ structure, and some theoretical claims are imprecise or potentially mismatched (e.g., interaction theory is not convincingly tested or operationalized). References are used broadly, but several sections make strong causal or directional claims without sufficient evidential grounding from the study design as described.
discussion_style_notes: Writing is generally understandable but contains frequent grammatical issues (e.g., run-on sentences, awkward phrasing, and inconsistent capitalization/plurals), and there are several unclear or incorrect terms (e.g., “worked significantly better,” “therapy changed from online to in vivo which was not the case”). APA formatting is inconsistent: references include non-APA sources/URLs (and one APA 2017 entry without clear author details), in-text citations vary in style (comma/ampersand consistency, missing parentheses, inconsistent “et al.” formatting), and punctuation/spacing is uneven. Paragraph structure is mostly readable, but there is redundancy and several long sentences that reduce precision. Overall, clarity and scientific precision are not consistently achieved for crucial claims, especially when discussing mechanisms and limitations.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Several rubric distinctions are hard to apply precisely. For example, the “line of reasoning… culminating in the main conclusion” bands mix coherence, correctness, and evidential adequacy, but do not specify how to judge when conclusions are partially correct yet insufficiently supported by the described results (a common student-papers scenario). The reference criterion (“relevant and up to date” / “used responsibly”) would benefit from a clearer threshold definition (e.g., what counts as outdated, and how many references may be loosely related before it drops a band). Finally, for language/style, the rubric treats “vague or ambiguous statements” as a count threshold, but does not clarify whether “vague mechanism claims” and “vague limitation justifications” count equally or whether crucial-vs-noncrucial language should have separate weighting.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric could be made more actionable by giving clearer, more concrete examples for what counts as “vague or ambiguous statements,” “crucial parts,” and the thresholds “five to ten” vs “more than ten” errors (e.g., specifying whether APA spacing/punctuation variants count separately). The criterion “Overall use of scientific language is at least 60% correct” is hard to operationalize without defining how to estimate the percentage. Consider splitting APA/layout errors into two subcategories (in-text citation formatting vs reference list vs punctuation/spacing), since students’ difficulties may differ across them. Finally, the redundancy threshold (“some redundancy” vs “a lot of unnecessary redundancy”) would benefit from example patterns (sentence-level vs paragraph-level vs conceptual repetition).

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a general rationale for studying Social Anxiety Disorder (SAD), summarizes CBT/exposure and virtual reality exposure, and motivates the need for more accessible formats (online/self-led) due to delays in care. However, the research question/hypothesis section is difficult to follow because the paper mixes background, multiple prior studies, and comparisons without clearly tightening to a single, explicitly stated gap. The stated hypotheses at the end are testable (improvements vs waitlist; equality between SVRET and TVRET), but the logical derivation from the earlier argumentation is partial and the discussion contains several methodological/statistical claims that are not sufficiently supported or clearly scoped to the RQ. References are present but not consistently recent/relevant for every claim, and some claims (e.g., waitlist effects or accessibility “interaction theory” arguments) are not sharply tied to the study’s variables and design.
intro_style_notes: Language is generally understandable but contains frequent grammar issues and non-native phrasing (e.g., “people their,” “TVRET condition,” “their help is lower”), as well as inconsistent capitalization (e.g., “TVRET/SVRET” and “VRET”). Paragraph structure is inconsistent: parts that should be background vs gap vs hypotheses are blended, and several sentences become long with multiple ideas. APA style is not fully compliant (e.g., “z.d.” usage, inconsistent spacing around years, and some citation formatting like “Patel et al. (2022)  shows” and “American Psychology Association, 2017” lacks consistent style details). There is also some redundancy and ambiguity in crucial places (e.g., what exactly distinguishes the conditions, and which prior findings directly inform the current comparison).
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction criteria would be more reliable if the rubric separated (a) whether the RQ/hypotheses are explicitly stated from (b) whether the reasoning connecting them is convincing. Consider clarifying what counts as “relevant and up to date” with a threshold (e.g., last 10 years, or proportion explicitly matched to SAD/VRET/self-led exposure) and distinguishing outdated references from merely tangential ones. The “conclusions to argumentation are convincing” phrasing could be operationalized more concretely (e.g., require clear gap statements and direct linkage to hypotheses). Finally, the Intro band descriptors could specify how much of the introduction must be coherent (e.g., “single thread culminating in RQ”) rather than relying mainly on broad coherence judgments.
methods_content_summary: The methods section describes a randomized allocation to SVRET, TVRET, and a waitlist, with a 10-week intervention and outcomes measured using the Liebowitz Social Anxiety Scale (LSAS) plus brief in-session anxiety ratings. The study design is broadly coherent with a treatment-comparison research aim, and key materials (VR headset, scenario sets, number of scenarios) are mentioned. However, important methodological details are missing or unclear (e.g., full participant diagnostic procedure, comorbidity/exclusion operationalization, randomization method and concealment, therapist qualifications, session fidelity, VR setup specifics, and what happens in the waitlist during the 10 weeks beyond being informed). Data analysis is stated only as “multiple regression” without specifying the actual model structure, covariates, handling of missing data, or pre/post-time structure. Overall, the section is around the minimum completeness threshold but not “complete and correct” as required for the higher rubric bands.
methods_style_notes: Language is generally understandable but includes multiple grammar and clarity issues (e.g., sentence fragments, inconsistent capitalization, and awkward phrasing such as “VR-listeners”). APA style is inconsistently applied (e.g., spacing in citations, missing consistent punctuation/format, and some unclear reference year formatting like “Kan et al. ( 2025)” and “Kan et al, 2025”). Paragraph structure is present (Participants/Materials/Procedure), but there is redundancy and some unclear variable definitions (e.g., what exactly the anxiety ratings are used for vs how they are analyzed). Several crucial terms are used loosely without operational clarity, which will likely hinder precision in reporting.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Method criterion focuses on coherence and completeness, but it is difficult to distinguish “somewhat related” vs “coherent and mostly related” without explicit anchors. It also states “methods are at least 75% complete” but does not define what counts as a “multiple important details” vs “one or two important details,” making the 75% threshold somewhat subjective. The rubric would benefit from examples of what constitutes core vs non-core methodological details for this course level (e.g., randomization procedure, primary outcome definition, intervention fidelity, and statistical model specification). Finally, the rubric mixes study design coherence with participant/material/procedure completeness, so weighting clarity could be separated to reduce subjectivity.
results_content_summary: The section reports a mixed factorial ANOVA with a between-participant condition factor and a within-participant pre/post factor, along with tests of normality and homogeneity. It provides F-tests for main effects and the interaction, and it describes several Holm-corrected post-hoc comparisons linking findings to the hypotheses. However, critical information is missing for evaluating alignment with the described design and for interpreting the statistics: the df values are inconsistent with the stated sample size and model, the treatment of the repeated-measures covariance/sphericity assumption is unclear, and the reported post-hoc p-values are not presented with full contrast details or consistent notation. Tables/figures are referenced but not actually included, and effect sizes, confidence intervals, and actual mean/SD values are omitted.
results_style_notes: Writing is generally understandable and mostly grammatical, but statistical reporting contains multiple precision issues (e.g., inconsistent/incorrect degrees of freedom notation such as F(1, 99) for a 3-level between factor, and p-value formatting like “p = < .001”). APA compliance is weakened because Table 1/2 content and the figures are missing, and section elements (e.g., headings/captions, consistent abbreviations like SVRET/TVRET) are not fully specified. Paragraph structure is acceptable, though there is some redundancy and several vague transitions (e.g., 'Indicating that...').
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion would benefit from clearer thresholds for what counts as “at least 50% correct” versus “75% correct,” given that partial correctness can depend heavily on whether df, factor levels, and repeated-measures assumptions are correct. The criterion could more explicitly require effect size reporting and proper post-hoc contrast specification as separate sub-bullets, rather than being bundled into 'in-depth knowledge.' “Correct, complete, coherent and relevant” is somewhat overlapping with “Demonstrating in-depth knowledge,” so separating correctness/completeness from sophistication could reduce ambiguity when students report only partial statistics. Finally, the rubric might specify what minimum reporting is expected for a mixed ANOVA (e.g., exact df for each effect, covariance approach, sphericity/repeated-measures correlation, and effect sizes) to make grading more consistent.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion restates the study purpose and broadly links the results to the stated hypotheses, especially the superiority of SVRET over TVRET and both over waitlist. It provides some theoretical interpretation using Bandura’s self-efficacy and Vygotsky’s interaction/zone of proximal development, and it proposes plausible limitations (age range and lack of follow-up). However, it repeatedly makes causal/theoretical claims that are not directly tested by the study (e.g., manipulation of self-efficacy/interaction mechanisms, or fear of negative evaluation) and some comparisons to prior studies are not sufficiently qualified by differences in paradigms, outcomes, and timing. The limitations and future directions are relevant but sometimes speculative and occasionally not fully aligned with what was actually measured in the present design.
discussion_style_notes: The writing is generally understandable but contains frequent grammar issues (e.g., run-on sentences, awkward phrasing such as “SVRET does also work,” and inconsistent verb tense). APA formatting is inconsistent: citations are sometimes missing the correct year formatting (e.g., “Lindner et al, 2018”), capitalization varies (LSAS vs FNE explanations are inconsistent), and the reference list includes some sources that appear web/unclear for scientific claims. Paragraphing is uneven, with several long paragraphs that mix interpretation, literature review, and future directions, reducing clarity. There are also several vague or ambiguous statements in crucial parts (e.g., what exactly is concluded regarding mechanisms and why), plus occasional redundancy in repeating results and limitations.
discussion_rubric_feedback: Some rubric descriptors are overlapping in ways that make band boundaries fuzzy—especially between “nearly complete, somewhat coherent” and “mostly coherent/complete,” and between “50% conclusions convincing” and “75% conclusions convincing,” which are subjective without concrete anchors. The Discussion criteria mix multiple constructs (correct reasoning, reference use, and insight into limitations/future directions) but do not specify how much weight each should receive if one subpart is strong while another is weak. The “AND/OR” structure could be clarified by explicitly stating whether statistical/theoretical justification for conclusions is a required element for the highest band, and by giving examples of what counts as “outstanding insight.” Finally, there is no explicit threshold for what qualifies as “irresponsible use of references,” which would benefit from more concrete examples (e.g., outdated vs misapplied vs unsupported causal claims).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria rely on thresholds like “overall use… at least 60% correct,” which is hard to apply consistently without a clear operational definition or examples of what counts as an error. The rubric also bundles APA issues with general layout, but does not distinguish severity (e.g., minor spacing vs. missing references or headings) which makes grading somewhat subjective. Adding brief, concrete examples for each performance band (e.g., 3–5 typical grammar/APA mistakes that place a paper in each range) would improve reliability and consistency. Finally, the wording “crucial parts” could be operationalized (e.g., RQ/hypotheses, methods variables, key stats) to make the “vague/ambiguous statements” threshold more uniform.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear clinical and developmental rationale for focusing on adolescents with SAD, and it motivates the need for more acceptable exposure-based treatments. It explains a theoretical account linking conditioning processes to SAD and briefly connects this to the mechanisms of IVET and VRET. The literature review is substantive and generally relevant, identifying limitations of adult-focused and non-equivalent comparisons and narrowing the gap to adolescents with clinically diagnosed SAD. The section ends with a stated aim and testable predictions (including the differential VRET vs. IVET effect), though the research question/hypotheses are not always tightly stated in a single, fully explicit, line of reasoning culminated into a clearly formulated RQ. Several constructs/mechanistic statements are somewhat asserted rather than tightly evidenced, and some comparisons use adult findings without fully justifying the assumed continuity to adolescents.
intro_style_notes: Overall scientific language is clear and mostly appropriate, with a logical progression from disorder prevalence → treatment gap → theory → VR rationale → prior evidence → current study aim/hypotheses. There are a few grammar/APA issues (e.g., inconsistent hyphenation/abbreviations like “in-vivo/IVET,” a missing space in “Ahrens et al. 2024” citation, and “exposed to real-life situations” wording), and some parenthetical definitions are slightly wordy. Paragraphing is mostly coherent, but the introduction contains a lot of dense, multi-claim paragraphs near the literature review, which can blur topical sentence focus. Referencing is generally consistent (author–year), but there are minor format inconsistencies (e.g., “APA, n.d.” and capitalization like “classical conditioning” references) that should be harmonized.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric could better distinguish between (a) whether an explicit RQ is stated vs. whether the hypotheses/aim imply an RQ, since these are often conflated in grading. Band descriptors would benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as “responsible use of references” (e.g., stating limitations of studies vs. over-claiming mechanisms without direct citation). The Discussion criteria (even though not assessed here) currently emphasize “reflection on the current study,” but could specify what elements qualify (e.g., internal validity, measurement limits, generalizability) to reduce subjectivity. Across criteria, it would help to clarify how to grade partially correct sections where the aim/hypotheses are present but the line of reasoning culminating in the RQ is only implicit.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a generally coherent study design: adolescents with primary SAD are randomized to VRET, IVET, or a waitlist condition, with pre- and post-assessment using the LSAS-CA. Participant recruitment/eligibility criteria, core treatment structure (10 sessions over ~5 weeks), and key delivery details for both VRET and IVET are described with reasonable completeness. Materials and procedures include several relevant implementation specifics for exposure tailoring in VRET and the setting/hierarchy logic for both conditions. However, some critical details are missing or insufficiently specified for full replicability (e.g., therapist number/roles, session fidelity/supervision, exact randomization/sequence generation handling, waitlist duration, adherence/compliance, and how the hierarchy is operationally matched across VRET vs IVET).
methods_style_notes: Language is mostly clear and scientific, with generally correct grammar and spelling. APA-style citation formatting appears consistent in-text, though sectioning could be tighter (e.g., subheadings for randomized assignment, randomization procedure, and outcomes could improve navigation). Minor redundancies and some vague phrasing occur (e.g., “until their anxiety decreased” without a concrete rule), and a few terms/amounts are stated in ways that may be unclear (e.g., “two blocks of 30 minutes” but treatment is said to be 10 sessions over 5 weeks without clarifying total dose equivalence). Paragraph structure largely follows the content (participants/materials/procedure), supporting readability.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Method criteria appropriately reward coherence between the design and the stated RQ/hypotheses and require participants, materials, and procedures to be complete and correct, which fits experimental treatment trials. However, the band descriptors are somewhat broad and could be anchored with concrete method elements relevant to this course rubric (e.g., explicit randomization details, assignment concealment, blinding, adherence/fidelity, outcome timing, handling of waitlist duration/crossover). The distinctions between “mostly correct/nearly complete” versus “complete and correct” are likely too subjective unless the rubric lists a checklist of required reporting items. Adding a short set of objective anchors (e.g., “replicable inclusion criteria + randomization method + session schedule + outcome measures/timepoints + fidelity/adherence info”) would reduce variability in scoring.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports the planned mixed ANOVA with relevant effect tests (main effects of time and condition, and the time-by-condition interaction), and it provides corresponding F, df, and p-values. It also includes follow-up (Holm-corrected) comparisons and describes how post-treatment changes differed across conditions, aligning broadly with the factorial design. However, several analytical details are missing or unclear for strict replicability and for judging correctness: the specific post-hoc comparison framework is not fully specified (which contrasts were tested, how directionality was handled), the approach to a violated normality assumption is not convincingly justified beyond citing the CLT, and the reported statement that waitlist scores “fit the scale” is not methodologically relevant to the analysis. Reporting of assumption checks is partial (e.g., no discussion of how outliers and non-normality were handled beyond inclusion).
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and uses appropriate statistical terminology, with mostly correct grammar and punctuation. The section has clear paragraphing by analysis step (data screening, assumptions, main ANOVA, post-hoc tests), but some statements are vague or potentially confusing (e.g., how the post-hoc comparisons map onto “between… and between different measurement points”). APA formatting for tables/figures is mostly present, but there are small inconsistencies (e.g., headings/tables not fully styled, decimals and spacing such as “p = .004”, and the use of “ANOVA” vs “Factorial Mixed Analysis of Variance (ANOVA)” without consistent capitalization). Redundancy is limited, though a few sentences (e.g., the outlier “fit the scale” comment) read as unnecessary for Results.
results_rubric_feedback: The Methods-to-Results alignment is partially successful: the mixed design is analyzed with the correct kind of omnibus test and interaction term, but the follow-up analysis is not sufficiently specified to verify that it matches the research questions/hypotheses and the factorial structure. The statistical reporting is mostly adequate (effect sizes are absent, and the post-hoc contrast set and correction logic are not concretely described), which limits demonstration of in-depth statistical reasoning. Assumption handling is discussed, but the justification for relying on the Central Limit Theorem despite violated normality is not adequately grounded, and outlier handling is described without linking to the analysis plan. For better use of the rubric’s “Results” criteria, more concrete anchors for what constitutes ‘complete’ (e.g., reporting effect sizes, df conventions, and post-hoc contrast specification) would improve reliability.
discussion_content_summary: The Discussion provides a clear, mostly consistent interpretation of the main results: both VRET and IVET reduced SAD symptoms more than the waitlist, but VRET was not superior to IVET at post-treatment. It links the overall findings to prior exposure-based evidence and offers theoretically grounded (though somewhat speculative) explanations for the null differential effect, including the role of CBT mechanisms and potential cognitive avoidance. The section also proposes plausible limitations related to VR scenario structure and therapeutic alliance, and it gives concrete directions for future work. However, it does not sufficiently revisit the study’s exact hypotheses/RQ in a tightly argued way, and some claims (e.g., why waitlist improved, and how specific constructs were/weren’t targeted) are asserted without clear connection to measured variables or results beyond post-treatment patterns.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and scientific, with mostly correct grammar and coherent paragraphing. There are some issues with precision and phrasing (e.g., “contrary to the expectation that the VRET condition will be superior,” “will be” vs. past outcomes; several explanations are broad and somewhat repetitive). APA compliance is mostly acceptable, but the reference list is dense and contains minor formatting/consistency concerns; also there is an inconsistency in how the intervention components are described (VRET vs VRET+CBT) relative to what was actually tested. Overall structure follows a typical discussion flow (summary → interpretation → limitations → future directions), but redundancy appears in re-stating baseline findings multiple times.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric bands could be made more concrete by specifying what “correct, coherent, and convincingly argued” should look like in a Discussion (e.g., explicitly addressing each hypothesis/RQ; distinguishing interpretation from speculation; linking limitations to specific design choices). The Discussion criterion currently combines multiple dimensions (reasoning quality, critical reflection, reference use) into one broad descriptor; separating these into sub-criteria (e.g., hypothesis-by-hypothesis evaluation, limitations/mechanisms, limitations-to-measurement linkage) would improve reliability. Some wording is slightly subjective (“very coherently,” “irresponsible use”), so adding anchors such as “explicitly tests rival explanations” or “uses measured outcomes rather than assumptions” would strengthen the scale. Consider adding a band descriptor for how to handle over-claiming beyond the data (e.g., when explaining null differential effects), since this is a common grading fault.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria would benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as “some redundancy” versus “numerous errors,” since those thresholds can be subjective. Consider adding examples or measurable indicators (e.g., proportion of sentences with vague terms, number of APA formatting deviations per page) to reduce rater variability. Clarify whether “vague or ambiguous statements” should be judged only in theoretically/analytically crucial parts or across the entire manuscript. Finally, the band descriptors could be tightened by explicitly linking them to both clarity/structure and APA compliance with clearer expected outcomes per proficiency level.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows solid scientific relevance and generally coherent rationale, with a mostly appropriate study design and clearly reported mixed ANOVA results. However, the line of reasoning to the RQ/hypotheses is not fully tightly articulated, and several crucial methodological details (e.g., fidelity/supervision, randomization specifics, hierarchy matching) are insufficient for full replicability. The Discussion is plausible and mostly consistent with the findings, but it does not critically and directly re-engage with the exact hypotheses/RQ to the same depth, leaving some claims somewhat asserted rather than tightly linked to measured outcomes. Given these gaps in scientific argumentation precision and reporting/replicability, the work fits the “Adequate” band more than “Good/Excellent,” landing at a holistic 6.5/10.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides clear background on SAD prevalence, onset in adolescence, and the developmental importance of peer interaction, leading to a rationale for improved adolescent treatment. It also offers a broadly coherent theoretical account linking conditioning mechanisms to exposure-based interventions and to the rationale for VRET as a potentially more acceptable alternative to IVET. The section reviews adult evidence and highlights limitations that motivate adolescent-focused comparison. The research aim is stated and testable, but the hypotheses are somewhat generic (e.g., the waitlist predictions) and the logical link between specific theoretical expectations and the exact directional VRET>IVET hypothesis could be sharpened.
intro_style_notes: Language is generally scientific and understandable with mostly correct grammar and terminology, and the section is logically structured in paragraphs moving from rationale → theory → VRET vs IVET → evidence → aim/hypotheses. There are a few awkward or imprecise phrases (e.g., “unconditioned stimulus and other social situations,” and “the outcome is not the expected negative one”) and occasional minor citation/format inconsistencies (e.g., “Ahrens et al. 2024” missing consistent APA parentheses; “More effective than IVET” phrased as if already established). APA n.d. and several citations are used appropriately, but some statements are broad and could be tightened to reduce redundancy (e.g., multiple repetitions of “effectiveness/less daunting/safe/comfort”). Overall paragraph focus is good, but redundancy and a few unclear causal formulations may affect precision.
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from clearer anchors for what counts as “clear and relevant” vs “very coherent and very convincingly argued,” particularly regarding the strength and specificity of the line of reasoning to the RQ/hypotheses. It also could define more concretely what “irresponsible use of references” means (e.g., outdated citations, mismatch between claim and evidence, missing citation for key statistics). For the Method criterion in particular (not assessed here), the band descriptors could distinguish between missing core elements (sample, design) and merely vague operational details. Finally, the rubric could explicitly separate whether hypotheses are “testable” from whether they are “logically derived” to give more actionable grading signals.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a coherent overview of participants, key inclusion/exclusion criteria, the intervention types (VRET vs IVET), materials used, and the overall procedure including random assignment and assessment timing. The treatment structure (10 sessions, twice per week, gradual exposure, and session block timing) is largely complete and consistent with an exposure-based trial evaluating SAD symptom change. However, some essential details are missing or unclear for full replicability (e.g., therapist/condition blinding, treatment fidelity, exact scheduling of assessments, and how the VRET anxiety ratings informed progression rules). The outcome measurement description is appropriate and reasonably specific, but the statistical analysis plan is not part of this section, so methodological precision relative to the hypotheses cannot be fully judged.
methods_style_notes: Overall scientific language is clear and mostly precise, with logical paragraphing by subheadings (Participants, Materials, Procedure). There are minor clarity issues due to dense phrasing (e.g., several parenthetical examples embedded in sentences) and occasional imprecision (e.g., “All participants received free treatment” without specifying whether all conditions received equal non-treatment supports). APA compliance is generally acceptable for in-text citations, but some wording could be tightened for consistency (e.g., consistent use of terms like “the participant” vs “participants” and defining abbreviations once).
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from clearer anchors for what “complete and correct” method reporting entails (e.g., inclusion of randomization procedure details, treatment fidelity, therapist details, assessment schedule, and blinding). The criteria currently blend design coherence with reporting completeness, which can make band distinctions feel somewhat subjective—adding sub-bullets (design; participants; intervention implementation; outcome measurement; procedure/assessment timing) would improve reliability. The language “somewhat comprehensible” vs “comprehensible” is still qualitative; consider examples of missing elements that place work in each band. Finally, results/dicsussion clarity are separate criteria, but for Methods it could explicitly note that the analysis plan is not required here (to avoid overlap with “in depth knowledge” phrasing).
results_content_summary: The section reports an overall 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed-factor ANOVA on LSAS-CA change, including key omnibus effects (main effect of time, null main effect of condition, and significant time×condition interaction). It then provides post-hoc Holm-corrected comparisons that specify where the differences lie (both active treatments lower LSAS-CA vs waitlist, with no IVET–VRET difference post-treatment), and it summarizes within-condition pre–post changes. The reporting is generally coherent with the described design and makes clear reference to outcome timing. However, several methodological-to-results connections are incomplete/unclear (e.g., degrees of freedom interpretation, handling of violated normality, and the mismatch between the non-significant interaction and the stated pattern only partially aligning with hypothesis structure).
results_style_notes: Language is mostly scientific and understandable, with clear signposting of analyses and key statistics. There are some issues with precision/terminology (e.g., asserting use of the Central Limit Theorem after a Shapiro-Wilk violation without clarifying implications for ANOVA assumptions), and a few minor ambiguities (e.g., where exactly Table 1/2 values apply, and the formatting/definition of participants). APA elements for tables/figures are partially present, but the section has formatting inconsistencies (e.g., missing alignment cues in the table header, occasional spacing issues like “N= 90”, and no explicit reporting of effect sizes/partial η²). Overall grammar is good, with limited redundancy.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion is solidly framed around correctness/completeness/coherence/relevance, but it could benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as ‘complete’ (e.g., requiring effect sizes and confidence intervals, reporting assumptions with clear actions, and consistent df reporting for mixed ANOVA). The descriptors like ‘somewhat relevant’ versus ‘relevant’ are somewhat subjective; adding examples (e.g., ‘reports omnibus F, df, p and post-hoc with correction plus effect sizes’) would reduce variability. It may also help to explicitly distinguish between ‘analysis plan adherence’ and ‘statistical reasoning depth’ so partial compliance can be graded more consistently.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion restates the study aim and provides a coherent summary of the main findings: all groups improved over time, VRET and IVET outperformed waitlist at post-treatment, and there was no VRET–IVET difference at post-treatment. The section attempts to align results with the introduction’s conditioning/exposure rationale and offers plausible theoretical explanations for the null VRET>IVET effect, including cognitive avoidance. It also adds relevant limitations (VR realism via semi-structured dialogues; potential therapeutic alliance differences) and suggests concrete future directions. However, the argumentative chain sometimes overreaches the evidence (e.g., interpreting IVET superiority despite no VRET–IVET difference) and the discussion does not clearly address potential alternative interpretations of the null effect (e.g., effect size/measurement timing, implementation differences) beyond alliance and realism.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally clear and appropriately scientific, with mostly correct grammar and terminology. Paragraphing follows a recognizable structure (findings → theory → limitations → future research), though there is some repetition of the same result across sentences/paragraphs. APA-style referencing in the text is mostly fine, but there are minor issues such as inconsistent typographical casing (e.g., “VRET + CBT” vs “VRET + CBT” is fine, but overall formatting is inconsistent) and an included reference list block that is not clearly separated from the discussion text. Some causal phrasing is somewhat imprecise (using “supported by/establishes” language without directly demonstrating the claimed comparisons in this study).
discussion_rubric_feedback: Several rubric descriptors are broad and could benefit from more concrete anchors distinguishing “nearly complete” vs “complete” reasoning and what specifically counts as “convincingly argued for.” The Discussion criterion currently mixes interpretation, limitations, and reflection under one umbrella; separating “consistency with RQ/hypotheses” from “critical reflection/limitations” would make judging more reliable. Clarifying expectations for statistical adequacy in Discussion (e.g., effect sizes, uncertainty, null effects handling) would improve objectivity. Finally, the Language & Style bands could specify what “graceful style / no errors” concretely means (e.g., tolerance for minor APA inconsistencies).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criterion usefully distinguishes clarity/structure from grammar, APA, vagueness, and redundancy, but some bands are still a bit subjective (e.g., what counts as “some” vs “few” vague statements). Adding concrete anchors—such as requiring consistent APA elements for tables/figures, or specifying that key terms/abbreviations must be defined once—would improve reliability. Also, the rubric could clarify whether APA layout issues are weighed equally to scientific language clarity, and whether sentence-level redundancy is treated differently from paragraph-level redundancy. Finally, using brief examples of “hardly any” versus “numerous” errors in the descriptors could reduce grader variance.
final_grade_justification: Holistically, the paper demonstrates an overall coherent exposure-based rationale from the introduction through to the interpretation in the discussion, with a clearly specified aim and appropriate core design (VRET vs IVET vs waitlist) and outcome (LSAS-CA). However, scientific argumentation is not always tightly aligned with the results (especially given the non-significant VRET–IVET difference and the somewhat overreaching inference of IVET superiority), and alternative explanations are only partially developed. The Methods lack a few details needed for full replicability and the Results, while generally correct and relevant, show some reporting/analytic-interpretation looseness that reduces statistical reasoning depth. Language and structure appear sufficiently clear overall, placing the paper in the “adequate to good” band but not reaching “good/excellent” for argumentative precision and statistical/replication completeness.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear clinical rationale for studying social anxiety disorder in adolescents and motivates exposure-based treatment as a practical target. It offers theoretical explanations (classical and operant conditioning) and links IVET/VRET to mechanisms intended to change maladaptive fear and avoidance. The gap in the literature is articulated reasonably well—prior adult-focused comparisons and limited adolescent evidence, including issues of equivalence and control conditions. The research aim is stated, and testable hypotheses are included, but the final rationale leading to the specific prediction (VRET outperforming IVET in adolescents) could be more tightly argued and more explicitly tied to prior adolescent findings and the chosen control/comparators.
intro_style_notes: Language is generally scientific and understandable, with mostly correct grammar and spelling, and the introduction follows a logical paragraph progression (disorder → treatment gap → theory → VRET rationale → prior studies → present study and hypotheses). However, there are some imprecise or confusing formulations (e.g., “unconditioned stimulus”/“conditioned stimuli” wording in the SAD description; “punishment” vs. “positive effect”; “outcome is not the expected negative one”) and occasional reference-format inconsistencies (missing/variable “et al.” punctuation/spacing; one “Ahrens et al. 2024” missing parentheses before the year; APA n.d. citation style needs verification). Paragraphs sometimes become long with multiple ideas and should use clearer topical sentences near each major subsection. Redundancy appears in the repeated rationale that VRET is safer/less daunting; tightening could improve clarity and flow.
intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction contains a coherent line of reasoning culminating in a clear research question/aim (compare VRET vs IVET in adolescents with SAD using an RCT with waitlist), and it uses references responsibly overall. The theoretical and practical relevance are strong, and the hypotheses are testable and logically derived from the claim that adolescents may find VR more safe/comfortable and thus engage more effectively. To reach a higher band, the link between adolescents’ presumed VR comfort and the specific directional hypothesis (VRET > IVET) should be more explicitly synthesized from the most relevant prior adolescent evidence (not only general advantages or entertainment familiarity). The theoretical conditioning explanation should be stated with more precise terminology and without potentially confusing “unconditioned/conditioned” mappings, so the argument is maximally convincing and scientifically careful.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section is largely coherent and generally matches the study aims and hypotheses: it specifies a three-arm design (VRET, IVET, waitlist), includes an adolescent SAD diagnosis, and provides a clear pre/post outcome measure (LSAS-CA). Participants, recruitment, inclusion/exclusion criteria, random assignment, treatment dose (10 sessions, twice weekly), and core therapy components (hierarchies and exposure blocks) are described sufficiently for replication at a high level. However, some critical procedural details are missing or ambiguous (e.g., therapist treatment fidelity, randomization details such as stratification/sequence concealment, waitlist assessment timing, and how “until anxiety decreased” is operationalized). The materials section is detailed about VR components and tailoring, but IVET implementation specifics (e.g., hierarchy construction steps beyond “from individual social situations,” therapist guidance structure across sessions, and handling of participant-specific differences) are less concrete.
methods_style_notes: Language is mostly precise and professional, with clear subheadings aligned to standard paper structure (Participants, Materials, Procedure). Grammar and spelling are accurate, and referencing appears consistent with APA in-text citation style. Paragraph structure is generally logical, but some long sentences reduce readability (especially within Materials/IVET/VRET). Minor ambiguity (e.g., what exactly constitutes tailoring rules and the operational criteria for anxiety reduction) affects clarity more than grammar or formatting.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as “complete and correct” methods description (e.g., specifying that randomization procedure, allocation concealment, blinding/assessment procedures, fidelity monitoring, and key operational definitions must be present). The Results and Discussion criteria mention statistical depth and critical reflection, but Methods lacks similar specificity on analysis-plan linkage (e.g., whether the analysis is foreshadowed or aligned with hypotheses). Additionally, band descriptors could be tightened to reduce subjectivity—e.g., define examples that distinguish “Adequate” vs “Good” Methods. Finally, the rubric could explicitly note whether waitlist timing and crossover handling (if any) should be required for coherence at higher levels.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports an overall 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA on LSAS-CA and provides key inferential statistics (main effects, interaction) with F, df, and p-values. It also includes assumption checks (Shapiro–Wilk for normality, Levene for homogeneity) and post-hoc comparisons with Holm correction, and the direction of effects is described consistently with the hypotheses at least for treatment vs waitlist. However, the handling of the violated normality assumption is not properly justified (the choice to rely on the CLT is stated without addressing within-cell distributional issues in ANOVA), and the reporting is somewhat incomplete/unclear about effect sizes and the exact structure of the mixed factor (e.g., whether time is within-subject and how df were obtained). Additionally, the inclusion of two post-treatment outliers is mentioned, but there is no sensitivity analysis or rationale beyond “fit the scale,” which leaves uncertainty about robustness of the findings.
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and scientific, and paragraphing follows a logical sequence (sample description → analysis plan/assumptions → omnibus tests → post-hoc tests). Grammar and formatting are mostly acceptable, but there are a few issues such as inconsistent spacing (e.g., “p =.004” vs “p < .001”), an inconsistent use of decimals and punctuation, and some missing/unfinished table formatting cues (e.g., Table 1 and Table 2 appear without their actual contents). APA compliance is partial: table headings are present, but figure/table numbering and referencing conventions are not fully specified, and Figure 1 is referenced without describing axis labels/units in text.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion would benefit from clearer anchors distinguishing between (a) incorrect/incoherent statistics, (b) missing but still interpretable reporting (e.g., effect sizes, confidence intervals), and (c) incomplete alignment with the Methods (e.g., mixed-model vs repeated-measures ANOVA details). The rubric currently emphasizes correctness/completeness but does not explicitly require effect sizes or reporting of post-hoc adjustment scope, which are often central to judging adequacy. Consider adding explicit sub-requirements (effect sizes like partial eta-squared, degrees-of-freedom consistency checks, and handling of assumption violations/outliers) so graders can apply band descriptors more consistently and objectively.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion addresses the study aim by summarizing that both VRET and IVET reduced SAD symptoms more than waitlist at post-treatment, while VRET was not superior to IVET, directly reflecting the reported results and hypotheses. It provides a generally coherent theoretical explanation for the null VRET vs IVET superiority effect (cognitive avoidance and reduced realism), and it proposes plausible limitations (VR dialogue constraints and therapeutic alliance differences). The discussion also extends to concrete future research recommendations and briefly restates clinical implications for adolescents. However, the reasoning sometimes makes claims that are not tightly evidenced (e.g., specific mechanisms and interview-related constructs) and the discussion does not adequately integrate effect size/robustness issues or distinguish between statistical non-significance and equivalence/modest differences.
discussion_style_notes: Language is mostly clear and scientific, with generally correct grammar and spelling. Paragraphing is structured around key claims (findings, interpretation, limitations, future directions), though there is some redundancy (re-stating the main findings multiple times) and a few imprecise phrases (e.g., “support” vs “supported by” without specifying evidence strength). APA formatting is inconsistent in places (e.g., a stray reference-format header “References” inside the text, and some citation punctuation/spacing issues such as the range for Günther et al.). Overall readability is good, but the inclusion of a full reference list and a few wording imprecisions reduce compliance.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric could better distinguish between (a) correctly interpreting and connecting results to hypotheses and (b) the quality of causal/theoretical explanations, because these are currently bundled under broad “reasoning” categories. Anchors for Discussion could explicitly require: mention of main effect/interaction in plain language, alignment with the research question, and appropriate handling of “non-significant” findings (e.g., no overclaiming mechanisms without mediators). Language/Style descriptors could be clarified to separate APA referencing compliance from general scientific writing, and to add concrete examples of what counts as “irresponsible” reference use. Consider adding an “evidence integration” sub-criterion (use of reported statistics/effect sizes/assumption caveats) to reduce subjectivity.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria are generally useful, but the band descriptors could be made more concrete by adding example “anchors” (e.g., what counts as “few” vs “numerous” errors, or how many APA formatting inconsistencies are tolerated per section). Some terms are somewhat subjective (e.g., “impedes proper understanding,” “some redundancy,” “vague or ambiguous”), so clearer thresholds or indicative counts would improve reliability. Consider separating grammar/spelling accuracy from APA/layout compliance and tightening the descriptors to distinguish between minor vs systematic issues.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows solid scientific reasoning: the introduction and discussion are coherent and the reported pattern of results is interpreted consistently with the hypotheses, including a plausible explanation for the null VRET vs IVET difference. Methods are mostly complete and aligned with the research aim, but key procedural and analytic details (e.g., randomization/fidelity, waitlist timing, operationalization of stopping criteria, full IVET/IVET implementation clarity) limit replicability. Results reporting is generally correct and relevant, yet it does not adequately handle the violated normality assumption or provide robustness/effect-size clarity given outliers, which weakens inferential confidence. Language and structure are acceptable, but the combination of these issues places the paper in the “Adequate to Good” band overall rather than “Good/Excellent.”

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear clinical rationale for studying adolescent social anxiety disorder and motivates the comparison of VRET vs. IVET. It includes theoretical reasoning (classical and operant conditioning) and explains how exposure and VR may change maladaptive associations, with references that generally align with these claims. The research aim is stated, a randomized controlled design and key comparisons are described, and hypotheses are testable (pre/post, treatment vs. waitlist, VRET vs. IVET). However, the line of argument becomes somewhat diffuse in places (e.g., over-generalization framing and operant “positive effect” language), and the hypotheses are not fully articulated in terms of the expected interaction/trajectory (e.g., whether change scores differ) and statistical framing is somewhat generic. Overall, scientific relevance and justification for the specific adolescent focus and the direct comparison are present but could be tighter and more theoretically and empirically grounded toward a crisp, single research question.
intro_style_notes: Language is largely scientific and readable, with an overall coherent progression from problem → treatment gap → theory → rationale for VR → prior evidence → study aim/hypotheses. There are minor grammar/clarity issues (e.g., parenthetical definitions that read awkwardly, and a potentially incorrect/unclear phrasing around “positive effect” in operant conditioning), plus a few APA-style inconsistencies (e.g., “aspect found to predict…” and citation punctuation/formatting). Paragraph structure is mostly controlled, though several long paragraphs contain multiple claims (and thus some redundancy/overstuffing). The introduction ends with a good consolidation of aim and hypotheses, but the hypotheses could be expressed more precisely (e.g., in terms of group × time effects).
intro_rubric_feedback: The rubric bands for Introduction are mostly clear, but the descriptors could benefit from more concrete anchors for what counts as a “clear” vs. “very clear” RQ and hypothesis (e.g., requiring explicit RQ wording and specifying predicted effects as group×time/contrast). The “irresponsible use of references” criterion is somewhat broad; specifying examples (e.g., unsupported causal claims, missing context, or mismatched citation-to-claim) would improve reliability. The rubric could also distinguish between having a stated RQ vs. having a logically tight line of reasoning, since a paper might include an RQ but with weak theoretical/empirical chaining. Finally, the method/hypothesis testability element in the Introduction could be clarified to explicitly mention operationalization (how constructs map to measures) or statistical form (main effects vs. interactions).
methods_content_summary: The Methods section is largely coherent and matches a randomized controlled design comparing VRET, IVET, and a waitlist, with appropriate pre/post assessment using LSAS-CA. Participants, recruitment/exclusion criteria, intervention structure (10 sessions, exposure hierarchy, session timing), and broad VR/in-vivo delivery details are provided in a way that supports replicability. The description of the VRET system, therapist training, and key VR scenario tailoring elements is reasonably specific, and the data collection timeline is clear. However, some critical methodological details are missing or underspecified (e.g., therapist number/roles per condition, fidelity/monitoring, adherence, blinding, outcome timing specifics beyond pre/post, and how waitlist affects assessment beyond initial post).
methods_style_notes: Language is mostly clear and scientifically appropriate, with generally precise terminology (e.g., LSAS-CA, exposure hierarchy). Paragraph structure is logical (participants/materials/procedure with subheadings), but there is some repetition (e.g., condition details reappearing across subsections) and occasional long sentences that could be split for readability. APA compliance appears mostly acceptable for citations, but there are a few ambiguities/typos in wording (e.g., “length and difficulty” phrasing, some phrasing around dialogue manipulation) that would benefit from tightening. No major grammar/spelling issues are evident, though consistency of phrasing and specification (e.g., “through advertisements” vs “via”) could be improved.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods rubric bands could be improved by adding more concrete anchors for what counts as “complete and correct” versus “mostly complete.” Currently, decisions like whether blinding, therapist fidelity, adherence, randomization implementation, and attrition handling count as “method details” are left implicit, making scoring potentially subjective. Adding a short checklist of essential RCT elements (e.g., unit of randomization, allocation concealment, assessor blinding, intervention fidelity/adherence, attrition/discontinuation procedures, and assessment timepoints) would make the rubric more reliable across graders. Also, results-focused criteria are separate, but for Methods the rubric could explicitly mention whether outcome measurement timing and handling of the waitlist control is specified, since that is pivotal for coherence with hypotheses.
results_content_summary: The section reports that data from 90 participants were analyzed and that LSAS-CA was tested with a 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA, including tests of assumptions (Shapiro-Wilk, Levene). It provides key omnibus results (main effect of time, non-significant main effect of condition, significant time × condition interaction) and follow-up Holm-corrected post-hoc comparisons summarized with p-values, including that both active treatments improved versus waitlist and that IVET and VRET did not differ at post-treatment. However, the results are under-specified for the described mixed design because the degrees of freedom and outlier handling/inclusion logic are not fully coherent with a mixed-factor framework, and effect sizes and variance explanations are omitted. Additionally, the results description does not clearly connect how the waitlist post-test was analyzed relative to expectations beyond pre/post decreases, and the reporting lacks the completeness typically expected for statistical results (e.g., F-statistics for each post-hoc contrast, df, and multiple-comparison structure).
results_style_notes: The writing is generally clear and uses appropriate scientific terminology, with mostly correct grammar and spelling. APA alignment is inconsistent: table and figure labels are present but the table contents are not fully specified in-line, and the text refers to Figure 1/Table 2 without clear formatting and without reporting effect sizes or additional statistics that would normally accompany results. There is some redundancy in explaining assumptions and reiterating hypothesis alignment, but overall paragraph structure is understandable. Minor precision issues occur (e.g., phrasing about the Central Limit Theorem and how it justifies the specific normality violation, and the relationship between outliers and inclusion).
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Results criteria are mostly adequate but could benefit from clearer anchors distinguishing “mostly correct” reporting from “complete” reporting. For example, the rubric could explicitly list common completeness expectations (e.g., reporting effect sizes, dfs for all tests, multiple-comparison correction details, and consistency of reported df with the design). The language “in depth knowledge of statistical analyses and reasoning” is somewhat subjective; adding concrete indicators (effect sizes like partial eta-squared, assumptions handling rationale, and coherence between design and df) would reduce subjectivity. Finally, separating “accuracy” from “reporting completeness/quality” more explicitly would help graders apply the same standards across different statistical approaches.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion addresses the study aim by interpreting that both VRET and IVET reduced adolescent SAD symptoms, with VRET not outperforming IVET at post-treatment. It connects findings to the introduction’s exposure-based rationale and provides plausible theoretical explanations (e.g., cognitive avoidance, overgeneralization, familiarity/comfort) for the null between-treatment difference. The section also offers targeted limitations (VR scenario structure realism; therapeutic alliance) and proposes concrete directions for future research (more interactive VR; alliance measurement; VRET vs. VRET+CBT). However, some claims are somewhat overextended relative to what is reported (e.g., emphasis on “supported” pre–post effects in specific conditions; causality implications), and the discussion does not clearly re-state and test the full hypothesis set (e.g., trajectory/interaction logic) as an explicit framework for interpretation.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally clear and scientific, with mostly correct grammar and readable paragraph flow. There are a few imprecisions and grammar issues (e.g., “patients view the probability…” in a quotation-like definition; occasional agreement/tense slips; “The results are in line with the hypothesis…” where multiple hypotheses are implied). APA formatting is mostly acceptable, but the inclusion of a large reference list inside the discussion section and some inconsistent formatting (e.g., missing/odd punctuation, varying journal/DOI line formatting) reduce compliance. Paragraphs are mostly single-focus, though some sentences pack multiple claims and some wording is repetitive (e.g., restating study conclusions several times).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric bands would benefit from clearer anchors distinguishing (a) correct interpretation of the primary hypothesis tests (e.g., group × time/interaction and between-treatment contrasts) from (b) plausible but speculative theorizing. “Discussion quality” could be operationalized more concretely by specifying that limitations should be linked to specific design features and that future directions should be explicitly derived from those limitations and/or the pattern of results. Consider splitting “critical reflection” into two sub-criteria (internal validity/statistical interpretation vs. external validity/clinical implications) to reduce subjectivity and improve reliability. Finally, the rubric could specify what level of referencing is expected in Discussion (e.g., “references directly supporting each claim” vs. “general background”) to tighten the “responsible use of references” descriptor.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criterion mixes multiple dimensions (scientific language, structure, grammar, APA compliance, vagueness, redundancy) in one scale, which can make scoring less transparent—consider splitting into sub-criteria or providing clearer weighting. Several descriptors are broad (e.g., “some vague or ambiguous statements,” “some redundancy”), so adding concrete anchors or example phrases per band would improve reliability. APA style/layout mentions are helpful, but the rubric could specify which APA elements are most critical (e.g., citation punctuation, headings, table/figure formatting) and which errors are typically non-fatal. Finally, the band descriptors could better separate “minor stylistic issues” vs “patterned issues,” since both can appear as “some errors” without guidance on frequency/severity.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows solid scientific relevance and a generally coherent rationale in the Introduction, leading to a testable RQ and hypotheses, which places it around the Adequate-to-Good band for scientific argumentation. Methods are mostly complete and replicable, but key elements needed for scientific rigor (e.g., therapist roles/fidelity, blinding/monitoring, detailed timing) are underspecified. Results are the main limitation: while the overall pattern is plausible, reporting is incomplete for the stated mixed design (missing key inferential details/effect sizes and some coherence issues around df/outlier logic). The Discussion largely matches the findings and offers relevant limitations and future directions, but it sometimes overreaches causally/interpretively relative to what is reported, resulting in a Good-to-Adequate holistic standing rather than Excellent.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a reasonably coherent rationale for studying VRET versus IVET for adolescents with SAD, including prevalence, treatment uptake gaps, and theoretical conditioning accounts. The VRET/IVET mechanisms are generally explained and linked to adolescent-specific considerations (perceived safety, familiarity with technology). Prior work is reviewed with some attention to limitations (e.g., confounds with CBT and non-equivalent delivery formats), culminating in a clear comparative purpose. However, the line of reasoning to the specific RQ and predictions is somewhat uneven, with the theoretical framework and conditioning account not tightly mapped onto the exact differential expectation, and key details such as the precise research question are not explicitly stated (though implied).
intro_style_notes: Overall scientific language is mostly clear and understandable, with a generally logical paragraph structure (problem → treatment gap → theory → VRET rationale → prior evidence → present study). There are some grammar issues and imprecise phrasing (e.g., capitalization/wording inconsistencies like “in-vivo exposure therapy” vs “IVET” explanations, and a sentence fragment risk in the conditioning/operant conditioning section). APA-style referencing is mostly in-text and consistent, but there are minor format issues (e.g., “Ahrens et al. 2024” missing comma/typical APA punctuation consistency, and occasional parenthetical clarity). Redundancy appears in repeated explanation of exposure mechanisms and the adolescent rationale, and topical sentence focus could be tighter in the later literature-review paragraphs.
intro_rubric_feedback: Your rubric’s criteria for the Introduction are strong in emphasizing (a) a coherent line of reasoning to a clear RQ/hypotheses and (b) responsible use of references. To make grading more consistent, the rubric could more explicitly anchor what “RQ is clear” means in practice (e.g., explicitly stated vs strongly implied; level of specificity such as population, IV, DV, comparison, and timeframe). The descriptors “somewhat convincingly argued for” vs “very convincingly argued for” are somewhat subjective; adding concrete indicators (e.g., hypothesis derives directly from reviewed findings/theory; conflicting evidence is acknowledged; constructs are operationally grounded) would reduce variability. Finally, the rubric could distinguish between “hypotheses are testable” and “hypotheses are logically derived,” since a paper can be testable but not well-justified.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a coherent overview of participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, intervention components for VRET and IVET, and the primary dependent measure (LSAS-CA) with basic scoring details. The study design is described as randomized assignment to VRET/IVET/waitlist with pre- and post-treatment assessment, and treatment dosage (10 sessions; twice weekly) is generally clear. Materials are mostly specified, including the VR hardware/software system and several example exposure scenarios, and therapist training is mentioned. However, key methodological details remain incomplete or insufficiently precise for replication and for evaluating internal validity (e.g., blinding, therapist equivalence, waitlist assessment schedule, handling of adherence/attrition, and how tailoring/hierarchy decisions are operationalized).
methods_style_notes: Language is generally clear and scientific, with mostly precise terms and logical paragraphing by method subcomponent (Participants, Materials, Procedure). Grammar and spelling are largely correct, with no major clarity-impeding errors. APA formatting is mostly consistent with in-text citations, though the text would benefit from more consistent formatting/structure (e.g., parallel headings and removal/standardization of some phrasing such as “comprises of”). Some statements are somewhat vague (e.g., “randomly assigned to one of the treatment conditions” after waitlist without specifying timing/details), which may affect methodological precision.
methods_rubric_feedback: Some rubric descriptors are broad (e.g., “somewhat coherently related” vs “clearly related”) and could be anchored to concrete checklist items (e.g., explicit randomization procedure, complete inclusion/exclusion criteria, full intervention description, and outcome timing). For Results/Methods, consider adding explicit anchors for “replicability” (enough operational detail) versus “statistical adequacy” (though that’s more for Results). The language/stylistic band definitions could specify what counts as an APA layout error and what severity threshold moves a paper from Developing to Adequate. Finally, the scale would benefit from clearer boundaries to avoid ambiguity in what distinguishes “Good” from “Excellent” (e.g., completeness of procedure vs completeness of materials vs mention of bias control).
results_content_summary: The Results section reports a mixed-factorial ANOVA with clear reporting of main effects, the time-by-condition interaction, and condition/time means in tables/figures. It also includes relevant diagnostic testing (outliers, Shapiro-Wilk normality, Levene’s test) and follows this with post-hoc comparisons using Holm correction. However, the handling of the normality violation is not coherent with the stated analysis plan, and the degrees of freedom reported (F(1, 87), F(2, 87)) are inconsistent with a 2×3 mixed design unless additional model details are provided. The post-hoc results are largely interpretable in relation to the interaction, but the narrative claims that results “matched the expectations of the hypotheses” without stating the specific hypothesis tests/expected direction(s) numerically, and the waitlist decrease is not discussed analytically as a potential artifact of assessment or regression. Overall, the section is mostly correct and relevant but lacks sufficient statistical precision and internal consistency for an excellent grade.
results_style_notes: Language is generally scientific and understandable, with mostly clear paragraph-level focus (assumptions, main ANOVA, post-hoc, interpretation). There are several clarity/precision issues in statistics wording (e.g., using the Central Limit Theorem to justify ANOVA despite a specific Shapiro-Wilk violation for one cell, and not explaining how this affects the validity of the parametric test). APA/formatting compliance is partially met (tables/figures are referenced, but the table captions and placement look incomplete, and p-value formatting is inconsistent: e.g., “p=.004” vs “p < .001”). Minor redundancy occurs (e.g., reiterating significance across multiple sentences) and the section does not explicitly report key effect sizes or model parameters that would normally support scientific reporting.
results_rubric_feedback: Some rubric bands could benefit from clearer, more concrete anchors for what counts as “complete and correct” vs “mostly correct,” especially regarding standard reporting expectations (e.g., effect sizes, df consistency, assumption handling). The “Methods–Results alignment” aspect is implied but not explicitly operationalized in the rubric; adding explicit subcriteria (e.g., degrees of freedom consistency, reporting of correction procedures, and how assumption violations are addressed) would reduce subjectivity. The rubric could also clarify whether the emphasis is on inferential/statistical completeness (effect sizes, model details) versus narrative interpretation (linking to hypotheses) and how to balance them when one is strong and the other is weak. Finally, “in depth knowledge of statistical analyses” is broad; specifying examples (e.g., sphericity checks for repeated measures, proper handling of non-normality/outliers) would improve reliability across graders.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion clearly reiterates the main outcome: VRET and IVET both reduced SAD symptoms from pre- to post-treatment, with VRET not outperforming IVET, while both outperformed the waitlist at post-treatment. It provides several plausible explanatory accounts (conditioning similarity, adolescent technology familiarity, cognitive avoidance, realism/uncertainty targeting) and offers future directions focused on improving VR dialogue interactivity and measuring therapeutic alliance. However, the section only partly links back to the specific hypotheses/RQ with the same precision as required, and it includes an interpretive leap about “maximum efficiency” without fully integrating the study’s measured outcomes/statistical results. Critical reflection is present, but it is somewhat speculative and not consistently tied to alternative explanations (e.g., assessment effects, adherence/dose, and the waitlist pattern mentioned earlier).
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and scientifically appropriate, with mostly clear paragraph structure and signposting (“However,” “A limitation,” “Given these limitations”). There are minor grammar/wording issues (e.g., “will be superior” phrasing, “emphasize the effectiveness,” and a few awkward constructions) and some imprecision in APA conventions (a “References” header appears directly after the discussion text). Redundancy occurs in re-stating effectiveness across multiple paragraphs, and some claims are broad without tightly specifying what is supported by the presented results.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric could better specify what counts as “clear and relevant” hypothesis/RQ linkage in Discussion (e.g., explicitly test-by-test correspondence, direction of effects, and whether results support or contradict each prediction). The “critical reflection” criterion would benefit from concrete anchors distinguishing (a) methodological limitations directly observed in the study, (b) statistical/interpretive limitations, and (c) theory-based speculation, so reviewers can grade consistency more reliably. The Language & Style bands may need tighter descriptors for APA formatting in the Discussion (e.g., handling of headings and reference lists) to reduce subjectivity. Adding explicit expectations for avoiding overinterpretation beyond the data (e.g., “maximum efficiency”/mechanism claims) would improve agreement across graders.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria could benefit from clearer, more concrete anchors for what counts as “some” vs “few” vs “hardly any” vague statements, redundancy, and APA layout errors, since these are currently somewhat subjective. Adding exemplar error types (e.g., p-value formatting inconsistency, section/header placement mistakes, capitalization inconsistencies, sentence fragments) would make judgments more consistent across graders. Separating “clarity/precision in crucial parts” from general scientific language use could also improve reliability, because precision issues often matter more than wording polish.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a generally coherent scientific rationale and a plausible comparative design, with good alignment between intervention goals and the primary outcome (LSAS-CA), supporting a “Good/Adequate” performance band. However, the Introduction-to-RQ mapping and explicit hypothesis testing are uneven, and the Methods lack several internal-validity-critical details (e.g., blinding, therapist equivalence, adherence/attrition handling, and operationalization of tailoring). The Results are mostly relevant and interpretable but contain statistical imprecision/inconsistencies (degrees of freedom) and incomplete explanation of the waitlist pattern, limiting rigor. The Discussion provides relevant interpretations and future directions, yet only partly and sometimes speculatively re-integrates the findings with the precise hypotheses, which prevents an “Excellent” rating.

intro_content_summary: The introduction clearly defines SAD, motivates the adolescent focus with prevalence and functional impairment, and explains why treatment uptake for exposure-based approaches may be limited. It provides a reasonably coherent theoretical rationale linking associative learning (classical conditioning), fear/avoidance maintenance (operant reinforcement), and how IVET and VRET target these mechanisms. The introduction also synthesizes prior evidence on exposure therapies, including comparisons of VRET vs IVET/controls, and identifies a clear gap for adolescents. The study aim and testable hypotheses are stated, but some claims and transitional logic are occasionally overextended (e.g., mechanism statements and the specificity of the learning theory), and the reference set quality/recency is not consistently verifiable from the text (e.g., “APA, n.d.” and one potentially imprecise theoretical citation format).
intro_style_notes: Language is generally clear and professional, with mostly precise terminology (SAD, IVET, VRET, CBT) and logical paragraph progression. Grammar and phrasing are mostly accurate, though there are a few awkward constructions (e.g., “rewarding experience breaks the cycle,” “a more in-depth manipulation… through avatars’ reactions,” and some long sentences) that could be tightened for readability. APA compliance appears largely acceptable for in-text citations, but there are potential inconsistencies (e.g., “Ahrens et al. 2024” missing comma/space formatting; “Leigh & Clark, 2018” okay; use of “APA, n.d.” without further citation detail) and one or two citation formatting uncertainties (e.g., “Maples-Keller et al., 2017”). Overall structure is coherent with topical sentences; redundancy is limited but some sections repeat “main effect/treatment reduces symptoms” without adding new discriminating rationale.
intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction includes a clear line of reasoning to an RQ/aim and provides enough theoretical and empirical support to justify studying VRET vs IVET in adolescents. However, the rubric would be easier to apply if it specified what counts as a ‘clearly stated RQ’ vs a ‘study aim/hypotheses’ when the RQ itself is not explicitly written as a single sentence question. The criteria distinguishing “mostly coherent” vs “sound and mostly coherent” (and the thresholds like 60% vs 75% references up to date) would benefit from more concrete guidance on how students should evidence recency (e.g., publication year cutoffs) and how to treat general sources (e.g., DSM/APA pages).
