Finally, the rubric could more explicitly define what qualifies as “convincing” conclusions for argumentation so raters score consistently.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a generally coherent randomized controlled design with three conditions (VRET, IVET, waitlist) and 10 sessions over ~5 weeks, which aligns with the stated goal of comparing exposure-based approaches for adolescents with SAD. Participants, core diagnostic inclusion/exclusion criteria, and the main outcome measure (LSAS-CA) are described with sufficient specificity to understand who was studied and what was measured. The materials section clearly distinguishes the VRET system and IVET approach and gives some detail on VR scenario content and therapist tailoring parameters. However, key methodological details are still missing or too vague (e.g., therapist training scope, fidelity/monitoring, randomization/allocations concealment procedures, blinding of assessors, handling of dropouts/adherence, and the exact timing/analysis approach for outcomes), limiting confidence in internal validity and reproducibility.
methods_style_notes: The language is mostly clear and appropriately scientific, with generally well-structured subsections (Participants, Materials, Procedure). Grammar and spelling are largely correct, and terminology is used consistently (e.g., VRET/IVET; LSAS-CA). APA referencing appears mostly in-text and complete for cited sources, but the section occasionally uses parentheses inconsistently (e.g., “adopted from …” phrasing) and includes several long sentences that could be split for readability. Overall redundancy is low, but some procedural details could be presented more compactly (e.g., session structure) to improve precision.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Methods criterion appropriately rewards coherence and completeness, but it blurs the boundary between “somewhat related” and “mostly related” to the RQ/hypotheses without explicitly stating what counts as the RQ-dependent methodological requirements. The band descriptors could be operationalized with concrete required elements (e.g., randomization procedure, assessor blinding, treatment fidelity/monitoring, adherence, attrition handling) so that “75% complete” is more consistently judged. The Methods criterion also does not explicitly distinguish whether missing details are minor vs. internal-validity critical, which can lead to large grading variability. Adding examples of what would typically qualify as “unnecessary details anywhere” would further improve reliability across graders.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports sample size, outlier handling, and descriptive statistics references (Tables 1 and 2). It includes an appropriate factorial mixed ANOVA for LSAS-CA across time and condition, and it reports key omnibus effects (main effect of time, non-significant condition main effect, and significant time-by-condition interaction). Post-hoc Holm-corrected comparisons are provided for pre- and post-treatment differences and within-condition pre–post changes, with conclusions aligned to those tests. However, the reporting is incomplete for mixed ANOVA (e.g., no F for the interaction with condition/time clearly reconciled with post-hoc structure), and inferential reporting lacks effect sizes and key model/check details (e.g., handling of violated normality beyond stating CLT; no correction/robustness strategy stated).
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and uses standard scientific phrasing, with mostly correct grammar. APA/table/figure referencing is partially compliant, but the tables are not formatted per APA (e.g., headings and placement are inconsistent, and the tabular content is missing). There are a few imprecise or awkward expressions (e.g., 'Their values of SAD symptoms still fit the scale of the LSAS-CA' and spacing in N= 90; alpha formatting). The paragraphing is mostly logical (assumptions → omnibus ANOVA → post-hoc → interpretation), but there is some redundancy and a couple of vague transitions (e.g., 'as planned' without specifying the planned analysis details).
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion bands are mostly distinguishable by whether reporting is ~50% vs ~75% correct/complete, but it can be hard to judge without explicit checklist items (e.g., effect sizes, dfs consistency, assumption handling, and whether stats match the design). The criterion also blends 'correctness/completeness' with 'in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses and reasoning', which is not concretely defined—students could meet one part while missing the other. Consider splitting Results into (a) statistical reporting completeness (effects/df, assumption checks, correction strategy, effect sizes) and (b) analytical reasoning (linking omnibus to post-hocs, justifying handling of assumption violations). Adding concrete thresholds (e.g., 'must report at least one effect size such as partial eta-squared') would reduce ambiguity.
discussion_content_summary: The Discussion addresses the study aim by summarizing the main findings: both VRET and IVET reduced SAD symptoms more than the waitlist, with no reliable superiority of VRET over IVET at post-treatment. The reasoning generally remains consistent with the introduction (exposure mechanisms and adolescent relevance) and offers plausible explanations for the null VRET>IVET result (e.g., cognitive avoidance and differential fit of CBT components). The section also reflects on limitations (VR dialogue realism/uncertainty, therapeutic alliance) and proposes concrete future directions (more interactive VR scenarios; measuring alliance; testing VRET vs VRET+CBT). However, several theoretical claims are somewhat speculative and occasionally overconfident given the reported statistical reporting limitations in the earlier Results section, and the linkage to specific hypotheses is not always tightly argued.
discussion_style_notes: Overall scientific language is clear and mostly precise, with generally grammatical and readable sentences and a logical flow from findings to interpretation to limitations and future directions. APA-related issues include minor inconsistencies (e.g., “The therapy used…” phrasing; some awkward or imprecise constructions like “as was hypothesized” without restating which hypothesis/interaction), and the References list appears beyond what is needed for a single section (though the formatting itself is mostly acceptable). Paragraphing follows a standard structure (overview → prior evidence/theory → limitations → future work), but there is some redundancy (re-stating that all conditions decreased) and a few statements are ambiguous/vague (“offering further support,” “truth-value of the hypothesis”) or overly interpretive without reporting effect sizes or model-level details. Minor word-choice/grammar issues (e.g., “will be superior,” “emphasize the effectiveness”) reduce polish slightly.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric could more explicitly distinguish what counts as “in-depth knowledge” in Results versus “outstanding insight” in Discussion; right now, the Discussion criterion mainly repeats correct reasoning and responsible referencing without concrete anchors for statistical adequacy, effect size interpretation, or alignment with specific hypotheses. The thresholds for convincingness (e.g., 60% vs 80% conclusions) are difficult to operationalize consistently across graders; adding examples of evidence types that would raise a band (e.g., direct hypothesis-by-hypothesis mapping, effect size discussion, alternative explanations) would improve reliability. Finally, the rubric treats “responsible use of references” as a binary-like concept; clarifying whether it means correct citation, recency, and appropriate causal/precision claims (as opposed to merely mentioning prior work) would help assess quality more consistently.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric would benefit from clearer, more operational thresholds for “vague/ambiguous statements” and for what counts as “crucial parts of the text,” since those judgments can vary across raters. The “errors in APA style referencing or layout” criterion could be made more specific (e.g., specifying which APA elements—spacing, punctuation, table titles, in-text formatting—count equally) to improve consistency. Similarly, the rubric could clarify whether redundancy is evaluated qualitatively or via rough limits per section (it currently relies on broad descriptors like “some” vs “a lot”). Adding 1–2 concrete examples for each performance band (e.g., a typical APA mistake and a typical vague statement) would make the band distinctions easier to apply.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a coherent motivation by describing SAD’s adolescent prevalence, impact on peer development, and the treatment–implementation gap for exposure-based CBT. It lays down a theoretical rationale linking classical and operant conditioning to fear/avoidance, and it explains how IVET and VRET target maladaptive associations. The research gap is clearly articulated around the need to compare VRET vs. IVET in adolescents with a clinically diagnosed SAD population and against a waitlist control. The research aim and testable hypotheses are stated, but the logic for some hypothesis components is somewhat under-supported (e.g., the explicit superiority prediction for VRET over IVET is not fully derived from the prior adolescent-focused evidence presented).
intro_style_notes: Language is generally clear and appropriately scientific, with mostly precise construct use (SAD, IVET, VRET). Grammar and phrasing issues appear (e.g., some long/stacked sentences, and a few unclear or nonstandard formulations such as “participants that benefit from treatment” vs. “treated participants”), but errors do not seem severe. APA referencing is mostly present, though a couple of inconsistencies stand out (e.g., “Ahrens et al 2024” missing a comma; “APA, n.d.” is unclear without a full reference). Paragraphing is logical and topic-focused, with limited redundancy.
intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction banding could be made more distinguishable by specifying how many missing/weakly justified hypothesis elements (or how much of the hypothesis-to-evidence link) would separate adjacent bands. Consider adding explicit guidance on what counts as “relevant and up to date” references (e.g., whether seminal older theory references are automatically “outdated” or if they should be exempt). Likewise, defining “conclusions to argumentation” is ambiguous for introductions—authors could add a short definition such as whether it refers to inferences leading to the RQ/hypotheses. Finally, the rubric might benefit from a clearer threshold for what makes an RQ “vague or not entirely appropriate” versus “appropriate but may be flawed,” especially when the RQ is implicit rather than explicitly labeled.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section provides a generally coherent description of participants, random assignment, treatment conditions (VRET vs IVET), materials/systems used for VRET, and the primary dependent measure (LSAS-CA). The study procedure is described in sufficient operational detail (number of sessions, session structure, hierarchy creation, and exposure progression), making it mostly consistent with a trial comparing VRET and IVET against a waitlist. However, key methodological elements are still under-specified for scientific rigor (e.g., therapist fidelity/supervision, blinding, primary outcome timing beyond pre/post, handling of dropouts, and specifics of waitlist procedures). Overall, the design can be evaluated as mostly complete and appropriate, but not fully “complete and correct” according to the rubric.
methods_style_notes: The writing is generally clear and uses appropriate scientific vocabulary with mostly precise terms (e.g., VRET/IVET, LSAS-CA, exposure hierarchy). Grammar and wording are understandable with only minor issues (e.g., some long sentences and a few phrasing choices like “severe cognitive impairment” without operational definition). APA referencing appears mostly consistent in-text, though the section lacks details such as whether terms are introduced consistently (e.g., DRVET vs DRVET system) and some parenthetical references are used in a dense way. Paragraph structure is mostly logical (Participants/Materials/Procedure), with limited redundancy.
methods_rubric_feedback: To improve the rubric’s discriminability, the Methods bands could more explicitly separate “coherence with RQ/hypotheses” from “completeness/correctness of reporting,” since a design can be coherent but still lack several standard trial-specification elements. Consider adding concrete sub-criteria for trials (e.g., randomization procedure implementation details, allocation concealment, blinding for outcome assessment, treatment fidelity, timing of primary outcome, and attrition handling), because these are commonly decisive for whether methods are “complete.” The Results criterion mentions “in-depth knowledge” but the Methods criterion does not; adding guidance on what counts as sufficient “operationalization” (e.g., defining session content, adherence, and outcome timing) would make bands easier to apply consistently.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports sample size, basic outlier handling, descriptive-statistics pointers (Tables 1–2), and an appropriate overall 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA with main effects and a time-by-condition interaction. It then provides Holm-corrected post-hoc comparisons that match the stated design and reports relevant effect patterns (treatment conditions improving more than waitlist, and both showing pre–post reductions). However, there is limited transparency about the statistical values underlying the F-tests (e.g., degrees of freedom consistency/expected error term for the interaction) and the treatment of the Shapiro-Wilk violation is justified via the Central Limit Theorem rather than by an analysis/robustness strategy. The section also does not clearly report effect sizes, confidence intervals, or a coherent handling of the reported “no outliers at pre” vs “two outliers at post” and how those affect model assumptions beyond inclusion.
results_style_notes: Writing is generally clear, with logical paragraphing (sample/outliers → ANOVA → post-hoc → condition-by-time interpretation). Grammar and punctuation are mostly acceptable, but there are recurring minor issues (e.g., inconsistent spacing around p values, and “Their values ... and they were included” is wordy). APA formatting is partially compliant (tables/figure labels present) but the table captions are incomplete/unclear and statistical reporting would benefit from consistency (e.g., consistent formatting of F and p, and reporting effect sizes). Some redundancy occurs (e.g., reiteration of significant comparisons already summarized by F/interaction) and a few crucial details (assumption handling, df reasoning) are missing rather than just under-specified.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results criterion could more explicitly distinguish between (a) correct selection of analyses and (b) completeness/quality of statistical reporting (e.g., effect sizes, assumptions/robustness, and transparency of post-hoc structure). The band descriptors would benefit from clearer thresholds for what counts as “50%” vs “75%” correct reporting, possibly using concrete examples (e.g., reporting partial eta-squared, consistent df, and complete post-hoc specification). The criterion mentions “in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses” but does not define operational markers (effect sizes, correction details, model checks), making it harder to judge consistently across graders. Consider adding sub-bullets under the Results criterion to specify required elements for each design type (mixed ANOVA, repeated measures factors, post-hoc correction).
discussion_content_summary: The Discussion coherently restates the main results: all groups improved pre-to-post, waitlist showed symptom reduction, IVET and VRET both outperformed waitlist at post-treatment, and there was no significant difference between VRET and IVET. The section links the findings back to the theoretical rationale (classical/operant conditioning and exposure mechanisms) and offers plausible explanations for the lack of VRET superiority, including a role for cognitive avoidance and differential integration of CBT components. Limitations are addressed with relevant constructs (realism in VRET content, fear of uncertainty, and potential therapeutic alliance differences), followed by concrete future directions. Overall, the reasoning generally fits the hypotheses and research question, though some claims (e.g., “supported by previous research”) are asserted more broadly than the specific comparison warrants and could be more tightly grounded in the presented study outcomes and earlier evidence.
discussion_style_notes: Language is mostly clear and appropriately scientific, with a generally logical flow from results to interpretation to limitations and future work. Grammar and phrasing are understandable but include a few awkward forms and precision issues (e.g., “the expectation that the VRET condition will be superior” and “emphasize the effectiveness”); there are also a couple of inconsistent references to what exactly the hypothesis states versus what was found. APA referencing is mostly consistent, but there is at least one capitalization/style inconsistency (“References” block) and one potentially unnecessary/dodgy APA in-text phrasing (“as was hypothesized” without specifying the precise tested component). Paragraphing is generally structured with clear topic transitions; redundancy is limited.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric for Discussion could more clearly distinguish between (a) correct interpretation of results and (b) the depth/quality of explanation for null findings; currently, “most of the reasoning culminating in the main conclusion is correct” can overlap with “critical reflection” in ways that are hard to calibrate. It may help to add an explicit sub-criterion for alignment with the stated hypotheses and research question (e.g., whether each hypothesis is revisited and evaluated), separate from limitations/future directions. Additionally, the rubric does not define what counts as “outstanding insight” or “all references used responsibly” (thresholds for sufficient citation, avoiding overclaiming, and appropriateness of every reference), which can create grading subjectivity. Finally, the statistical reasoning expectation is only specified for Results, but Discussion often also makes inferential claims; clarifying whether inferential/causal language should be constrained could improve reliability.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric would benefit from clearer, more operational definitions of “scientific language use impedes proper understanding” versus “overall use is at least 60% correct,” since that threshold is subjective. The error-count cutoffs (e.g., 5 vs 10 vs >10) could be complemented with guidance on what counts as an “APA style/layout” error (e.g., spacing, capitalization, reference completeness) to improve consistency between graders. “Vague or ambiguous statements” is also somewhat underspecified—examples of what qualifies as “crucial parts” vs non-crucial would reduce ambiguity. Finally, the redundancy criterion could distinguish between acceptable repeated phrasing for structure versus redundant statistical restatement, which are different in severity and frequency.

intro_content_summary: The introduction clearly defines social anxiety disorder, motivates the adolescent focus using prevalence, onset timing, and developmental impact, and narrows to the clinical/treatment gap regarding limited uptake of in-vivo exposure. It provides a mostly coherent theoretical rationale linking conditioning processes to avoidance, and it explains how IVET and VRET target maladaptive associations and perceived safety. The paper’s aims and testable hypotheses are stated at the end, including a comparison of VRET vs IVET vs waitlist and predictions about differential symptom reductions. However, the chain from theory and prior work to the specific prediction that VRET will outperform IVET in adolescents is somewhat under-justified, and the final section blends study-aim wording with design details without sharpening the research question/hypothesis structure (e.g., primary outcome and exact contrasts).
intro_style_notes: The writing is generally clear and academically appropriate, with mostly well-structured paragraphs that follow the typical funnel from disorder → gap → mechanisms → prior evidence → study aim/hypotheses. Grammar and phrasing are mostly correct, though there are a few awkward or imprecise formulations (e.g., minor semantic repetition such as “exposure to…fear most,” and occasional clause-level issues). APA compliance appears mostly adequate for in-text citations, but reference formatting (e.g., “APA, n.d.” and inconsistent use like “Ahrens et al. 2024” vs “Ahrens et al., 2024”) suggests potential APA inconsistencies. Overall redundancy is moderate-to-high in places where mechanisms and advantages are restated, but it does not severely impede understanding.
intro_rubric_feedback: Some rubric thresholds could be made more concrete, especially distinguishing “line of reasoning mostly sound” (developing/adequate band) from “sound and coherent” (good/excellent). The criteria for the Introduction currently mix three constructs—RQ/hypothesis clarity, coherence of reasoning, and reference relevance—without specifying how many missing/weak elements should move a paper between adjacent bands. It would help to separate (a) RQ/hypothesis specification quality from (b) theoretical justification quality, and to clarify what counts as “outdated” or “irrelevant” references with an explicit time window or examples. Finally, the rubric could explicitly mention whether adequate design details belong in the Introduction, so the distinction between Introduction vs Method responsibilities is less ambiguous.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section is generally coherent and matches the stated aim of comparing VRET, IVET, and a waitlist for adolescents with SAD. Participants, key inclusion/exclusion criteria, random assignment, treatment length (10 sessions over ~5 weeks), and the primary measurement instrument (LSAS-CA) are described with reasonable completeness. However, crucial methodological details are missing or underspecified: treatment fidelity/therapist competence beyond initial training, primary outcome specification and timing (e.g., immediate post vs longer follow-up), details of randomization procedures (allocation concealment/blinding), and the analytic plan (which is required for a full “Methods” section but is not present). Overall, the design is mostly appropriate for the hypotheses, but the methods do not reach the level of completeness expected for the highest rubric bands.
methods_style_notes: Language is clear and mostly precise, with a logical flow from Participants → Materials → Procedure. Grammar and spelling appear correct, and terminology (VRET/IVET/LSAS-CA) is used consistently. APA referencing is mostly consistent for citations, though parenthetical citation formatting could be checked for strict APA style consistency (e.g., uniform use of “et al.” and year placement). Paragraphing is generally structured, but some sub-sections (e.g., VRET materials) contain multiple implementation details that would benefit from more tightly signposted organization.
methods_rubric_feedback: Some rubric descriptors overlap (e.g., “coherent and somewhat related” vs “coherent and mostly related”), which can make assigning boundaries between adjacent bands subjective—adding concrete indicators (e.g., whether blinding, fidelity, and analytic plan are required in this course) would help. The Methods band focuses on design coherence and completeness of participants/materials/procedures, but it doesn’t explicitly clarify whether an analytic/statistical plan belongs in the Methods section for this rubric’s purposes. The Results and Discussion criteria are fine, but for consistency you might define what “complete and correct” means operationally (e.g., minimum required details list) for Methods.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports the sample size, basic outlier handling, and a 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA on LSAS-CA, aligned with the methods’ aim of comparing VRET, IVET, and a waitlist. It provides key inferential statistics for main effects and the crucial interaction, plus post-hoc comparisons with Holm correction and descriptive references to Tables/Figure. However, the reporting lacks important analysis details (e.g., effect sizes, df justification for mixed ANOVA, use/handling of sphericity not addressed, and which post-hoc comparisons were included in the Holm procedure). The results are mostly coherent, but the statistical reporting is not fully complete or fully justified, and the claim about matching hypotheses is more interpretive than supported by specific contrasts/effect sizes.
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and uses appropriate statistical terminology, with sentences that mostly follow a logical order (assumptions → main effects/interaction → post-hocs). APA formatting issues appear in the table headings and inconsistent spacing (e.g., p=.004 vs p = .004, and table/figure label formatting), and there is minor inconsistency in naming (e.g., “The data of 90 participants” and mixed use of “post-treatment/post-treatment measurement”). Grammar/spelling errors are limited, but there are several vague/under-specified phrases in crucial analytic parts (e.g., description of Holm correction scope). Redundancy is low, though the section could be tighter by avoiding repeated “significant decrease” statements without additional quantitative context.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Results bands hinge on “correct/complete” reporting and “in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses,” but it does not specify concrete required elements (e.g., which effect sizes, how to report mixed ANOVA components, or whether sphericity is relevant for 2-level time). The boundary between “nearly complete” and “75% correct” is somewhat subjective—adding checklists or minimum reporting thresholds (effect sizes like partial eta-squared, confidence intervals, and exact planned contrasts) would improve reliability. The rubric also treats statistical reasoning and reporting as combined criteria, but provides no clear weighting or separate sub-criteria for (a) correctness of tests, (b) completeness of reporting, and (c) interpretive justification. Finally, it could clarify how to score when assumptions are tested but the response (e.g., robust methods or transformations) is not described.
discussion_content_summary: The Discussion states that VRET and IVET both reduced adolescent SAD symptoms from pre- to post-treatment, with both outperforming the waitlist, but without a significant VRET–IVET difference at post-treatment. It offers post-hoc theoretical explanations linking exposure-based mechanisms to conditioning and cognitive processes (e.g., cognitive avoidance), and it attempts to reconcile discrepancies with prior work (e.g., Bouchard et al., 2017). The section includes two limitations (VR scenario realism via semi-structured dialogue; potential therapeutic alliance differences) and provides some future directions (more interactive VR; measuring alliance; testing VRET+CBT). However, the discussion is unevenly anchored to the study’s specific hypothesis structure and the statistical results reported previously, and it contains some overconfident or slightly imprecise claims (e.g., effect comparisons stated as “emphasize” without clarifying the direction/size of the reported effects).
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally clear and understandable, with mostly appropriate scientific tone and coherent paragraphing (results recap → theoretical interpretation → limitations → future directions). Grammar is mostly correct, but there are several awkward/incorrect phrasing issues (e.g., “analyseS indicated,” “emphasize the effectiveness,” and “this present study found”). APA formatting is largely acceptable in references, but the section lacks explicit in-text APA consistency checks (e.g., some references are used as support without clear specificity to the exact finding). Some redundancy appears in restating general effectiveness, and there are a few vague or sweeping statements (e.g., about mechanisms) that are not tightly tied to the measured outcomes.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric could distinguish more explicitly between (a) correct alignment with hypotheses/RQ, (b) quality of interpretation, and (c) adequacy of limitations/future directions—currently these are bundled into broad bands that may be hard to differentiate. “Demonstrating outstanding insight into study limitations and/or future directions” is a helpful target but lacks concrete examples of what qualifies as “outstanding” versus “excellent/mostly relevant.” The language/style criterion mixes style with content clarity thresholds (e.g., “vague or ambiguous statements”), so clearer cutoffs (number/type and whether they must be crucial) would improve grading reliability. Finally, for Discussion, the rubric does not explicitly require linking limitations to specific study components and to the nonsignificant primary contrast, which could help standardize evaluation of hypothesis-consistent reasoning.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria use some broad thresholds (e.g., “overall scientific language at least 60% correct”) that are difficult to calibrate consistently across markers; it would help to define concrete indicators or example error types. The rubric mixes count-based cutoffs (e.g., vague statements, grammar/spelling, APA errors) with qualitative statements (e.g., structure clarity) without specifying how to prioritize them when they conflict. “APA style referencing or layout” could be decomposed into clearer subcategories (in-text citations, reference list, table/figure formatting) with exemplars. Finally, redundancy is assessed only qualitatively (“some” vs “lot”); providing a short rubric guidance on what counts as sentence-level vs paragraph-level redundancy would improve reliability.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear overview of SAD prevalence and developmental timing in adolescence, then motivates the clinical need for better-accepted adolescent treatments. It explains plausible learning-mechanism accounts for SAD and links these to exposure-based treatments, culminating in a focused rationale for comparing VRET and IVET. The research gap is articulated mainly as the lack of an adolescent RCT directly comparing VRET vs IVET with a control condition, and the hypotheses are stated as testable symptom changes across groups and time. However, the line of reasoning occasionally becomes overlong and partially underspecified (e.g., what exactly is meant by “a greater decrease,” what symptom measure/time window is targeted), and some inferences are asserted more strongly than the cited evidence supports.
intro_style_notes: The language is generally clear and mostly precise, with a logical paragraph flow from background → theory → prior evidence → present study. Grammar and spelling appear accurate overall, though there are minor issues such as awkward phrasing (e.g., “indulged in”) and a few imprecise or inconsistent terms (e.g., mixing IVET spelled-out descriptions and later “in-vivo exposure therapy” without consistent shorthand). APA style compliance is mostly acceptable, but there are at least one formatting inconsistencies/possible errors (e.g., missing space before a citation in “Ahrens et al 2024”; “APA, n.d.” use may be okay but should be checked for consistency). Paragraphing is coherent, but there is some redundancy and sentence-level complexity that could be tightened.
intro_rubric_feedback: Several rubric distinctions could be made sharper by specifying what counts as “clearly stated” vs “somewhat vague” for the RQ/hypotheses (e.g., require explicit operationalization: the exact outcome(s), time points, and comparison targets). The criteria for the introduction currently bundle together “line of reasoning,” “scientific relevance,” “hypothesis testability,” and “reference quality,” but without thresholds for how many references are “outdated/irrelevant” in practice. Adding concrete examples (e.g., what would qualify as “mostly unsound” reasoning vs “somewhat coherent”) would improve reliability, especially around the 40%/60%/75% bands for “convincing conclusions.” Finally, separating “RQs vs hypotheses” in scoring guidance would help because a study can have hypotheses while still lacking a truly coherent RQ statement.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section describes a randomized controlled trial with 90 adolescents with SAD, comparing VRET, IVET, and a waitlist condition. It provides reasonably detailed descriptions of VRET (equipment, scenarios, tailoring) and IVET (setting-based in vivo exposure), and it specifies the primary outcome measure using the LSAS-CA with scoring details. The procedure outlines screening, consent, randomization, session frequency/duration, and pre-/post-assessment. However, key methodological details are missing or under-specified (e.g., therapist allocation and treatment fidelity, blinding, waiting-list assessment timing, adherence/completion, randomization concealment, and how anxiety/hierarchy tailoring was operationalized over time).
methods_style_notes: Overall the language is clear and largely scientific, and the section is organized with headings for Participants, Materials, and Procedure. Grammar and terminology are mostly consistent (e.g., VRET/IVET, LSAS-CA), with only minor phrasing issues (e.g., “until their anxiety decreased” is vague). APA-style referencing appears largely correct in-text, though the section would benefit from more uniform citation formatting and clearer description of any borrowed procedures. There is some redundancy in describing session counts and exposure blocks, and several crucial methodological aspects are described narratively rather than with measurable/operational details.
methods_rubric_feedback: The rubric for Methods is helpful but could be made more discriminating by explicitly listing what “multiple important details” refers to (e.g., randomization concealment, blinding, treatment fidelity, adherence, attrition, therapist qualifications/number, and waitlist assessment schedule). The boundary between “somewhat related” and “related” to the RQ is not operationalized, making it hard to judge borderline cases. Consider adding examples for “unnecessary or irrelevant components” and for what counts as “complete and correct,” such as minimum sample description, outcome timing, and intervention delivery specifics. Finally, adding a brief checklist for design completeness would improve reliability of scoring across graders.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports sample size usage, handling of outliers, and provides a factorial mixed ANOVA with main effects and a time-by-condition interaction. It includes tests of normality and variance homogeneity and follows with Holm-corrected post-hoc comparisons across conditions and time points, referencing tables/figures for descriptive and visual results. However, several reporting and analysis details are incomplete or potentially inconsistent with a mixed design (e.g., degrees of freedom interpretation, justification for using CLT despite violated normality, and the treatment of waitlist pre/post change). The statistical reasoning remains mostly coherent, but the section lacks key information that would allow full verification of the reported inferential results.
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and appropriately scientific, with mostly concise sentences and logical paragraph progression. Grammar is mostly correct, but there are several issues with phrasing and consistency (e.g., missing spaces around punctuation, “Central Limit Theorem can be applied” wording, and minor formatting issues like table captions and indentation). APA compliance appears weak in places due to formatting inconsistencies (e.g., tables/figures presented without full APA-style structure) and unclear reporting completeness for statistical details (though APA is not the primary problem here). Overall redundancy is limited, but the section could be tightened by combining related assumption statements and post-hoc reporting.
results_rubric_feedback: The rubric’s Results criteria focus on “correctness” and “in-depth knowledge,” but it does not specify what minimum statistical reporting elements are expected (e.g., effect sizes, sphericity treatment/Greenhouse–Geisser if relevant, exact ANOVA model structure, and how df values should align with the design). Distinguishing between the “50%/75% correct” bands can be difficult without concrete thresholds or examples of typical missing reporting items. Consider adding sub-bullets that clarify what counts as incoherent versus merely incomplete reporting, and whether violations of assumptions require reporting specific corrective actions as part of “data analysis correctness.” Finally, “in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses” is broad; the rubric could name concrete indicators (effect sizes like partial eta-squared, correction methods, multiple comparisons structure).
discussion_content_summary: The discussion restates the study’s aim and summarizes that both VRET and IVET reduced SAD symptoms from pre- to post-treatment, while outperforming waitlist. It correctly notes the key non-significant VRET vs. IVET comparison at post-treatment and offers theoretical explanations for why VRET may not be superior. The section also provides limitations (VR dialogue structure and therapeutic alliance) and plausible future directions (more interactive VR and alliance measurement). However, parts of the argumentation are overconfident relative to what the (previously reported) statistical results would support, and some claims (e.g., emphasis patterns and “in line with” statements) are insufficiently tied to specific findings such as effect sizes or condition-by-time details.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally understandable and organized around the study’s results, mechanisms, limitations, and future directions. Grammar and spelling are mostly correct, though there are awkward phrases (e.g., “will be superior to,” “decreasing their effectiveness” phrasing) and some imprecise wording (“as a result of the randomized design”). APA formatting is mostly acceptable, but the reference list formatting includes inconsistent spacing (e.g., “Ahrens et al 2024”) and the discussion includes a citation style inconsistency like “Patients’ perceptions…” later; also some claims rely on citations without clearly stating which results those studies specifically support. Paragraph structure is present but occasionally dense, with multiple inferential claims in single paragraphs leading to minor redundancy (e.g., repeating that all conditions decreased).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric could more clearly separate “the reasoning culminating in the main conclusion is correct” from “interpretation is sufficiently supported by results,” because students can reach the same conclusion while still over-claiming beyond the statistical evidence. Distinguish more explicitly between (a) critical reflection that is relevant and (b) critical reflection that demonstrates outstanding insight into limitations/future directions (e.g., requiring alignment with design/measurement specifics and consideration of alternative explanations). For language & style, the threshold counts (e.g., “vague or ambiguous statements in crucial parts,” “different errors”) may be hard to operationalize; adding examples of what qualifies as a vague/ambiguous statement in quantitative inference could improve reliability.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style criteria would benefit from clearer, more operational cutoffs for “vague/ambiguous” statements and for what counts as an APA-style “error,” since those thresholds can be subjective. Consider adding examples or anchoring definitions (e.g., how to distinguish minor wording awkwardness from a true ambiguity; how to treat spacing/capitalization vs. missing structure in tables/figures). The band descriptions mix counts (e.g., errors) with qualitative judgments (e.g., “overall scientific language near faultless”), which could be better separated into distinct sub-dimensions or accompanied by a weighting rule.

intro_content_summary: The introduction provides a clear rationale for studying adolescent social anxiety disorder (SAD), including prevalence, developmental timing, and the treatment gap in exposure components. It then builds a theoretically grounded argument using classical and operant conditioning to explain avoidance and the mechanism of exposure therapies, before narrowing to VRET versus IVET in adolescents. The research problem is articulated as a lack of adolescent-specific, head-to-head evidence (especially with an appropriate control), and the study’s aim is stated as an RCT comparing VRET, IVET, and waitlist. The research question is not explicitly stated in a single sentence, and some references are used to support mechanism/attractiveness claims that are not fully tied to the specific hypotheses presented.
intro_style_notes: Language is generally clear and scientifically appropriate, with mostly well-structured paragraphs and topic focus. Grammar and phrasing are mostly accurate, though there are a few awkward constructions (e.g., “in-vivo exposure therapy (IVET; a type of therapy that focuses on…”) and some imprecise wording (“unconditioned stimulus” used as “initial negative interactions” without a tighter definition). APA issues are present: the text contains “APA, n.d.” for a source of prevalence-like descriptive information and there is inconsistent reference formatting (e.g., missing comma in “Ahrens et al. 2024”; occasional inconsistent capitalization/abbreviations like IVET vs IVET expansion). Redundancy is modest, but the mechanism section becomes somewhat explanatory at length before returning to the specific adolescent study aim.
intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction would benefit from a more explicit, concise research question (RQ) statement; the rubric currently evaluates “RQ clarity” but the criteria would be easier to apply if it distinguished clearly between an RQ being absent vs. being present but implicit. The band descriptors combine coherence and justification with reference relevance thresholds, which can blur the distinction between “sound but incomplete” reasoning and “reference problems” when only a few citations are off-topic. It would help to specify whether minor reference age/irrelevance affects the band only when it exceeds the stated proportion, and to provide an example of what counts as “mostly coherent line of reasoning.” Finally, the rubric could clarify how to treat theoretically relevant but indirectly supported claims (mechanism/attractiveness) when judging whether conclusions are convincing.
methods_content_summary: The Methods section gives a largely coherent overview of an RCT comparing VRET, IVET, and a waitlist, including participant recruitment, eligibility criteria, random assignment, treatment dose, session structure, and outcome measurement using the LSAS-CA. The description of VRET is fairly detailed (system components, headset used, scenario examples, and tailoring parameters), and IVET is described at an appropriate level (in vivo settings and therapist modelling). However, several key design/procedural elements are incomplete or insufficiently specified for scientific transparency (e.g., treatment manuals/control of therapist effects, blinding, handling of comorbidities/diagnostic procedures, and intercom/one-way mirror procedural specifics). Overall, the method is mostly complete and aligned with the research aims, but lacks multiple important details needed to fully evaluate validity and replication.
methods_style_notes: Language is generally clear and appropriately scientific, with mostly precise terminology (e.g., VRET/IVET, LSAS-CA subscales, session structure). Grammar and spelling are strong with only minor issues; the text is structured logically (Participants, Materials, Procedure). APA-style referencing appears mostly consistent in-text, though formatting of citations could be checked for exact APA compliance (e.g., consistent author–year style throughout). There is some redundancy/repetition in the description of the procedure timeline (e.g., session dosing and 5-week framing), but it does not substantially impede readability.
methods_rubric_feedback: The “Method” criterion would benefit from clearer thresholds for what counts as “75% complete” vs “lacking one or two important details.” Several sub-aspects that affect scientific rigor (e.g., blinding of assessors, allocation concealment, therapist treatment fidelity, and comorbidity diagnostics) are not explicitly enumerated in the criterion descriptors, making it harder to distinguish bands consistently. The band descriptors also mix “coherent relation to RQ” with “unnecessary components,” so it would help to separate coherence/alignment from completeness/replicability in the rubric wording. Adding concrete examples of “unnecessary details” and “important details/information” would improve reliability of scoring across raters.
results_content_summary: The Results section reports a coherent overview of the planned 3 (condition) × 2 (time) mixed ANOVA on LSAS-CA, including key assumption checks and the main inferential tests. It provides the crucial findings for main effects and the time-by-condition interaction, followed by Holm-corrected post hoc comparisons at both pre- and post-treatment time points, supported by references to Table 1/2 and Figure 1. However, some reporting is incomplete for a mixed design (e.g., degrees of freedom interpretation and the lack of effect sizes and baseline adjustment/estimated marginal means). Additionally, the justification for using the Central Limit Theorem after Shapiro-Wilk violation is questionable without clarifying how it affects ANOVA validity, and the outlier handling rationale is brief.
results_style_notes: Language is generally clear and suitably scientific, with mostly precise terms (e.g., main effect, interaction, post hoc, Holm correction). Paragraph structure is logical: assumptions → main ANOVA → post hoc → time-specific interpretations. There are minor precision/APA issues (e.g., inconsistent spacing like “N= 90”, and awkward phrasing such as “Their values… still fit the scale”). The section references Tables/Figure appropriately, but lacks reporting of commonly expected statistics (e.g., effect sizes), which affects precision more than grammar.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results-band distinctions would benefit from clearer, more concrete thresholds for what counts as “at least 75% correct” versus “correct and complete” (e.g., requiring effect sizes, estimated marginal means, and sufficient df reporting). The rubric could explicitly state whether “incorrect” includes misinterpretation/justification of assumption checks (e.g., CLT vs. ANOVA robustness) and how to grade partial compliance. The Results criterion also mentions “in-depth knowledge of statistical analyses,” but it does not define which elements are most important (effect sizes, assumptions for mixed ANOVA, handling outliers, multiple-comparison structure). Adding 1–2 example summaries of what “nearly complete” looks like for a mixed ANOVA would make the bands easier to distinguish.
discussion_content_summary: The discussion interprets the main pattern of results as improvements for all conditions, with both VRET and IVET outperforming the waitlist at post-treatment, and no reliable difference between VRET and IVET. It links these outcomes back to the conditioning/exposure rationale and uses prior RCTs to contextualize the waitlist and overall exposure effects. It also offers a plausible explanation for the null VRET>IVET effect via cognitive mechanisms (e.g., cognitive avoidance), and identifies limitations related to VR dialogue realism and therapeutic alliance. Overall, the discussion addresses the research aim reasonably, but some conclusions are overconfident (e.g., “decreased significantly more”) without acknowledging effect size/baseline/ANCOVA framing, and there is limited explicit connection to the specific RQ/hypothesis wording beyond broad statements.
discussion_style_notes: Language is generally clear and scientifically appropriate, with mostly coherent paragraphing and logical transitions. Grammar is mostly correct, but there are several awkward/incorrect wordings (e.g., “emphasize the effectiveness… compared to a baseline,” “patients’ view… probability… threatening and unacceptable” phrasing) and some tense/consistency issues (e.g., switching between “as was hypothesized” and later explanations). APA formatting is mostly acceptable, but in-text references and terminology could be more consistent (e.g., treatment-condition ordering and repeated constructs like “this present study”). Redundancy is moderate, with some repeated restatement of key findings and a few places where explanations could be tightened.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The rubric would benefit from clearer separation between (a) whether the discussion matches the results and research question and (b) whether the discussion demonstrates “outstanding insight” into limitations/future directions. For Discussion, the thresholds between bands (developing vs adequate vs good) are not fully operationalized—for example, “at least 75% of references are used responsibly” lacks concrete guidance on what counts as “responsibly” or how to judge relevance vs speculation. Adding explicit subcriteria such as: treatment of statistical evidence (effect sizes, non-significant comparisons, baseline adjustment) and calibration of claims to the strength of the findings would make grading more consistent. Finally, specifying what “outstanding insight” entails (e.g., identifying design threats with clear causal link to results, proposing testable future studies) would reduce ambiguity.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The Language & Style rubric could better separate APA-style/layout errors from content clarity: currently, “APA errors” and “vague/ambiguous statements” compete within the same thresholding, which makes grading feel less consistent. The numeric cutoffs (e.g., 5 vs. 10 grammar/spelling errors) would benefit from clearer operational definitions (e.g., whether formatting issues count, how to treat repeated similar errors, and how to count compound/related issues). “Overall use of scientific language is at least 60% correct/80% correct” is difficult to calibrate without examples or a scoring guide for what qualifies as an “incorrect” scientific-language usage. Adding brief anchor examples per band for each error category (APA, grammar, ambiguity, redundancy) would make the rubric easier to apply reliably.

intro_rubric_feedback: De introductie geeft een duidelijke klinische relevantie en werkt vanuit een onderhoudsmechanisme (vermijding/veiligheidsgedrag) naar een exposure-rationale en vervolgens naar VRET versus iVET. De RQ en hypothesen zijn aanwezig en doorgaans passend bij de aangehaalde literatuur, met een voorzichtige framing van generaliseerbaarheid naar adolescenten. De wetenschappelijke argumentatie is echter niet overal even strak/consistent (o.a. aansluiting tussen claims, specifieke condities en wat precies getest wordt), waardoor de overtuigingskracht richting de RQ niet op het hoogste niveau komt.
methods_rubric_feedback: De studieopzet en condities (VRET, iVET, wachtlijst) worden inhoudelijk redelijk beschreven, inclusief aantal sessies, duur en algemene exposure-opbouw. Het meetinstrument en meetmomenten zijn duidelijk, en basis inclusie/exclusie staat vermeld. Tegelijkertijd ontbreken methodologische details die nodig zijn om de analyse-interpretatie volledig te dragen (randomisatieprocedure/stratificatie, exacte operationalisatie van DSM-criteria via SCID-5 Junior, interventietrouw/therapeut-effect en primaire/sekundaire uitkomstdefinitie).
results_rubric_feedback: Er wordt een passend mixed ANOVA/Factorial (met meetmoment als within-factor) gerapporteerd en er worden assumptiechecks genoemd, wat de resultatenbasis goed ondersteunt. De interpretatie van post-hoc vergelijkingen en de gerapporteerde p-waarden lijken echter niet consistent (en sommige zinnen zijn statistisch/taalkundig problematisch), waardoor de argumentatie vanuit de toetsing minder betrouwbaar overkomt. Bovendien ontbreken effectgroottes en (idealiter) betrouwbaarheidsintervallen, waardoor de sterkte van de conclusies onvoldoende onderbouwd is.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie koppelt de belangrijkste uitkomst (VRET toont verandering, iVET en wachtlijst niet) aan verwachtingen en aan relevante theorie (habituatie en inhibitory learning), en positioneert dit binnen eerdere literatuur. De critical reflection is aanwezig (individualisering, zelfrapportage), maar blijft vrij algemeen en soms niet volledig afgestemd op specifieke design- of analysebeperkingen die de resultaten kunnen verklaren. Ook worden alternatieve verklaringen (bv. timing/therapeut contact, meet-/selectie-effecten, multiple comparisons en ontbrekende effectmaten) niet sterk genoeg systematisch besproken.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst is overwegend goed gestructureerd met duidelijke sectiekoppen en academische taal. Er zijn echter meerdere formuleringen die onnauwkeurig of verwarrend zijn (o.a. statistische zinsbouw/interpretatie in Results en enkele type-/spelfouten in de literatuurlijst), wat de wetenschappelijke helderheid vermindert. APA-lay-out is niet consequent (tabellen/figuurverwijzingen en formatting), waardoor het taal- en stijlniveau niet volledig het hoogste bandniveau haalt.
final_grade_justification: De paper bevat een duidelijke probleemstelling, plausibele rationale en een logische opbouw van de RQ en hypothesen, maar de argumentatie heeft op meerdere plekken zwakke/onjuiste statistische en methodologische onderbouwing waardoor de wetenschappelijke lijn niet volledig stevig is. De Methods zijn grotendeels compleet (design, condities, instrument, procedure), maar cruciale details zoals randomisatieprocedure, toewijzingscontrole en procedurele consistentie (incl. “cut-off” als DSM-criteria operationalisatie) blijven onvoldoende precies. In de Results worden wel correcte basisanalyses gerapporteerd, maar post-hoc p-waarden en interpretaties zijn inconsistent/onzorgvuldig geformuleerd (o.a. Holm-analyse waaruit strijdige claims volgen) en er ontbreken effectgroottes/vertrouwenmaten, waardoor de argumentatie rond bevindingen minder overtuigend is. De Discussion herneemt de uitkomsten coherent en koppelt ze aan theorie en eerdere literatuur, maar critical reflection blijft beperkt en er worden problemen onvoldoende systematisch naar de data/analyses teruggekoppeld; overall resulterend in een middenmarge beoordeling.

intro_rubric_feedback: De introductie bouwt een grotendeels coherente redeneerlijn op (SAS bij adolescenten → onderhoudsmechanismen → exposure → VRET als potentiële verbetering → rationale voor RQ/hypotheses). De RQ en hypotheses zijn als doelstelling en verwachtingen herkenbaar, en verwijzingen worden overwegend inhoudelijk passend ingezet. Wel ontbreekt soms scherpte/precisie (bijv. wat precies ‘effectiever’ inhoudt in statistische termen, en enkele claims over generaliseerbaarheid/instapdrempel zijn niet volledig onderbouwd), waardoor de argumentatie net niet helemaal ‘heel coherent en overtuigend’ is.
methods_rubric_feedback: De methodes zijn qua opzet en condities coherent (drie-arm design, aantal sessies, algemene procedure, meetinstrumenten) en bieden veel praktische details over VRET/iVET-techniek. Tegelijkertijd ontbreken enkele kerncomponenten die de wetenschappelijke controleerbaarheid sterk beïnvloeden, zoals randomisatieprocedure en eventuele blindering, comcomitante behandelingen/therapietrouw, verloop/uitval (CONSORT-flow), en meer concrete operationalisaties (o.a. exacte interpretatie/cut-off en scoring). Daardoor is het moeilijk om de studie volledig te reconstrueren en te beoordelen op interne validiteit.
results_rubric_feedback: De resultatenrapportage bevat relevante analyse-keuzes (mixed ANOVA met meetmoment × conditie) en controle op assumpties wordt benoemd. Echter, de post-hoc toetsing en de gerapporteerde p-waarden/tekst zijn inconsistent (bijv. er staan p-waarden die niet passen bij ‘geen significant verschil’ in de tekst, en er wordt ook een interpretatie gegeven die niet eenduidig uit de gerapporteerde post-hoc kan worden afgeleid). Daarnaast ontbreken effectgroottes en voldoende statistische details om de conclusies inhoudelijk sterk te onderbouwen.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie verbindt de gevonden patroonverschillen aan theorie (habituatie en inhibitory learning) en plaatst resultaten in de bredere literatuur, wat de interpretatie richting geeft. De kritische reflectie benoemt relevante beperkingen (individualisering van scenario’s, zelfrapportage, follow-up), maar behandelt niet alle belangrijke interpretatievragen (zoals de statistische/rapportage-inconsistenties en wat dit betekent voor causaliteit en sterkte van conclusies). Hierdoor komt de discussie niet volledig in de ‘zeer overtuigend’ band qua wetenschappelijke argumentatie en reflectie.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De taal is grotendeels helder en wetenschappelijk, en paragrafen zijn doorgaans logisch opgebouwd van probleem → interventie → resultaten → interpretatie. Er zijn wel meerdere slordigheden en inconsistenties in formulering/terminologie (o.a. spelling/cijfers in de tabellen en onduidelijke of foutieve statistische formuleringen), plus enkele redundante of vagere passages in cruciale delen. Ook zijn er problemen met APA-lay-out/bronvermelding (zoals inconsistenties in referenties en een lastig leesbaar literatuuroverzicht), waardoor de kwaliteit net onder ‘goed/excellent’ uitkomt.
final_grade_justification: De paper presenteert een duidelijke klinische focus en bevat een relevante introductielijn richting de RQ en hypotheses, maar de onderbouwing is op onderdelen slordig/ineensprongen en er ontbreken enkele cruciale preciseringen voor een volledig wetenschappelijk argument. De methodes zijn grotendeels compleet op constructie- en procedureel niveau (design, condities, meetinstrument), maar belangrijke details voor replicatie/validiteit ontbreken (o.a. randomisatieprocedure, blindering/therapietrouw, interventiecheck, uitval/flow, exacte meetmomenten en scoreberekening). In de resultaten worden wel de juiste kernanalyses gerapporteerd (mixed ANOVA), maar de post-hoc conclusies bevatten tegenstrijdigheden en er worden significantiewaarden gerapporteerd die niet logisch aansluiten op de tekst (en effectgroottes/variantieverklaringen ontbreken). De discussie koppelt resultaten aan theorie en benoemt beperkingen, maar de kritische reflectie blijft selectief en de interpretatie van de iVET-uitkomst en de statistische inconsistenties wordt onvoldoende wetenschappelijk uitgewerkt.

intro_rubric_feedback: De introductie bouwt een (globale) redeneringslijn op van prevalentie/klinische relevantie naar onderhoudsmechanismen en naar een rationale voor VRET, wat leidt tot een duidelijke en relevante RQ/hypotheses. Referenties worden over het algemeen verantwoord ingezet om claims te onderbouwen. De samenhang richting de specifieke vergelijking VRET vs. iVET is overtuigend, maar de tekst zou baat hebben bij strakkere onderbouwing van waarom iVET in deze populatie specifiek zou kunnen onderpresteren (meer expliciete koppeling aan meet/ontwerp).
methods_rubric_feedback: De studieopzet (3 condities, pre/post, random toewijzing) en de interventies zijn grotendeels goed beschreven, inclusief duur, sessieopbouw en belangrijke apparatuur/materialen. Tegelijkertijd ontbreken enkele cruciale methodologische ankers die voor reproduceerbaarheid en interne validiteit nodig zijn (randomisatieprocedure, blinding, therapietrouw/continuïteit, uitval en missing data). Ook is niet volledig duidelijk hoe de LSAS-CA cut-off-score precies in de inclusie is gebruikt en hoe andere therapie-/sessievariabelen zijn gestandaardiseerd tussen condities.
results_rubric_feedback: De data-analyse wordt inhoudelijk passend gemotiveerd (mixed ANOVA met meetmoment en conditie) en de kernboodschap (alleen VRET toont significante afname) wordt consistent doorgezet. Rapportage van assumptietests en tabellen/figuur-verwijzingen ondersteunt de transparantie. Er zijn echter rapportageproblemen/inconsistenties in de post-hoc p-waarden/correcties en ontbreekt meer inhoudelijke statistische onderbouwing zoals effectgroottes en (idealiter) expliciete interpretatie van de interactie in effecttermen.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie koppelt de belangrijkste bevindingen aan de verwachtingen en positioneert VRET als effectiever dan iVET en wachtlijst, met een mechanistische interpretatie via habituation/inhibitory learning. De kritische reflectie is aanwezig (scenario-individualisering, zelfrapportage) maar blijft vrij algemeen en mist soms een scherpere confrontatie met alternatieve verklaringen voor het onverwachte iVET-resultaat. Voor het bandniveau “Excellent” ontbreekt nog een zeer overtuigende, diepere reflectie die de effectiviteit/niet-effectiviteit logisch verbindt met concrete ontwerpaspecten en statistische beperkingen.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De taal is overwegend wetenschappelijk en goed te volgen, met duidelijke alinea’s en logische overgangen. Er zijn wel meervoudige tikfouten/inhoudelijke onhandigheden in zinsbouw (o.a. in tabellen/codering en enkele doublures) en enkele onduidelijkheden in APA/format (zoals ontbrekende/gescheurde tabelinformatie). Over het geheel is de stijl voldoende gestructureerd, maar de precisie van formuleringen en consistentie in rapportage kan beter.
final_grade_justification: De paper presenteert een duidelijke inhoudelijke lijn: sociale angst in adolescentie, onderhoudsmechanismen, en waarom exposure via VRET mogelijk aantrekkelijker/haalbaarder is dan iVET, wat resulteert in een relevante RQ en hypotheses. De methodes zijn grotendeels compleet (design, participanten, instrument, interventieprocedures), maar er ontbreken essentiële details zoals randomisatieprocedure/allocatie, therapietrouw en uitval/ontbrekende data, en de mixed ANOVA-rapportage bevat inconsistenties die de interpretatie verzwakken. De resultaten zijn overwegend correct gerapporteerd qua analyses en patroon (VRET significant verbeterd, iVET niet t.o.v. wachtlijst), maar er worden p-waarden gerapporteerd die niet logisch lijken met Holm/correctie en met ontbrekende effectgroottes/confident intervals. In de discussie worden de belangrijkste conclusies redelijk coherent afgeleid, met plausibele mechanismen en beperkte kritische reflectie, maar de paper mist sterkere argumentatie rond waarom iVET faalde en rond generaliseerbaarheid/validiteit.

intro_rubric_feedback: De Introduction bevat een herkenbare lijn van klinische relevantie en theoretische mechanismen (vermijding/veiligheidsgedrag → exposure → inhibitory learning/habituatie → VRET als alternatief), wat leidt tot een duidelijke RQ en verwachtingen. Sommige delen blijven echter conceptueel of redactioneel wat algemeen/ambigu (o.a. formuleringen over “verwachte verschillen” en de onderbouwing van waarom VRET specifiek superieur moet zijn ten opzichte van iVET in deze populatie). De literatuur lijkt doorgaans relevant en wordt met redelijke voorzichtigheid gebruikt, al zijn er plekken waar claims meer afbakening of expliciete aansluiting op adolescenten-SAS nodig hebben.
methods_rubric_feedback: De Methods sectie beschrijft deelnemers, instrument, interventiecomponenten (aantal sessies, globale opbouw, materialen) en procedure, waardoor de design- en interventie-informatie over het algemeen coherent is. Toch ontbreken cruciale details die nodig zijn voor volledige reproduceerbaarheid en correcte wetenschappelijke interpretatie (o.a. randomisatieprocedure/allocatie, blindering, therapietrouw/controle, kenmerken van de meetprocedure/afname, en volledige instrument-/cut-off onderbouwing). Ook staan er consistente rapportageproblemen in de resultaten/ANOVA-interpretatie die wijzen op tekortschietende statistische precisie, en dat hangt samen met methodologische zorgvuldigheid.
results_rubric_feedback: De analyses worden op hoofdlijnen passend gepresenteerd (mixed ANOVA met conditie en meetmoment, assumption checks, post-hoc met Holm-correctie). Er zijn echter serieuze slordigheden/consistentieproblemen in de post-hoc beschrijving (meerdere p-waarden “p = .007” en mogelijke verwarring tussen significantie en p-waarde; bovendien ontbreken tabellen voor groepsgemiddelden/SE’s/effectgroottes). Daardoor wordt de interpretatie minder betrouwbaar en wordt de eis van “correct, complete en coherent reporting” niet volledig gehaald, zelfs al is de algemene richting van effecten plausibel.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De Discussion leidt redelijk naar de hoofdconclusie (VRET effectiever dan iVET en wachtlijst), en de interpretatie wordt gekoppeld aan relevante exposure-mechanismen en eerdere literatuur. De kritische reflectie benoemt beperkingen (individualisering, zelfrapportage) en doet suggesties voor vervolgonderzoek, wat positief is. De discussie mist echter een overtuigend wetenschappelijk doordenken van alternatieve verklaringen en van de implicaties van de statistische onduidelijkheden (zoals waarom iVET uitblijft en welke analyses/effectgroottes dit ondersteunen), waardoor de argumentatieve sterkte beperkt blijft.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Taal en structuur zijn grotendeels wetenschappelijk en leesbaar, met duidelijke alinea’s en een begrijpelijke opbouw van secties. Er zijn echter meerdere inhoudelijk-relevante formulering- en typografische/formattingproblemen (o.a. inconsistenties in figuur/tabelpresentatie en slordige statistische formuleringen), plus enkele APA-/opmaakissues in de literatuurlijst. Bovendien is er in een aantal passages redundantie of dubbeling (zelfde punt meerdere keren herhaald) en ontbreken kerninformatie/duidelijkheid op plaatsen waar die cruciaal is voor interpretatie.
final_grade_justification: De paper levert een duidelijk onderzoeksdoel en een logisch effectmechanisme voor VRET, en de studie-opzet (3 condities, pre/post) past inhoudelijk bij de onderzoeksvraag. Tegelijkertijd zijn er duidelijke problemen in de methodologische precisie/rapportage en in de statistische uitvoering en interpretatie (o.a. tegenstrijdige/onzorgvuldige post-hoc p-waarden en het ontbreken van effectgroottes en betrouwbaarheid van de conclusies). De Discussion is inhoudelijk behoorlijk coherent en koppelt aan theorie en beperkingen, maar mist overtuigende kritische wetenschappelijke afbakening van wat de resultaten precies bewijzen. Als geheel komt dit uit op een midden-band: deels adequaat wetenschappelijk, maar met tekortkomingen die de argumentatieve sterkte en validiteit van de claims beperken.

intro_rubric_feedback: De introductie bevat een redelijk coherente redeneringslijn (klinische relevantie → instandhoudingsmechanismen → exposuretherapie → rationale voor VRET i.p.v. iVET) die uitmondt in een duidelijke RQ en toetsbare hypothesen. Referenties worden overwegend verantwoord ingezet om claims/theorie te ondersteunen. Wel profiteert de argumentatie van meer expliciete aansluiting tussen de eerdere bevindingen en precies waarom iVET in deze adolescentpopulatie zou falen (in plaats van alleen algemeen intensiteits-/contextargument).
methods_rubric_feedback: De methodes zijn over het algemeen goed gestructureerd en bevatten voldoende informatie over participanten, instrument, interventieduur/-onderdelen, en procedure om het onderzoek te begrijpen. Randomisatie wordt genoemd maar zonder procedurele details, en belangrijke kwaliteits-/controlecomponenten (bv. fidelity, adherence, blinding van meet/analisten, co-interventies, uitval/ missing data-plan) worden niet beschreven. Verder zijn de inclusie/exclusiecriteria grotendeels helder, maar sommige beslissingen rond cut-off/SCID-5-criteria blijven te weinig onderbouwd of concreet uitgewerkt.
results_rubric_feedback: De analysekeuze (mixed ANOVA met meetmoment en conditie) is passend bij het pre-post design en assumpties worden kort gerapporteerd. Rapportage is grotendeels compleet (F-waarden en p-waarden), maar er ontbreken effectgroottes en duidelijke tabel/tekstlinking met de claims. Daarnaast zijn er inconsistenties in de post-hoc beschrijving/interpretatie (meerdere p-waarden met Holm-correctie lijken niet overeen te komen met de latere samenvatting van (on)significantie), waardoor de argumentatieve kwaliteit van de statistische conclusie daalt.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie herneemt doel en verwachtingen en koppelt de belangrijkste bevinding (alleen VRET significant verbeterd) aan de theorie en eerdere literatuur. Kritische reflectie op de iVET-uitkomst is aanwezig en de beperkingen (self-report, scenario-individualisering) zijn relevant, met suggesties voor toekomstig onderzoek. Toch blijft de bespreking van alternatieve verklaringen en robuustheid (bv. meetgevoeligheid, procesmechanismen, mogelijke beperkingen van de statistische interpretatie) relatief beperkt, waardoor de diepgang richting de hoogste rubricband niet wordt bereikt.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Taal en structuur zijn grotendeels wetenschappelijk en begrijpelijk, met duidelijke paragrafen en logische overgangen. Er staan echter meerdere type-/spelfouten en inconsistenties in formuleringen (o.a. in de resultaten/figuurverwijzingen en enkele namen/termen), en de APA/layout is niet volledig consistent (o.a. literatuurlijst en tabel/figuurpresentatie). Ook bevat de tekst in enkele plaatsen ambiguïteit of net iets te stellige interpretaties zonder extra ondersteuning, wat de precisie van argumentatie beperkt.
final_grade_justification: De paper levert een duidelijke wetenschappelijke probleemstelling en een logische redeneringslijn richting RQ/hypothesen, met relevante theoretische koppelingen naar habituatie en inhibitory learning. Methodologisch is het ontwerp coherent en vrijwel volledig beschreven (steekproef, design, interventiecomponenten, meetinstrument), maar enkele cruciale details ontbreken of zijn niet volledig uitgewerkt (bv. exacte randomisatie-/toewijzingsprocedures, adherence/fidelity, ontbrekende statistische effectgroottes en onduidelijkheden in de post-hoc interpretatie). De resultatenrapportage is grotendeels correct en aansluitend op het design, maar de post-hoc p-waarden lijken inconsequent met de claims en er worden geen effectgroottes/intervalschattingen gepresenteerd, waardoor de statistische argumentatie minder sterk is. In de discussie wordt de hoofdconclusie grotendeels correct gekoppeld aan de resultaten, met passende beperkingen en suggesties; toch blijven sommige interpretaties speculatief en ontbreekt diepere kritische reflectie op alternatieve verklaringen (bv. procesdata, therapietrouw en meet/analyses-issues).

intro_rubric_feedback: De lijn van redenering naar de RQ en (expliciete) hypothesen is aanwezig en over het algemeen coherent: probleemstelling, rationale voor VRET versus iVET en onderbouwing via exposure-mechanismen worden duidelijk uitgewerkt. De kernreferenties zijn grotendeels relevant en actueel, maar er zijn enkele slordigheden/ambiguïteiten in terminologie (o.a. DSM/SCID verwijzingen) en de literatuurclaim rond uitblijven van effecten bij iVET blijft theoretisch minder strikt gekoppeld aan concrete verwachtingen. Daardoor is het argumentatief geheel sterk, maar niet volledig “perfect” volgens de rubriccriteria.
methods_rubric_feedback: De studie-opzet is coherent met het beoogde doel en bevat relevante onderdelen (steekproef, inclusie/exclusie, meetinstrument, condities, procedure en algemene analysecontext). De methode mist echter enkele cruciale details/helderheid die bij een perfecte uitvoering verwacht worden (bijv. exacte randomisatieprocedure, timing/duur van interventie voor iedereen, therapist-blinding/onderzoekersrol, interventietrouw/monitoring, en concreet operationaliseren van iVET-procedures). Ondanks dat zijn de kernmethoden voor deelnemers, materialen en procedure grotendeels compleet en begrijpelijk.
results_rubric_feedback: De statistische analyse (mixed ANOVA) en belangrijkste effecten/assumptietests worden gerapporteerd, en het is duidelijk dat het ontwerp (conditie × meetmoment) is meegenomen. Tegelijkertijd is de post-hoc rapportage inconsistent/onduidelijk (waarschijnlijke p-waarden die niet logisch lijken per vergelijking) en ontbreken essentiële informatie voor interpretatie (bijv. effectgroottes/df’s per vergelijking, meerdere vergelijking-correcties per contrast helder, en effectgrootte/CI’s). Ook wordt de claim over “uitsluitend VRET significant” niet volledig onderbouwd met een transparante set resultaten voor alle contrasten.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie koppelt de bevindingen aan de verwachtingen en aan relevante theorie (habituatie/inhibitory learning) en gebruikt literatuur over VRET/iVET en doelgroepverschillen verantwoord. Er is ook aandacht voor beperkingen en toekomstige richtingen (individualisering, zelfrapportage, follow-up), wat de critical reflection ondersteunt. De kwaliteit van het argument voor waarom iVET uitblijft in deze studie is echter nog wat algemeen en niet volledig getoetst aan alternatieve verklaringen (bv. power, drop-out/uitval, fidelity, baseline variatie, contextverschillen), waardoor de “outstanding insight” niet helemaal wordt gehaald.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst is overwegend wetenschappelijk en redelijk gestructureerd per onderdeel, met duidelijke overgangen en doorgaans begrijpelijke formuleringen. Er is echter sprake van meerdere taal-/vormproblemen (o.a. inconsistenties/typografische fouten en enkele onhandige of onduidelijke formuleringen rond kernbegrippen en p-waarde-rapportage), plus enkele APA-/opmaakissues zoals ontbrekende/ondanks verwijzingen niet volledig consistente figuur- of tabelvermeldingen. Daardoor is de algemene wetenschappelijke helderheid niet consequent “bijna foutloos”, wat de score beperkt.

intro_rubric_feedback: De introductie bevat een duidelijke en grotendeels logische lijn die van prevalentie/maatschappelijke relevantie via mechanismen van sociale angst naar een toetsbare verwachting over VRET vs. iVET leidt. De RQ/hypotheses worden expliciet geformuleerd en de theoretische onderbouwing (vermijding/veiligheidsgedrag → exposure; habituatie/inhibitory learning → VRET) is coherent. De literatuur is overwegend relevant en actueel, maar er is ruimte om selectiever en consistenter om te gaan met wat precies wordt ingezet om de specifieke vergelijking (VRET vs. iVET in deze adolescentendoelgroep) te dragen.
methods_rubric_feedback: De methodes zijn over het algemeen goed gestructureerd en het design sluit aan op de verwachtingen (randomisatie naar VRET/iVET/wachtlijst; pre/post; LSAS-CA). De beschrijving van participanten, interventiecomponenten en apparatuur is grotendeels concreet en maakt replicatie redelijk mogelijk. Toch ontbreken meerdere belangrijke details (o.a. exacte randomisatieprocedure, toewijzing/allocatie-informatie, therapietrouw/controle van behandelfidelity, aantal/rol van uitvallers of missing data-afhandeling, en meer procedurele info rond meting/therapieduur/frequentie).
results_rubric_feedback: De analyse-adressering (FM-ANOVA met interactie en post-hoc met Holm) is op hoofdlijnen coherent met het 3x2 design en wordt kwantitatief gerapporteerd. Echter, de resultaten zijn incompleet in de rapportage: essentiële effectmaten/df-correctheden/volledige post-hoc details ontbreken of zijn inconsistent beschreven (o.a. onduidelijke p-waarden en verwarring in significante versus niet-significante uitspraken). Daarnaast ontbreekt informatie over inferentiële statistiek waarop conclusies stevig kunnen rusten (bijv. effect sizes, betrouwbaarheid/CL, en consistente interpretatie van de condition×time interactie t.o.v. de post-hoc bevindingen).
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie vat de hoofdbevindingen helder samen en biedt een plausibele theoretische verklaring voor het VRET-effect, met een poging tot verklaring voor het uitblijven van iVET-effect. Kritische reflectie op beperkingen is aanwezig en redelijk relevant (individualisering/scenario’s en zelfrapportage) met concrete suggesties voor vervolgonderzoek. Tegelijkertijd is de discussie niet volledig overtuigend/onderbouwend waar het gaat om causaliteit en om alternatieve verklaringen (bijv. fidelity, sessiebetrouwbaarheid, implementatieverschillen, power/klinische relevantie, en mogelijke interpretatieproblemen door de manier van resultatenrapportage).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst gebruikt overwegend wetenschappelijke taal en de onderdelen (intro/methodes/resultaten/discussie) zijn herkenbaar, met meestal duidelijke zinsbouw en doelgerichte formuleringen. Er zijn echter meerdere taalkundige/APA-layout-issues en inconsistenties (bijv. enkele spelfouten/inkleuring in het Nederlands/Engels, en inconsistent gebruik van symbolen en p-waarde-notatie), plus een paar vaagheden in crucialere interpretatiezinnen. Het geheel blijft leesbaar, maar de stijl voldoet niet consequent aan de hogere bandbreedtes voor “bijna foutloos” en “geen” ambiguïteit.

intro_rubric_feedback: De inleiding bevat een duidelijke en grotendeels coherente lijn van redeneren die uitmondt in een relevante onderzoeksvraag en passende hypothesen. De referenties lijken overwegend relevant en actueel, en de rol van theorie (vermijding/veiligheidsgedrag, inhibitory learning/habituatie) wordt functioneel ingezet. Wel is de afbakening rond de specifieke vergelijking (waarom VRET boven iVET in deze populatie) op sommige plekken iets minder scherp onderbouwd dan de hoeveelheid theorie suggereert.
methods_rubric_feedback: De methodes zijn grotendeels coherent met de RQ/hypothesen: design, steekproef, condities, meetinstrument en interventie-essentie worden goed beschreven. De details over VRET (sessies, opbouw, hardware/software, scenario’s) en iVET (parallelle principes, realistische setting) geven voldoende indruk van uitvoerbaarheid. Er ontbreken nog enkele typisch cruciale methodedoelen voor een “paper-grade” reproductie (bijv. primaire uitkomststrategie/analysesplan meer expliciet, procedurele timing/intensiteit en randomisatie-/blinderingdetails).
results_rubric_feedback: De resultatenrapportage is inhoudelijk grotendeels correct en in lijn met het (mixed) ANOVA-design, inclusief assumptietests, hoofdeffecten en interactie. De post-hoc beschrijving is echter niet volledig helder en bevat inconsistenties/interpretatieproblemen (o.a. de gerapporteerde p-waarden voor verschillen bij prebehandeling lijken niet met de tekst “geen significante verschillen” te rijmen). Er ontbreekt ook een expliciet effectmaatkader (naast F en p) en volledige controle op de gerapporteerde toetsrichting/vergelijkingen.
