discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie vat de belangrijkste bevindingen helder samen, koppelt ze aan theorie en positioneert ze in eerder onderzoek. De kritische reflectie op beperkingen is aanwezig en leidt naar plausibele toekomstrichtingen (individualisering, meetmethode, follow-up). De discussie gebruikt referenties doorgaans verantwoord, maar de argumentatie over ‘waarom’ iVET uitblijft en de statistische implicaties (bv. sterkte van effecten, relevante covariaten/alternatieve verklaringen) kunnen concreter en beter onderbouwd worden met de resultaten.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst is over het algemeen begrijpelijk en wetenschappelijk van toon, met duidelijke alinea’s en een logische opbouw per sectie. Er blijven echter diverse taal-/stijlissues die net onder de “goede” band vallen (o.a. grammaticale formuleringen, spatiëring/consistentie, en een paar onzorgvuldige of onduidelijke formuleringen). De APA/layout is niet consequent (o.a. tabel/figuur-adressering en inconsistenties in verwijzingen/terminologie), wat de leesbaarheid beïnvloedt.

intro_rubric_feedback: De inleiding presenteert een duidelijke argumentatielijn van prevalentie/maatschappelijke relevantie naar theoretische mechanismen en vervolgens naar de rationale voor VRET versus iVET. De RQ/hypothesen worden helder geformuleerd en de meeste referenties zijn relevant en up-to-date. Het geheel blijft vooral sterk in coherentie en wetenschappelijke positionering, met ruimte om de laatste stap naar de specifieke onderzoeksvraag nog strakker te formuleren (bijv. termen rond “effectiviteit” en de specifieke uitkomstmaten/verwachtingen nog preciezer af te bakenen).
methods_rubric_feedback: De methode is over het algemeen coherent met de RQ, inclusief deelnemers, instrumentatie, conditie-interventies, en een logische procedure met pre/postmetingen en randomisatie. De beschrijving van VRET en iVET bevat veel operationele details (sessies, duur, opbouw, scenario’s/techniek) en lijkt methodologisch compleet. Wel zijn er relatief kleine tekortkomingen: exacte kenmerken zoals randomisatieprocedure/blinding en sommige kwaliteits-/controlemaatregelen worden niet gerapporteerd, en de cut-off/SCID-5-de operationalisatie wordt summier omschreven.
results_rubric_feedback: De statistische analyse wordt vermeld (mixed ANOVA, assumptiechecks, hoofdeffecten en interactie) en er is post-hoc rapportage met relevante p-waarden. Echter, de resultatenrapportage is deels onvolledig/tegenstrijdig: meerdere p-waarden zijn inconsistent (bijv. “geen significante verschillen” met p = .007) en er worden geen F-waarden/effectgroottes/df-analyses per contrast gegeven, waardoor het moeilijk is de interpretatie volledig te verifiëren. Bovendien ontbreekt functionele toelichting bij de richting/omvang van effecten (bijv. gemiddelden per conditie worden wel deels getoond, maar zonder duidelijke volledige tabel en zonder effectgroottes).
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie interpreteert de kernbevindingen helder en koppelt ze aan eerdere literatuur en aan plausibele mechanismen (habituatie/inhibitory learning). Er wordt kritische reflectie gegeven op beperkingen (o.a. individualisatie van exposure en zelfrapportage) en er volgen toekomstgerichte suggesties. De beperkingstoetsing en onderbouwing van de belangrijkste alternatieve verklaringen kan nog sterker/meer specifiek zijn, en sommige beweringen zijn wat stelliger dan wat de gerapporteerde resultaten volledig onderbouwen (bijv. iVET versus wachtlijst zonder effectgroottes/sterkte van bewijs).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst gebruikt grotendeels wetenschappelijke, academische formuleringen en is goed te volgen door duidelijke paragrafen en logische overgangen. Er zijn echter meerdere taalkundige/typografische en APA-/referentiegerelateerde problemen (o.a. inconsistenties/typenaamvarianten in citaties en meetmomenten/tabelkoppen) en enkele omschrijvingen zijn minder exact, wat tot ambiguïteit kan leiden. De stijl is verder niet consistent op bepaalde plaatsen (bijv. kleine formulering- of afbreukmomenten in kritieke termen), wat de leesbaarheid net voldoende houdt voor de lagere band.

intro_rubric_feedback: De inleiding biedt een duidelijke lijn van redeneren met een passende koppeling van SAS-mechanismen naar exposure en vervolgens naar VRET vs. iVET en de relevantie voor adolescenten. De onderzoeksvraag en hypotheses worden expliciet en grotendeels logisch afgeleid uit de literatuur. Wel is er ruimte om de relevantie/argumentatie rond het verschil iVET vs. VRET nog scherper te specificeren (welke mechanismen precies, en waarom dat tot het specifieke patroon van resultaten leidt), en een aantal bronnen lijken minder direct noodzakelijk of mist iets aan up-to-date focus in details.
methods_rubric_feedback: De methodes zijn grotendeels coherent: ontwerp, populatie, inclusie/exclusie, interventie-opbouw, meetinstrument en procedure worden in begrijpelijke mate beschreven. Enkele essentiële details ontbreken echter of zijn onvoldoende gespecificeerd (bijv. details over randomisatieprocedure/allocatie, blindering/uitvoerders, therapietrouw/controle, en hoe/door wie de psychometrische cut-off voor SCID-5 Junior praktisch is ingezet). Daardoor zijn de methodes niet overal op het niveau van “complete en correct”, al is het merendeel wel correct en bruikbaar.
results_rubric_feedback: De analyse is qua opzet redelijk onderbouwd (assumptiechecks, factorial mixed ANOVA met interactie), maar de post-hoc conclusies zijn methodologisch onduidelijk/inconsistent uitgewerkt: de gerapporteerde Holm-gecorrigeerde p-waarden lijken niet logisch te passen bij het vervolg (en er staan ook inhoudelijke fouten/typos waardoor interpretatie wordt bemoeilijkt). Bovendien ontbreekt effectgrootte-informatie en nauwkeurige verslaglegging (bijv. df-interpretatie en exacte manier van post-hoc onder de interactie), waardoor de “in-depth statistical reasoning” niet overtuigend wordt aangetoond.
discussion_rubric_feedback: De discussie herneemt het doel en sluit inhoudelijk aan op de belangrijkste uitkomsten (alleen VRET significant, iVET niet t.o.v. wachtlijst) en probeert dit te verklaren via relevante exposuremechanismen. De kritiek op het onderzoek (individualisering van scenario’s, zelfrapportage) en toekomstig onderzoek zijn relevant en overwegend verantwoord, met een logische terugkoppeling naar toegankelijkheid en klinische implicaties. Toch blijft de koppeling tussen de exacte statistische bevindingen en de voorgestelde verklaring soms beperkt (o.a. zonder verdere toetsing/alternatieve verklaringen zoals power, attritie, therapietrouw of baseline-variantie), waardoor het niveau “compleet/coherent” niet overal gehaald wordt.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: De tekst gebruikt overwegend wetenschappelijke taal en is meestal duidelijk gestructureerd met logische secties en paragrafen. Er zijn echter meerdere taalkundige/APA-gerelateerde inconsistenties en opvallende formuleringen/typo’s (o.a. inconsistent gebruik van termen, enkele fouten in verwijzingen/typenamen in de tekst, en slordige kopjes/figuur- en tabelpresentatie). Daarnaast bevat het stuk enkele vage of potentieel verwarrende claims in cruciale interpretatiestappen (met name in resultaten/interpretatie), waardoor de stijl net in de adequaat-band blijft in plaats van goed.

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction provides a generally correct line of reasoning from social anxiety and treatment barriers to inhibitory learning and then to VR exposure and variability/safety signals, culminating in a relevant RQ/expectation. The argumentation is mostly coherent and references are used in a responsible, academically appropriate way. Still, the paper does not explicitly state a formal research question/hypotheses in a single unambiguous, testable form, leaving some parts slightly diffuse.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods describe the sample recruitment, inclusion/exclusion criteria, measures, and a broadly coherent 3-condition design with pre/post assessments and an 8-week exposure schedule. However, key procedural details are missing or unclear (e.g., randomization process details, therapist characteristics/standardization, adherence/engagement, VR session content/variability schedule), and the accounting of participants across sections is inconsistent. Because of these gaps and inconsistencies, the design cannot be fully evaluated as complete and correct.
results_rubric_feedback: While the Results claim use of a Factorial Mixed ANOVA with assumption checks, the statistical reporting is not sufficiently complete or consistent (e.g., unclear degrees of freedom, missing effect sizes/interaction details, and post-hoc thresholds without the required supporting numbers/tables). The text also conflicts with the described sample size and dropout statements, and mentions figures/tables that are not actually provided with the needed numeric values. As a result, the analysis cannot be verified and does not meet the rubric standard for correct, complete, coherent reporting.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion offers a conceptually plausible interpretation consistent with inhibitory learning (variability/safety signals) and comparative claims against prior VR studies. However, the main conclusions are not tightly justified by the reported statistical outcomes (which are themselves under-specified/inconsistent), and the unexpected waitlist increase is discussed with speculative explanations without adequate linkage to what was actually measured or controlled. Critical reflection is present, but it does not convincingly address major threats to validity that follow from the reported reporting/design issues.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally understandable and written in an academic tone, with mostly clear paragraphs and a logical flow. APA referencing is mostly present, but there are noticeable formatting and citation issues (e.g., a problematic citation entry formatting in the Discussion reference list, and missing/unclear table/figure content). Some crucial statements are vague or internally inconsistent, which reduces clarity in parts where precision is essential.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a plausible topic and a generally coherent rationale linking inhibitory learning, variability, and VR exposure, leading to clear directional expectations. However, the Methods and especially the Results are substantially under-specified or internally inconsistent (e.g., participant numbers/discrepancies, unclear dropouts, incomplete reporting, and statistical details that do not add up), which undermines the scientific credibility of the findings. The Discussion provides a reasonable thematic interpretation but lacks careful alignment with the actual reported results and does not critically address major methodological/statistical reporting issues. Language and structure are mostly readable, yet the presence of unclear/incorrect elements in crucial parts limits the ability to evaluate the study scientifically.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction builds a mostly coherent line of reasoning from prevalence/treatment barriers to inhibitory learning mechanisms and then to VR exposure, leading to a clear, testable hypothesis contrasting self-led vs therapist-led VRET against waitlist. Several transitions are supported with citations and the rationale for the self-led vs therapist-led comparison is conceptually plausible. Still, parts of the argument are somewhat broad (e.g., economic claims) and the reference-to-claim mapping would benefit from sharper empirical grounding for the specific proposed mechanism differences.
methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is broadly coherent (screening, random assignment, pre/post, three conditions), and key elements of participants, measures, and session structure are described. However, essential methodological details are missing or unclear for scientific replication (e.g., how variability/safety signals are operationalized across conditions; therapist role fidelity; session timing and adherence; handling of outliers; exact randomization/assignment and inclusion of the waitlist-after-8-weeks). Additionally, there are internal inconsistencies in the reported N and dropout reporting that should be resolved for the methods to be considered complete and correct.
results_rubric_feedback: The intended analysis approach (mixed factorial ANOVA with main effects and interaction, plus post-hoc comparisons) is stated and the general pattern of effects is described. Yet the reporting is not sufficiently complete or verifiable: the factorial ANOVA degrees of freedom appear implausible given the stated sample sizes, at least one outcome table/figure is missing, and the results narrative conflicts with the earlier claim about dropouts/no dropouts. Effect sizes, confidence intervals, and clear presentation of descriptive stats needed to interpret magnitude are absent, limiting demonstration of statistical reasoning.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion articulates a main conclusion consistent with the stated expectations (both VRET conditions outperform waitlist; therapist-led stronger than self-led) and connects it back to inhibitory learning concepts. Critical reflections are relevant (waitlist deterioration, single-therapist concern, limited treatment components), and future directions are proposed. Still, the strength of the conclusions is not well justified given the uncertainties/inconsistencies in the results reporting, and the causal mechanism claims (variability/safety signals) are not sufficiently tied to measurable study manipulations.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Overall scientific language is mostly understandable, with generally structured paragraphs and appropriate terminology. However, there are noticeable APA/style and presentation problems (e.g., missing Table contents/figure support, reference formatting inconsistencies, and apparent citation/bibliographic mismatches). Several statements are vague or overconfident relative to what is reported, and the clarity of the reporting suffers from missing or inconsistent numeric details.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows a generally understandable conceptual rationale linking inhibitory learning, variability, and safety signals to VRET, and it states clear directional expectations for the three conditions. However, the Methods and Results lack critical information and contain inconsistencies (e.g., sample size/dropouts vs. reported N; questionable or missing reporting of key descriptive/statistical outputs), which undermines scientific transparency and credibility. The Discussion provides relevant considerations (waitlist change, therapist effects, CBT vs exposure-only context) but the overall inferential claims are not tightly anchored to the reported analysis quality. Across the paper, multiple presentation/APA issues and vague missing elements (e.g., absent Table/Figure content) prevent the study from meeting an “adequate” scientific reporting standard.

intro_rubric_feedback: The line of reasoning leading to the RQ/hypotheses is largely present and mostly coherent: it links SAD treatment barriers to VRET, then to inhibitory learning, and derives an expectation about therapist-led superiority. However, the RQ is not explicitly stated as a formal, unambiguous research question/hypothesis statement, and some claims are strengthened by citations without sufficiently specifying how they translate into testable predictions. References are generally used responsibly, but a few parts rely on broad or loosely justified assertions (e.g., inhibitor learning/safety signals mapping to “being aware at home” vs “being with a therapist”) that would benefit from tighter conceptual operationalization.
methods_rubric_feedback: The method description includes key elements (design, participants, measures, sessions, random assignment, and timing), and the VRET conditions are described with some specificity. Nonetheless, important inconsistencies undermine coherence: the dropout reporting conflicts with the later claim of no dropouts and the final sample size used in analyses is unclear/inconsistent. The procedure timing for the waitlist crossover is also described ambiguously, and crucial methodological details needed for reproducibility are missing (e.g., therapist characteristics/session adherence checks, assignment concealment, randomization at the participant level, details of the VR exposure content schedule, and handling of the stated “outlier”).
results_rubric_feedback: Results reporting is incomplete and sometimes internally implausible for the stated design: the paper references Table 1, Table 2, and Figure 1, but these are not provided in a way that fully supports the claims. The factorial mixed ANOVA degrees of freedom are presented in a way that does not match typical expectations given the sample size, and effect size/parameter reporting is absent, limiting scientific interpretability. Post-hoc comparisons are reported only with p-values, without indicating which tests were used, correction for multiple comparisons, or providing means/SDs aligned with those tests.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion provides a coherent thematic summary that matches the stated hypothesis direction (both VRET conditions outperform waitlist; therapist-led outperform self-led) and connects back to inhibitory learning. However, the main conclusions are not sufficiently anchored in the presented/verified statistical results because the results section is underreported and internally questionable, and there is no demonstration of effect magnitudes or confidence inferences. The discussion of limitations is reasonable at a conceptual level (sample size, therapist effects, lack of CBT components), but it also includes speculative explanations (especially for the waitlist worsening) that are not supported by measured covariates or additional analyses.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally understandable and uses mostly appropriate scientific terminology, with a readable overall structure across sections. That said, there are frequent clarity and precision issues in crucial areas (e.g., ambiguous “inspired by” phrasing, inconsistent reporting of sample/dropouts, and unclear statistical/method details), and there is at least one obvious referencing/layout issue (e.g., a malformed Wolitzky-Taylor citation within the Discussion and incomplete content around tables/figures). Grammar and style are mostly serviceable, but the scientific communication lacks the precision expected for APA-style, reproducible reporting.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a reasonable conceptual motivation for comparing self-led versus therapist-led VRET and links these to inhibitory learning, which supports the Introduction somewhat. However, there are major problems with scientific reasoning and the methodological/reporting coherence: the participant accounting is inconsistent (dropouts vs final N), key figures/tables are missing or not properly populated, and the statistical reporting is internally questionable (e.g., extreme degrees of freedom). The Discussion is broadly aligned with the stated hypotheses but is not grounded in adequate, verifiable results reporting, and it makes causal claims beyond what the analysis as reported can support. These issues place the work mainly in the lower rubric bands despite some strengths in narrative structure.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a relevant topic and a reasonably clear comparative rationale for self-led versus therapist-led VRET, with citations used mostly responsibly. The line of reasoning from inhibitory learning and “safety signals” to the specific expectation that therapist-led will outperform self-led is conceptually motivated but not articulated with enough precision for testable, mechanism-specific predictions. Additionally, some claims are broad or not tightly linked to the exact design (e.g., what variability and safety signals operationally mean in this study).
methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is broadly coherent with the stated aims (three conditions; 8 weekly sessions; LSAS-SR pre/post). Participants and materials are described at a moderate level, but key methodological details are missing or unclear (e.g., randomization procedure integrity, therapist/session standardization, adherence/engagement monitoring for self-led VRET, handling of outliers, and exact waitlist procedures). The outcomes are defined, yet the analytic plan and data handling choices are not sufficiently specified to evaluate correctness.
results_rubric_feedback: The results section states the use of a Factorial Mixed ANOVA and reports several significant effects, but the reporting is not statistically credible or consistent (e.g., degrees of freedom values that do not match the described sample/structure, and statements like “no dropouts among 75” while earlier dropout numbers suggest a different N). Post-hoc reporting lacks essential elements such as effect sizes, correction for multiple comparisons, and the exact pattern of changes across groups is not evidenced because tables/figures are incomplete/absent. Assumptions are claimed to be met, but the supporting information is missing, and the integrity/outlier handling is described without transparent criteria.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion draws a conclusion broadly consistent with the intended hypotheses (VRET improves symptoms; therapist-led may be stronger), and it attempts to link findings back to inhibitory learning. However, the central interpretive claims are not supported adequately given the results reporting problems and the lack of quantitative evidence (e.g., no effect sizes, no clear demonstration that the interaction drives the claimed direction/magnitude). The critical reflections are somewhat generic and sometimes speculative without tying directly to specific methodological or analytic weaknesses revealed by the study.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The writing is generally clear and uses scientific language appropriately, with most sections following a conventional structure. APA-style issues and layout problems appear (e.g., an incomplete/odd figure/table presentation and reference formatting inconsistencies), and there are also some ambiguous or overconfident formulations where evidence is not adequately shown. Overall, comprehension is possible, but credibility is weakened by formatting/reporting gaps that would be expected in a scientific paper.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a generally plausible study rationale and a mostly coherent design (two VRET formats plus waitlist; pre/post assessment). However, major issues in scientific reasoning/argumentation, especially in the introduction’s mechanism-to-hypothesis logic, and substantial problems in results reporting and statistical coherence strongly limit the scientific quality. Methods also lack key procedural details needed to judge replicability and validity, despite describing some core components. Language and style are generally understandable, but multiple reporting/formatting and referencing issues reduce credibility.

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction provides a generally coherent topic rationale and connects the RQ topic to inhibitory learning theory and to the practical problem of waitlists. However, the paper does not clearly specify a precise research question and hypothesis structure in the Introduction (e.g., what specific contrasts are tested and what statistical/operational meaning “better” has). References are largely used responsibly, but some claims are broad or loosely justified for the specific design, and the “safety signals” argument is not sharpened into a testable mechanism.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods include a basic design description (three arms, pre/post, weekly sessions) and name core instruments and session timing, but several required details are missing or unclear. Participant flow is internally inconsistent (dropouts vs final N), exclusion/screening procedures are not sufficiently operationalized (who conducted allocation, how randomization was implemented in practice), and therapist-led standardization/compliance/adherence measures are not described. The materials and procedure are described at a high level, but not with enough specificity to verify fidelity across conditions.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results section reports an ANOVA framework and lists significant main and interaction effects with p-values, but the reporting is not sufficiently complete to meet scientific reporting expectations (no effect sizes/intervals, and incomplete descriptive statistics usage). There are major numerical inconsistencies: degrees of freedom and the stated N do not align with the earlier participant counts, and the tables/figure are not actually provided with values. The post-hoc comparisons are also described only by p-values without specifying which correction (if any) was applied and without indicating the exact contrasts in a way that can be verified.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion attempts to map findings back onto inhibitory learning concepts (variability and safety signals) and compares the overall pattern to prior VRET studies, which is directionally appropriate. However, the critical reflection is weakened by speculative explanations for the waitlist increase without connecting them to measurable study factors (e.g., assessment effects, regression to the mean, life events measures, compliance) or to a clearly justified alternative hypothesis. The main conclusion is also expressed with incorrect directional language (“negative effect”) and the paper does not convincingly reconcile the unexpected control outcome with the causal claims.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally understandable and mostly scientifically worded, with a clear macro-structure by sections. Still, there are recurring issues with precision and clarity in crucial places (e.g., incorrect wording of the main conclusion direction, ambiguity around key procedures and outcomes), and APA/formatting is inconsistent (e.g., references and headings, missing table/figure content). Grammar and style are mostly serviceable, but accuracy and terminological consistency need improvement to support scientific credibility.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows some relevant conceptual linkage between the inhibitory learning model, variability, and a comparison of self-led vs therapist-led VRET, but the scientific argument remains partly overgeneralized and the study rationale for the specific comparisons is not translated into precise, testable expectations beyond a broad directional claim. Methodological reporting is incomplete in key places (e.g., missing details on randomization process/stratification, therapist role standardization, adherence/compliance, and attrition inconsistency), and there are major inconsistencies in the reported sample size and ANOVA degrees of freedom that undermine the credibility of the analyses. The Results section reports statistical tests and p-values, but without effect sizes, confidence intervals, or coherence between the stated N and the reported F-tests; required tables/figure values are also absent. The Discussion offers plausible qualitative explanations, yet the main conclusion (“VRET has a negative effect”) is phrased incorrectly and the handling of the unexpected waitlist increase is more speculative than critically grounded in the reported data and design.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a clear conceptual line from social anxiety and access barriers to inhibitory learning, variability, and the rationale for comparing self-led vs therapist-led VRET. The research gap and the study’s expectation are articulated, and the majority of cited sources are relevant and generally up to date. However, the precision of the RQ/operational comparison (what exactly differs between conditions and how that maps onto “safety signals”) could be tightened, and some claims (e.g., effect wording like “negative effect”) are not consistently phrased scientifically.
methods_rubric_feedback: The design, sample, measures, and overall procedure are described in a mostly coherent way and allow a reader to follow the study’s flow. Still, important methodological details are missing or insufficiently specific (e.g., randomization procedure details, therapist qualifications/intervention fidelity, exact timing of waitlist exposure, adherence/engagement measures, and how sessions 2–7 were standardized within/between conditions). Some inconsistencies in the reporting of sample counts across sections also reduce clarity.
results_rubric_feedback: Statistical reporting contains major incoherences (e.g., degrees of freedom values such as F(2, 10484.21), and inconsistent treatment of dropouts—stated as none, yet earlier a drop-out count is given). Post-hoc results are reported only with p-values (no effect sizes, no correction for multiple comparisons), and the tables/figures are not actually provided with the corresponding numerical values. The analysis described is not sufficiently verifiable, so correctness cannot be credited.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion links findings back to inhibitory learning (variability and safety signals) and to prior VRET literature, and it proposes plausible limitations and future directions. However, the reasoning and interpretation are not fully consistent with the reported results language (and the paper’s earlier statistical issues make the certainty of conclusions questionable). Some critical points (e.g., waitlist worsening) are discussed, but explanations remain speculative without integrating concrete study details (e.g., adherence, testing effects, or regression to the mean) and without robust statistical reflection.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific terminology is generally used appropriately and the sections are logically organized. There are several problems with clarity and precision in wording (e.g., overly broad or poorly defined claims such as “VRET has a negative effect,” and some ambiguous phrasing). APA/reference presentation is mostly acceptable, but there are noticeable inconsistencies and at least one reference-entry formatting problem in the discussion.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a clear conceptual line from SAD to inhibitory learning and then to why VRET (including variability and safety signals) may matter for self-led vs therapist-led formats. However, some claims are asserted somewhat broadly without tight justification (e.g., economic “astronomical” framing), and a few details about the exact comparison rationale remain a bit underspecified.
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods include the key design elements (three groups, pre/post, random allocation, screening and exclusion criteria, and a basic session structure). However, several essential methodological details are missing or insufficiently specified for a reader to evaluate validity and replication (e.g., exact randomization/sequence handling, therapist characteristics, fidelity/standardization, adherence/engagement monitoring, and the exact post-waitlist timing/procedure). Some inconsistencies in the reported sample counts and inferential reporting also raise concerns about internal consistency.
results_rubric_feedback: The results generally match the stated factorial mixed ANOVA approach (main effects and an interaction), and you report key F tests and p-values with post-hoc directions. Yet, critical reporting is incomplete or unclear (no effect sizes/estimated means in-text tied to the figure/tables as written, no degrees of freedom consistency checks, and assumptions are referenced without actual presentation). The narrative also contains logical overgeneralizations (e.g., describing “no dropouts” while earlier data suggest a different attrition flow).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion connects the findings to the inhibitory learning framework and offers plausible explanations for the unexpected waitlist pattern, including limitations such as sample size and therapist effects. It also proposes future directions (larger samples, more therapists, broadened CBT components). However, the critique sometimes relies on speculative causal language without anchoring it in more rigorous study-specific evidence, and the concluding summaries are phrased in overly categorical terms (e.g., “negative effect” without guarding against alternative interpretations).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Overall structure is readable and the scientific language is mostly appropriate, with paragraphs focused on single ideas. There are some unclear or awkward phrasings (e.g., “negative effect” wording, phrasing around results/outlier handling), and several APA/reference/layout problems (notably the corrupted/incorrect reference formatting in the reference list and missing or unclear table/figure content). Grammar/spelling errors appear more than minimally, and some crucial statements are somewhat vague.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a reasonably coherent line of reasoning from social anxiety and treatment barriers to inhibitory learning, variability, VRET, and the specific comparison between self-led and therapist-led formats. methods_rubric_feedback: The method describes the overall design (random assignment to three conditions, pre/post assessment, and an 8-week intervention) and gives basic details about sampling, materials, and session structure. However, key methodological details are missing or unclear/incorrectly specified: the planned allocation numbers vs. final N, the waitlist timeline and whether waitlist participants receive any active treatment immediately after post, the reported ANOVA degrees of freedom/sample size inconsistencies, dropouts accounting, and the intervention “evaluation of thoughts” procedures for both conditions. The description is therefore not fully complete and contains enough ambiguities to make replication difficult.
results_rubric_feedback: The results report that a mixed factorial ANOVA was run and provide F-tests and p-values for main and interaction effects, plus a post-hoc pattern consistent with the stated hypotheses. However, the reporting is incomplete and internally inconsistent: there are no actual descriptive statistics (Table 1/2 are missing), the degrees of freedom values appear implausible given the stated sample sizes, effect sizes and confidence intervals are not reported, and assumptions are claimed without substantive reporting. The statistical reasoning is therefore only partially verifiable and not presented at a level consistent with the rubric’s higher bands.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion connects the direction of findings to inhibitory learning concepts (variability and safety signals) and compares broadly to prior VRET studies. It also offers critical reflection on limitations and future directions (sample size, single therapist, and absence of CBT components), which supports the study’s interpretive scope. Still, the conclusion wording is overly global (e.g., “negative effect” without clarifying what is compared and by how much), and the limitation arguments could be tied more directly to specific methodological features and potential alternative explanations (e.g., regression to the mean, expectancy/assessment effects).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable and the structure largely follows introduction–methods–results–discussion. APA and scholarly conventions are mostly followed, but there are notable issues: incomplete/incorrect APA formatting in places, at least one obvious citation-format problem in the reference list, and some ambiguous or vague phrasing in crucial interpretive parts. intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a clear overall line of reasoning from prevalence/treatment barriers to inhibitory learning, then to VR/VRET and the key gap (self-led vs therapist-led). However, the argumentation could be tightened by more precisely stating the research question and ensuring the literature review clearly justifies each comparison and mechanism claim without broad or somewhat overextended generalizations.

intro_rubric_feedback: The line of reasoning toward a clear RQ/hypotheses is mostly present: the paper motivates exposure, then contrasts SVRET vs TVRET and motivates waitlist comparison. methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is coherent and generally aligned with the hypotheses, with participants, conditions, and a plausible 10-week procedure described. However, key method details are incomplete or imprecise (e.g., randomization description is thin, comorbidity/exclusion is vaguely defined, details of adherence, therapist qualifications, intervention fidelity, and precise scoring/handling of LSAS-derived regression are missing). results_rubric_feedback: The results section provides the core mixed ANOVA structure and reports main and interaction effects, followed by post-hoc comparisons. lang_style_rubric_feedback: Overall scientific language is understandable and the structure largely follows conventional IMRaD flow with clear paragraphing around themes. final_grade_justification: The paper shows an overall coherent scientific aim and generally appropriate connections between background literature, the study rationale, and testable hypotheses. The Introduction and Discussion contain mostly plausible theoretical interpretation, but the argumentation is weakened by some unclear/unsupported claims (e.g., causal links in the rationale) and an overconfident explanation that aligns results with theory without direct manipulation/measurement of the proposed mechanisms. The Methods and Results are largely understandable and match the stated design (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist; pre–post LSAS with factorial mixed ANOVA), but there are notable statistical/reporting issues (e.g., degrees of freedom and effect phrasing inconsistencies, missing/insufficient details about data handling, and incomplete reporting of tables/figures). intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction contains a plausible line of reasoning connecting SAD, exposure principles, and the potential role of self-led versus therapist-led formats. methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is broadly coherent with the stated aim (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist) and major elements like participant criteria, measures, and session structure are described. lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally understandable and the paper is organized into standard scientific sections with mostly readable sentences. final_grade_justification: The paper shows a generally coherent study aim and a relevant rationale in the Introduction, but several claims and comparisons are not tightly argued to the specific research question and hypotheses. The Methods and Results contain multiple critical inconsistencies and incompletions (e.g., factorial ANOVA df/reporting uses incorrect/unclear degrees of freedom; statistical model details, effect sizes, and key reliability/validity information are missing), which weakens scientific rigor. The Discussion provides some theory-based interpretation and limitations, yet it does not fully address alternative explanations, measurement scope (e.g., missing fear of negative evaluation), or whether the statistics support all claims. intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction contains a recognizable line of reasoning toward an RQ/hypotheses and provides relevant background on exposure, VRET, and accessibility. However, the argument sometimes becomes descriptive rather than tightly deductive, and the proposed theory-to-hypothesis mapping (self-efficacy vs. interaction theory) would benefit from clearer, testable links. References are generally used responsibly, but some claims about prior studies and mechanisms are stated without enough specificity, which makes the evidential strength feel uneven.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods are mostly coherent and related to the stated hypotheses, with participants, materials, and a 10-week protocol described. Still, important details are missing or unclear for replication and proper scientific reasoning (e.g., how random allocation was done, what “any comorbidities” entails, adherence/engagement monitoring, therapist procedures and standardization, and handling of outliers/assumptions for the analysis). Measurement use is described, but the description of the analysis plan is not fully aligned with what is reported in Results (e.g., mention of multiple regression vs. mixed ANOVA).
results_rubric_feedback: Results reporting is generally understandable: the main effects, interaction, and post-hoc pattern align with the study’s pre–post, between-condition design. That said, there are inconsistencies/omissions that reduce confidence in the statistical reporting (e.g., unclear/incorrect ANOVA degrees of freedom and condition coding, and the narrative sometimes treats within-condition and between-condition comparisons interchangeably). Tables/figures are referenced but not actually shown, and effect sizes/estimation (confidence intervals) are absent, which limits the “in-depth statistical reasoning” component.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion provides a mostly correct overall conclusion that exposure-based virtual therapy reduced LSAS scores compared to waitlist, and it acknowledges multiple limitations (age range, lack of follow-up, and lack of manipulation checks). The theoretical explanation (self-efficacy vs. interaction theory) is presented but not convincingly tested, so causal claims remain stronger than the evidence. The paper would benefit from clearer differentiation between results support, theoretical speculation, and direct implications for future experimental design.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally comprehensible and the structure follows the expected scientific sections, with mostly clear topical focus. However, there are recurring grammar and phrasing issues (including multiple unpolished transitions and some awkward/incorrect terms) and occasional ambiguity in crucial descriptions. APA/reference formatting is mostly present, but there are noticeable inconsistencies (e.g., citation completeness and some punctuation/author-year discrepancies), which prevent it from reaching the highest language/style band.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows a generally coherent scientific aim and compares SVRET vs. TVRET vs. waitlist with an appropriate overall pre–post analytical approach. Introduction and Discussion largely build a plausible argument around exposure mechanisms and self-efficacy, but some theoretical claims are not tightly operationalized and several statements about prior work are vague or unsupported. Methods are mostly described at a usable level, yet key procedural details (e.g., randomization method, comorbidity handling, adherence/attrition, therapist/on-call standardization, and regression/assumption reporting coherence) are insufficient for full scientific transparency. Results reporting is partly inconsistent (e.g., ANOVA degrees of freedom vs. stated group coding) but the core findings and statistical logic are mostly understandable; Discussion appropriately uses limitations and suggests future work, but the causal interpretation is sometimes overstated relative to the design.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a recognizable line of reasoning from SAD relevance to exposure-based mechanisms and then to the practical motivation for comparing SVRET vs TVRET. methods_rubric_feedback: The method section is coherent and generally linked to the aim, with participants, key materials (VR headset/software, scenarios), measures, and a plausible procedure timeline. However, important design details are incomplete or unclear (e.g., the exact randomization process, what counts as “comorbidities,” whether assessors were blinded, how adherence/dose was monitored for SVRET, and what “on call” entails). results_rubric_feedback: The results use an analysis consistent with the stated mixed ANOVA framework and report main effects, interaction, and post-hoc comparisons with p-values. discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion draws main conclusions that are largely consistent with the results and includes relevant limitations (generalizability to older adults, absence of follow-up/waitlist-treatment duration issues, and the need for manipulation checks). lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally understandable and the structure follows standard IMRaD conventions, with many paragraphs having clear topics. There are noticeable grammar/word-choice issues and some APA/reference formatting problems (e.g., inconsistent capitalization, incomplete/duplicated descriptions, and minor citation inaccuracies) that detract from polish. Overall scientific style is adequate, but improved clarity, consistency, and adherence to APA formatting would better match the higher rubric bands.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows generally coherent scientific intent and a mostly appropriate rationale culminating in clear, testable hypotheses. Methods and results are described in a broadly usable way and the main statistical analysis is consistent with a pre–post, between-condition design, but there are notable gaps/ambiguities (e.g., randomization details, comorbidity/exclusion handling, and incomplete reporting of key descriptive/assumption details), limiting scientific clarity. The discussion largely follows from the results and provides critical reflection (age range, follow-up absence, and lack of manipulation checks), yet the theoretical interpretation is not tightly evidenced and some claims are phrased more strongly than the design supports. Overall, the study’s reasoning and reporting reach an Adequate-to-Good band, but weaknesses keep it from Excellent.

final_grade_justification: The paper shows an overall coherent scientific arc from rationale to study purpose, with a clear research question framed as comparing SVRET, TVRET, and waitlist, and hypotheses grounded in exposure theory and self-efficacy.
