lang_style_rubric_feedback: The writing is understandable overall and scientific terminology is used with some consistency (e.g., LSAS, SVRET/TVRET). However, language issues and APA/reference/layout problems are frequent enough to reduce clarity (grammar, phrasing, and a number of awkward/incorrect constructions), and there are several ambiguous or imprecise statements that affect readability in key parts. Structurally, headings are present, but sentence-level flow and accuracy are not reliably at the higher bands.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction presents a clear line of reasoning toward the research aim and hypotheses, and most claims are linked to prior theory (exposure, VRET, self-efficacy, interaction). Several references are relevant, but some are used in ways that are loosely connected to the specific SAD/VRET/SVRET-TVRET logic, and a few citation details appear inconsistent or potentially inaccurate. The overall framing is appropriate for justifying the research question, but the literature-to-RQ mapping is not fully tight throughout.
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods section includes the main design elements (n, diagnoses, group allocation, interventions, measures, pre/post, and basic exclusion criteria). However, important procedural details and operationalizations are missing or unclear (e.g., randomization procedure, how “comorbidities” and “psychotherapy” were assessed, specifics of the VR progression/therapist involvement in SVRET vs TVRET, and details of the regression/mixed-ANOVA setup). The measure scoring description is also too informal/incomplete for full reproducibility.
results_rubric_feedback: The results report an appropriate factorial mixed ANOVA framework with the key effects (main effects and time×condition interaction) and follow-up comparisons aligned to the hypotheses (pre vs post within conditions and post-test group differences). The interpretation is generally coherent, and you demonstrate basic statistical reasoning (assumptions, outliers, correction mention). Still, the reporting is incomplete (e.g., degrees of freedom appear questionable/inconsistent with N, no effect sizes/estimated marginal means, and some p-value formatting ambiguities).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion provides a coherent interpretation of the main findings (both exposure conditions improve over waitlist; SVRET outperforms TVRET) and links results to prior literature and the proposed theoretical accounts. Limitations and future directions are present and relevant, with thoughtful suggestions (age/technology, follow-up, manipulation checks, FNE vs LSAS). However, the theoretical evaluation is somewhat indirect (less evidence offered for why self-efficacy vs interaction is the driver), and not all references are used precisely to support specific claims.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is mostly understandable and generally consistent in terms (SVRET/TVRET, LSAS, exposure/VRET), and the structure by section is clear. There are, however, multiple grammar/wording issues and several clarity problems caused by awkward phrasing (e.g., unclear sentences, inconsistent terminology, and occasional incorrect or missing constructs). APA/style issues appear in the references and formatting (e.g., occasional reference-year/author mismatches and inconsistent capitalization/punctuation), keeping the writing in the developing-to-adequate range.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a generally coherent line of reasoning toward the research purpose and hypotheses, and the research relevance is mostly clear. However, several claims are not tightly linked to the stated RQ/hypotheses, and some citations appear only loosely connected or are not consistently used to support key argumentative steps. The reference coverage is largely relevant/credible but includes some potential mismatches/outdated or non-academic sources (e.g., web/blog-style items) and a few unclear in-text attributions/years that weaken “up-to-date and relevant” confidence.
methods_rubric_feedback: The method section describes the participants, overall design (SVRET vs TVRET vs waitlist; 10-week program), key materials (VR headset, scenarios), and the main outcome measure (LSAS), which supports coherence with the study aims. Nonetheless, important details are missing or imprecise (e.g., randomization procedure, allocation concealment, how comorbidities were determined/exclusion operationalized, what “multiple regression” refers to despite using ANOVA results, and whether the therapist was standardized/training and adherence/check-in procedures). Some terminology (e.g., “within-participant variable” but only two timepoints) and measurement scoring description would benefit from tighter accuracy.
results_rubric_feedback: The reported factorial mixed ANOVA and the overall pattern (time effect, condition effect, time×condition interaction; post-hoc comparisons) are mostly interpretable and aligned with the design. Still, the reporting has gaps: degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with N=99 (e.g., F(1, 99)), outlier handling (“all data was used”) is stated but not clarified, and the specific post-hoc tests/contrast structure and exact p-value formatting are unclear in places. The “in-depth knowledge” aspect is only partially demonstrated because effect sizes, correction approach details, and assumption checks are not fully expanded.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion links the findings to prior work and offers theoretical interpretations (Bandura/self-efficacy vs interaction/zpd), and it provides multiple limitations and future directions. However, the theoretical reasoning sometimes becomes speculative or internally inconsistent (e.g., claiming interaction theory is not supported while not testing it directly, and conflating related constructs such as motivation vs self-efficacy). The limitations cover several relevant issues, but they are not always convincingly tied to the design/results (e.g., fading effects and relapse claims without direct evidence), and the reference use is not uniformly precise (some citations are contextually asserted rather than clearly substantiated).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: The paper uses generally understandable scientific language and includes clearly separated sections with mostly focused paragraphs. There are, however, notable issues with grammar, word choice, and tense consistency (including awkward phrasing throughout) plus multiple APA/reference-layout inconsistencies (e.g., in-text citations and bibliographic accuracy/consistency). Several sentences are vague or imprecise in crucial places (especially around methodology/statistics), and there are formatting/typo-like problems that reduce clarity even when the overall meaning is sometimes recoverable.

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction presents a generally correct and coherent line of reasoning from SAD to exposure-based treatments and then to VRET, culminating in a reasonably clear RQ/hypothesis. Scientific relevance is strong, with responsible referencing overall, but some claims are broad (e.g., mechanisms and superiority assumptions) and the adolescent-specific rationale could be made more directly testable. Theoretical constructs (conditioning/operant reinforcement vs. cognitive avoidance/uncertainty) are introduced but not consistently integrated into one unifying hypothesis logic.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Method section is coherent and largely aligned with the stated RQ, with clear participant criteria, intervention description, measurement of SAD symptoms, and a plausible RCT procedure. Materials and procedures are described comprehensibly, including VR scenarios and exposure hierarchy logic. However, key methodological details are missing/unclear for scientific rigor (e.g., therapist training fidelity, session adherence/counterbalancing, how exposure was terminated “until anxiety decreased,” and outcome scoring/handling beyond cut-off), and some assumptions/choices in analysis are later justified in a way that would be better anticipated in the Methods.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results report a clear mixed ANOVA framework with time, condition effects, and an interaction, and includes pre/post descriptive comparisons and post-hoc testing. Some reporting is incomplete/rough (tables/figures are referenced but not fully verifiable from the text, and the exact correction/structure of post-hocs is unclear). There is also an interpretive/statistical reasoning gap: the Shapiro-Wilk violation is noted but then addressed via a Central Limit Theorem rationale without a clear justification that matches the specific ANOVA cell structure and distributional issue.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion provides a mostly correct mapping from findings to the main conclusion (both treatments improve; no superiority of VRET over IVET), and it offers relevant, theory-based explanations (e.g., cognitive avoidance, perceived realism/credibility). The limitations are reasonably argued and future research suggestions are appropriate and methodologically concrete (interactive VR, therapeutic alliance measurement, VRET+CBT comparison). However, the interpretation would benefit from more rigorous alignment with the reported interaction pattern and effect magnitudes, and from tempering causal claims given the presence of multiple explanations offered simultaneously.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally clear and scientific, and the paper is structured with readable paragraphs and topical focus. Grammar and APA formatting are mostly acceptable, but there are some inconsistencies (e.g., capitalization/abbreviations, references to “Figure/Table” without full context, and minor wording issues). Overall clarity supports understanding, though occasional ambiguous phrasing and redundancy in justification sections slightly reduce precision.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a mostly coherent scientific rationale and an appropriate RCT design addressing the stated research question. The Introduction builds a plausible argument for expecting VRET to outperform IVET in adolescents, but the link from theory to the specific adolescent mechanism and the study’s hypothesis is sometimes overstated or not tightly scoped. Methods and Results are largely complete and mostly correctly analyzed, yet there are notable reporting and statistical-interpretation issues (e.g., justification for the mixed ANOVA decisions despite assumption violations, and some inconsistencies/ambiguities in reporting post-hoc results). The Discussion provides relevant, theory-informed interpretations and practical future directions, but it does not fully grapple with the statistical pattern (e.g., differential change and significant interaction) to more rigorously justify the null superiority finding.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD prevalence and treatment gaps to why VRET might outperform IVET, culminating in a clear, relevant RQ/hypothesis. References are generally used responsibly and the theoretical conditioning/learning framing supports the exposure rationale. However, some theoretical links are broad (e.g., operant mechanisms and “cognitive avoidance” explanations) and the RQ/hypothesis could be stated more explicitly in a way that cleanly maps onto the exact outcome comparisons made later (and the comparator choice/justification for waitlist could be sharper).
methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is coherent and clearly related to the RQ, with adequate description of participants, diagnoses, exclusion criteria, random assignment, intervention dose, and general session structure. The operationalization of VRET/IVET content and the use of LSAS-CA are described comprehensibly, and the procedure is mostly complete for replication. Some key methodological details are missing or could be tightened (e.g., therapist training/competence checks, fidelity/supervision, blinding of assessors, handling of assumption violations beyond noting CLT, and more explicit analytic approach details such as which post-hoc contrasts were used).
results_rubric_feedback: The analyses are broadly appropriate for the design (mixed ANOVA with time and condition, followed by Holm-corrected post-hocs) and the main pattern—improvement over time and condition-specific change—is reported coherently. However, statistical reporting is incomplete: effect sizes, degrees of freedom/consistency checks for the mixed model, and the exact structure of post-hoc comparisons beyond selected p-values are not fully transparent. The normality violation is noted but the justification and sensitivity/robustness handling are not convincingly documented, which limits confidence despite the reasonable analytical choice.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly summarizes the key findings and offers a relevant, critical interpretation focused on why VRET may not be superior to IVET. Limitations (realism constraints in semi-structured dialogue, therapeutic alliance differences) are plausible and connected to intervention delivery. Explanations for the null superiority effect are somewhat speculative and not always tightly tied to measurable constructs from the current study; the discussion would benefit from more direct linkage to what was actually tested/measured and from clearer handling of alternative explanations (e.g., equivalence rather than only superiority).
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is mostly precise and understandable, with clear sectioning and generally coherent paragraph organization. APA-like referencing is mostly correct and the writing is largely free of grammar/spelling errors. There are minor issues with ambiguity/overgeneral statements in theoretical claims and a few imprecisions in reporting structure (e.g., tables/figures referenced but not fully supported in text), but overall style remains academic and readable.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a coherent scientific aim and a largely well-developed rationale from theory to RQ/hypotheses, and it tests those expectations with a randomized controlled design. Methods and results are mostly complete and largely consistent with the stated design, including appropriate use of a mixed ANOVA and post-hoc comparisons. The main weaknesses are in statistical reporting (limited assumption-handling detail, incomplete reporting of the post-hoc/figure/table evidence) and in the discussion’s handling of unexpected findings, where explanations are plausible but sometimes speculative and not tightly anchored to the study’s observed pattern. Language and structure are clear and largely professional, with minor issues in clarity and reporting precision.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD relevance to exposure-based rationales, culminating in a clear aim, RQ/expectations (VRET vs IVET vs waitlist), and hypotheses. The scientific relevance is supported with multiple citations and the argument is generally convincing, though some theoretical links (e.g., specific conditioning mechanisms and how they uniquely favor VRET) are asserted more strongly than they are operationally tested. References are largely used responsibly, but there are places where claims would benefit from tighter qualification or more direct linkage to the proposed comparison in adolescents.
methods_rubric_feedback: The study design is coherent and generally well matched to the hypotheses, with clear participant inclusion/exclusion criteria, assignment to three conditions, an appropriate diagnostic measure, and an exposure-based procedure described in enough detail to understand what was done. The materials section specifies VR scenarios and tailoring variables, and IVET contexts are described. Still, the methods leave interpretation gaps (e.g., no clear description of therapist/experimenter treatment fidelity or session-by-session adherence, limited specificity about waitlist handling/assessments, and insufficient transparency about attrition/compliance and how non-normality was treated in analysis).
results_rubric_feedback: The results are mostly coherent with the stated design (pre/post by condition) and use a reasonable mixed ANOVA framework with post-hoc testing and multiple-comparison control. Reporting includes the key omnibus effects and the pattern of VRET/IVET vs waitlist at post-test, and the discussion of what supports versus contradicts hypotheses is appropriate. However, the statistical reasoning is not fully rigorous (e.g., justification for proceeding under violated normality using the CLT is weak and assumes conditions that may not be met; effect sizes and confidence intervals are missing; and the treatment of outliers is mentioned without transparent influence diagnostics or robustness checks).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly summarizes the core findings (both active treatments improved vs waitlist; VRET not superior to IVET) and provides limitations that are relevant to internal validity and realism/credibility of exposure. The explanations offered for why VRET may not outperform IVET are theoretically plausible but remain somewhat speculative because the study does not directly measure the proposed mechanisms (e.g., cognitive avoidance, fear of uncertainty, or therapeutic alliance). Future research directions are sensible and linked to the stated limitations, though stronger anchoring to what was measured (and not measured) would make the argumentative chain more convincing.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is generally scientific and understandable, and the overall structure follows IMRaD with clear paragraph focus. There are minor issues such as occasional awkward phrasing, a few imprecise statements (e.g., assumption handling and some theoretical wording), and table/figure formatting irregularities (e.g., tabular content presented in-band and inconsistent formatting). APA style appears mostly consistent in citations, but presentation/layout details (tables/figures and some typographic inconsistencies) reduce polish. Overall, clarity is adequate-to-good, with some room for more precise and concise scientific expression.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows generally coherent scientific reasoning from the introduction to the testable RQ/hypotheses, and the method is largely aligned with the stated aim (VRET vs IVET vs waitlist) with relevant measures and a plausible procedure. Results reporting includes appropriate overall modeling (mixed ANOVA) and post-hoc comparisons with correction, and the main pattern supports the authors’ core claims. However, several reasoning and statistical decisions are not tightly justified (e.g., handling of violated normality, treatment of waitlist changes, and some inconsistencies/precision in how assumptions and planned analyses are defended), and key details that affect interpretation (e.g., effect sizes, handling of attrition, and more complete reporting) are missing or unclear. The discussion provides relevant limitations and future directions and broadly maps conclusions to findings, but some causal explanations remain speculative rather than strongly anchored in the study’s design and data.

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction provides a broadly coherent line of reasoning from SAD prevalence and treatment gaps to the choice of comparing VRET and IVET, culminating in a clear RQ/hypothesis structure. References are generally used responsibly, but the theoretical mechanisms (classical/operant conditioning, overgeneralization, operant reinforcement, cognitive avoidance) are sometimes layered without a fully tight link to the specific predicted VRET advantage. The stated novelty/research gap is present, yet the RQ could be framed more precisely to foreground the exact primary comparison and expected effect pattern.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods section describes participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, random assignment, interventions (key components, scenarios, session structure), and the dependent measure in a mostly complete way. The study design is clearly an RCT with appropriate baseline and post-treatment measurement, and procedures are described in understandable terms. However, several analytical/operational details are missing or too vague for full reproducibility (e.g., therapist treatment fidelity/monitoring, exact randomization/checks, handling of the assumption violation at analysis, and specifics of exposure delivery standardization across conditions).
results_rubric_feedback: The Results provide the main ANOVA outputs (main effects and interaction) and a reasonable summary of post-hoc comparisons relative to waitlist and between VRET/IVET. Reporting is sufficiently coherent and relevant, and the inclusion of outliers is justified only minimally (by scale fit) without further sensitivity checks. Several statistical reporting elements are missing for an excellent standard (notably effect sizes, degrees-of-freedom consistency scrutiny, confidence intervals, and clearer reporting of post-hoc correction application and decision logic).
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion returns to the hypotheses, summarizes the pattern of findings, and offers plausible limitations and future directions, including realism of VR scenarios and therapeutic alliance. The critical reflection is relevant, but the causal/mechanistic explanations (e.g., cognitive avoidance and CBT pairing) are speculative relative to the evidence presented and are not tested within the current design. It would be stronger if the interpretation more directly addressed the null VRET–IVET difference using inferential/statistical reasoning (magnitude, uncertainty) rather than primarily theoretical plausibility.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is largely clear and scientifically appropriate, and the paper is structured into conventional sections. There are some ambiguous or imprecise phrases in crucial areas (especially around assumptions and statistical handling), and there is occasional redundancy. APA-style formatting is mostly consistent, though tables/figures are not fully rendered in-text and some presentation choices (e.g., wording around randomization and assumptions) reduce clarity.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a generally coherent study question (VRET vs. IVET for adolescent SAD) and a method that is mostly complete and aligned with the design (RCT with pre/post LSAS-CA and waitlist). Scientific reasoning and argumentation are strongest in the Introduction and are generally supported by the cited literature, but the theoretical account and predictions are somewhat overspecified and not fully integrated with the statistical results. The Results section reports key analyses and effects clearly, yet includes questionable handling/justification of assumptions (e.g., CLT invocation despite a violated normality test) and lacks effect sizes and detailed post-hoc decision reporting/assumptions. The Discussion provides a mostly plausible interpretation and references limitations, but the reasoning connecting non-differences between conditions to specific mechanisms is not fully substantiated and does not engage deeply with inferential/statistical implications (e.g., magnitude, power, alternative explanations).

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction presents a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD and learning theories to exposure mechanisms and a clear comparison between VRET and IVET for adolescents, culminating in a relevant aim and hypotheses. References are generally used responsibly to support key claims, and prior work is summarized in a way that motivates the study. Some conceptual links (e.g., specific mechanisms like cognitive avoidance and why VRET would fail to outperform IVET) are not yet fully tightened to the exact empirical gap stated, which limits the precision of the hypothesis-building.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods describe participants, eligibility, random assignment, the dependent measure (LSAS-CA), and the overall treatment structure in sufficient detail for understanding the study. The design is coherent with the stated RQ/hypotheses, and VRET/IVET procedures and materials are specified at a reasonable level. However, details that would improve scientific replicability and analytic validity are incomplete or ambiguous (e.g., treatment fidelity/supervision specifics, handling of outliers, and more precise description of the waitlist assessment experience).
results_rubric_feedback: Results are largely coherent and appropriately matched to the design, including reporting of main effects, the time-by-condition interaction, and post-hoc comparisons with an adjustment (Holm). The reporting is generally complete in terms of the inferential conclusions needed to evaluate the hypotheses. The statistical reasoning has weaknesses: the decision to proceed with ANOVA despite violated normality is not well justified for the particular case, and the presentation contains formatting issues (tables/figures embedded in text) that reduce clarity.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion correctly synthesizes the main findings: both treatments outperform waitlist, and VRET is not superior to IVET at post-test. Critical reflection includes plausible limitations (e.g., VRET scenario interactivity constraints, therapeutic alliance differences) and future directions (measuring alliance, improving interactivity, testing VRET+CBT). However, some explanatory reasoning for the null VRET-vs-IVET difference is speculative and not strongly tethered to what the study actually measured or tested, which weakens the strength of the causal argument.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is mostly clear and scientific, with generally structured paragraphs and readable transitions from rationale to methods to interpretation. Grammar and APA-style elements are acceptable overall, but there are noticeable formatting/structural disruptions in the Results (e.g., table/figure presentation) and occasional wordiness or imprecise phrasing that affects precision in key scientific statements. Redundancy is present but not excessive, and the manuscript is understandable overall despite those presentation issues.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows generally coherent scientific reasoning tying SAD, exposure-based mechanisms, and the rationale for comparing VRET vs. IVET, and the RQ/hypotheses are reasonably clear and relevant. The Methods section is mostly complete and the design matches the stated aim (randomized trial with diagnosed adolescents, LSAS-CA pre/post, and 10-session exposure), though several procedural details needed for full reproducibility are missing or imprecise. Results reporting is largely coherent (mixed ANOVA, assumptions checked, interaction interpreted, and post-hoc comparisons reported), but there are conceptual/statistical issues (e.g., questionable handling of the violated normality assumption) and some reporting/formatting problems. The Discussion mostly provides an appropriate interpretation and limitation/future-work logic consistent with the findings, but the argumentation about why VRET is not superior is somewhat speculative and not strongly anchored to the reported analyses. Overall, the work aligns best with the “Adequate–Good” bands for several sections, but not consistently the “Good” or “Excellent” level due to methodological/statistical reasoning gaps and less rigorous argument strength in the Discussion.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a coherent theoretical framework and a clear line of reasoning that culminates in a testable RQ/hypothesis about VRET vs. IVET in adolescents. References are largely relevant and fairly up to date, supporting the justification for the study. To reach higher bands, the argumentation would benefit from more precise linking between the proposed adolescent-specific mechanism (comfort/familiarity) and the specific outcome predictions, and from making the research question/hypothesis even more explicitly stated as a direct comparison suitable for the later design.
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods are mostly complete and coherent with the stated aim: design, diagnosis, random assignment to three conditions, treatment dose (sessions and timing), and the primary measure (LSAS-CA) are clearly described. Participants’ inclusion/exclusion criteria and key VR/IVET procedure elements are provided, and the analytic plan is consistent with the design. For an even higher band, you could add a bit more procedural detail needed for replication (e.g., therapist/task fidelity, blinding/assessment procedure, and specifics on waitlist handling beyond later randomization), while avoiding any potentially unclear operationalizations (e.g., “until their anxiety decreased”).
results_rubric_feedback: Results reporting includes an appropriate mixed ANOVA framework, checks (normality/homoscedasticity), and post-hoc comparisons that generally match the design and hypotheses. However, statistical reporting is incomplete: effect sizes, confidence intervals, assumption handling when normality is violated, and clear description of the omnibus interaction/post-hoc correction details are missing or under-specified. The results also include some potentially problematic interpretation/wording (e.g., stating outcomes “matched expectations” despite non-significant VRET vs IVET), and does not demonstrate in-depth statistical reasoning beyond significance tests.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly summarizes the key finding pattern (both therapies outperform waitlist, but no VRET>IVET advantage) and provides plausible theoretical explanations linked to the study’s mechanisms. Critical reflection includes relevant limitations (VRET dialogue structure limiting realism; therapeutic alliance differences) and reasonable future directions (more interactive VR; measuring alliance; testing VRET+CBT). To score higher, the discussion should more explicitly integrate the exact statistical findings (including the interaction/main effects pattern and post-hoc comparisons) and strengthen the limitations-to-conclusion logic with more methodological specificity, plus ensure all claims remain tightly bounded by the presented results.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Overall scientific language is clear and mostly structured, with topic-relevant paragraphs and generally consistent terminology (VRET/IVET/SAD/LSAS-CA). Grammar and APA-style formatting are mostly acceptable, and references are formatted consistently. A few issues reduce polish (occasional imprecise phrasing and some clutter/formatting problems in tables/figures like alignment and headings), and minor ambiguities appear in crucial places (e.g., operational details of exposure progression).

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction presents a clear line of reasoning from SAD’s relevance to exposure mechanisms, then narrows to a specific adolescent comparison question. The RQ/hypotheses are stated, but the rationale for why VRET should outperform IVET (beyond “comfort”) could be tightened and more directly linked to the proposed outcome pattern. References are generally relevant and mostly up to date, but a few claims would benefit from clearer grounding or more precise integration with the final comparison logic.
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods are largely coherent with the RQ: sample, diagnosis, random assignment, intervention structure, and an appropriate SAD measure are provided. However, important implementation details are missing or ambiguous (e.g., session therapist treatment fidelity/standardization across conditions, specifics of waitlist nature/dose control, blinding/assessment procedures, and attrition/flow information). Some methodological choices are justified only partially (e.g., handling non-normality and repeated-measures assumptions) and would need more completeness for full clarity.
results_rubric_feedback: The results reporting is partially coherent with the stated mixed ANOVA design and includes key inferential tests and post-hoc comparisons. However, there are major gaps in the statistical reporting: effect sizes and confidence intervals are missing, the handling of the violated normality assumption is not clearly justified beyond invoking the CLT, and the description of outliers does not connect to any robust/adjusted analysis. Additionally, the mixed ANOVA degrees of freedom usage and the stated post-hoc structure are not fully transparent, limiting confidence in the correctness and completeness of the analysis.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly interprets the main finding that both active conditions outperform waitlist, while VRET is not superior to IVET, aligning with the reported results. It provides critical reflection through plausible explanations and addresses at least some limitations (e.g., VRET realism and therapeutic alliance), plus reasonable future directions. Use of references is generally responsible, though some theoretical explanations could be more directly tied to measured variables/processes rather than inferred mechanisms.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is mostly clear and the paper uses appropriate terminology with generally consistent constructs (VRET/IVET, LSAS-CA, time points). The structure follows section conventions and readability is good, with limited grammar/spelling issues and generally acceptable APA-style formatting. However, there are some ambiguities and formatting rough edges (e.g., truncated/duplicated table headers, some methodological/statistical wording that is a bit unclear), which prevent it from reaching near-faultless clarity.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD prevalence and treatment gaps to classical/operant learning mechanisms and the rationale for VRET vs IVET, ending in a clear RQ/hypothesis. The theoretical framing is generally sound, and most citations are relevant and reasonably up to date. However, some logical links and assumptions (e.g., operant conditioning “law of effect” mapping, and claims about adolescents’ VR familiarity driving differential effects) are not tightly argued, and the RQ/hypothesis could be stated more precisely in one place before the aims.
methods_rubric_feedback: The method is broadly coherent with the stated RQ (VRET vs IVET vs waitlist), with clear information about recruitment, diagnoses, random assignment, session structure, and the primary outcome (LSAS-CA). Several important details are still missing or under-specified (e.g., trial registration/ethics statement, therapist training fidelity/monitoring, medication changes handling, specifics of therapist modelling in IVET, and handling of missing data/dropouts—if any). The overall procedures are described in a way that is understandable and mostly complete, but the level of detail is not consistently at the “complete and correct” standard.
results_rubric_feedback: Reporting includes the key inferential steps (mixed ANOVA structure, assumption checks, Holm-corrected post hoc comparisons) and the main findings align with the described analysis plan. Still, the results lack depth in statistical reasoning: effect sizes and confidence intervals are not reported, the handling of the violated normality assumption is not sufficiently justified (CLT is invoked but the exact robustness is not discussed), and there is no clear accounting of multiple comparisons strategy across the full set of tests. Additionally, the tables/figures are not fully presented in text (sample table values are awkwardly separated), which reduces clarity of what was actually analyzed.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly interprets the main pattern (both active conditions improved vs waitlist; no VRET superiority over IVET) and provides theory- and literature-based explanations for the null difference. Limitations are addressed in a reasonably relevant way (VRET dialogue structure, therapeutic alliance), and future directions are plausible and tied to the limitations. However, the discussion could be more rigorous in linking each limitation to specific outcomes/analyses (e.g., why dialogue-line structure would specifically eliminate differences), and some references are used in a broadly supportive way rather than carefully and precisely substantiating each claim.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally appropriate and the paper is readable, with clear sectioning into Introduction/Methods/Results/Discussion. However, there are some issues with clarity and precision (e.g., occasional vague formulations and a few terminology inconsistencies such as VRET/IVET definitions and the use of “operant conditioning” explanation), and some presentation/APA-like layout problems (e.g., tables/figures formatting and “IVET | VRET | Waitlist” blocks embedded at the end). Grammar/spelling is mostly fine, but the overall style is not near-faultless and includes enough small inconsistencies to fit the lower passing/adequate band.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction presents a clear line of reasoning from SAD relevance and treatment gap to the rationale for comparing VRET vs. IVET in adolescents, culminating in well-specified hypotheses. Most citations are relevant and generally up to date, and the theoretical mechanisms for exposure therapies are coherently connected to the predictions. To improve further, ensure the logical contribution of each referenced study is more precisely tied to the specific adolescents/VRET-vs-IVET question, and tighten any slightly overgeneral theoretical claims that are not fully supported by the cited evidence.
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods section is largely coherent with the stated RQ and hypotheses: participants, random assignment, key materials (VRET system and IVET contexts), dependent variable, and a session structure are described. However, several methodological details are missing or under-specified (e.g., therapist training specifics, randomization/allocations concealment details, handling of Shapiro-Wilk violation beyond stating CLT, and whether assessments were blinded), which prevents reaching the highest band. Reporting could also be improved by stating attrition/consent flow and specifying outcomes/analysis plan more explicitly.
results_rubric_feedback: The results address the stated design with an appropriate overall ANOVA framework, reporting main effects and the time×condition interaction, and presenting post-hoc comparisons aligned with the hypotheses. However, the statistical reporting is incomplete/unclear in places (e.g., degrees of freedom consistency, no effect sizes or confidence intervals, and limited description of assumptions/robustness beyond CLT). The discussion of outliers is minimal and there is no explicit statement whether corrections/robust methods were used, and the Results do not demonstrate deep statistical reasoning beyond p-values.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly summarizes the main findings, interprets them relative to prior work, and provides plausible theoretical explanations for the lack of VRET superiority. Limitations and future directions are included and mostly relevant (e.g., VRET dialogue realism/uncertainty, therapeutic alliance), and references are used responsibly. To reach a higher band, the critical reflection could be more systematically tied to specific design choices and the obtained statistical pattern (not just general plausibility), and additional or more directly tested alternative explanations could be considered.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable and the paper is structured into appropriate sections with mostly clear topic focus. There are some grammar/wording issues and occasional clarity problems (e.g., minor inconsistencies in terminology and phrasing, and some redundancy), but errors do not dominate comprehension. APA/formatting issues are present (e.g., missing/unclear Figure/Table presentation details and minor layout inconsistencies), keeping it from the highest language/style band.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction presents a clear line of reasoning toward the research question and hypothesis, linking SAD mechanisms to exposure-based interventions and to the rationale for VRET versus IVET. Most references are relevant and fairly up to date, and the need for the adolescent-focused comparison with an RCT is well motivated. Some argument steps could be tightened (e.g., stronger justification of why VRET should outperform IVET given the described mechanisms), and the research gap could be stated more explicitly as an RQ-level contrast (what exactly is being tested beyond “effectiveness”).
methods_rubric_feedback: The method is generally coherent and largely complete: recruitment, diagnosis, assignment to conditions, key instruments, treatment dose, and basic session structure are described in a way that supports replication. However, some crucial operational details are missing or not sufficiently specified (e.g., specific randomization/sequence concealment, therapist-competence checks, adherence/implementation fidelity, exact waitlist procedure, and how LSAS-CA change was handled when assumptions were violated). The statistical plan/assumption handling is not aligned with the results section (e.g., normality/CLT rationale vs. what alternative analyses were considered).
results_rubric_feedback: The reporting provides the main ANOVA effects and several post-hoc comparisons, showing a mostly coherent mapping to the factorial mixed design. However, key analysis details are missing or questionable: the handling of the violated normality assumption is not really justified by the analysis approach taken, effect sizes (and confidence intervals) are absent, degrees of freedom and model specification are not fully transparent, and the ANOVA/degrees-of-freedom logic appears inconsistent with N=90. Also, the paper claims results “matched expectations” even though some hypothesis predictions are not supported, and it does not address multiple-testing/statistical correction clearly beyond mentioning Holm post-hoc tests.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion correctly interprets the main findings (both active treatments outperform waitlist, but VRET is not superior to IVET) and ties them back to theory and prior work. Study limitations are included and are at least partially convincing (e.g., fidelity/realism constraints and therapeutic alliance plausibility), and future directions are proposed that directly address those limitations. Some reference use is a bit mechanistic/associative rather than clearly evidence-anchored to the specific explanation, and deeper consideration of alternative explanations (e.g., equivalence/measurement sensitivity, demand effects, or differential homework/fidelity) is limited.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally appropriate and readable, and the paper is structured with clear sectioning and mostly consistent terminology (VRET/IVET, LSAS-CA, pre/post). There are some language/APA issues and a few imprecise or awkward formulations (e.g., definition-heavy parentheses, occasional phrasing that blurs causal vs. correlational claims), and some statements are vague (e.g., about “matched expectations” despite non-support for a specific hypothesis). Formatting of tables/figures is also somewhat inconsistent (e.g., headings and figure/table captions), and some sentences could be tightened for clarity.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a relevant topic and a recognizable clinical motivation, and it does culminate in an RQ/hypothesis comparing self-guided vs therapist-guided VRET. However, the line of reasoning is sometimes under-justified or conceptually imprecise (e.g., how exposure mechanisms are described), and several transitions rely on assertions rather than tightly linked evidence. The final hypothesis is stated, but the introduction should more explicitly connect existing findings to the specific predicted pattern across time and condition, with cleaner separation of background vs study rationale.
methods_rubric_feedback: The method is largely coherent and includes key elements: diagnosis procedure (SCID-5), randomized assignment, materials for both conditions, and a clear session schedule and measurement points. Participants, inclusion/exclusion criteria, and the primary outcome measure are described comprehensibly, enabling replication in broad strokes. That said, several methodological details are missing or unclear for a full scientific paper standard (e.g., session structure/content specifics, therapist/vtherapist procedural standardization, adherence/engagement metrics, and how guidance/steps are operationalized), and the randomization/stratification description is not clearly consistent with the reported analyses.
results_rubric_feedback: The results section reports an appropriate mixed ANOVA framework for the stated design and includes key F, p, and effect-size values, plus a corresponding post-hoc logic. However, reporting is incomplete/inconsistent: the table is not clearly readable/complete (including repeated blocks and missing/unclear SEs), and the ANOVA degrees of freedom appear inconsistent with the described number of levels and outlier treatment. The outlier-handling decision is stated but not integrated into the analysis plan (e.g., whether robust checks were done), and some inferential statements in the interaction description/post-hoc comparisons are internally hard to evaluate.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion provides an overall, mostly correct interpretation that both groups improve, with therapist-guided showing a larger immediate reduction and convergence by follow-up, aligning with the reported time comparisons. Critical reflection is present and addresses plausible threats to validity (response bias and contextual effects), and future directions are sensible. Yet the mechanistic explanation (self-efficacy) is plausible but not directly tested, and some conclusions rely on earlier inferential claims that are not fully supported by the clarity/consistency of the statistical reporting.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally understandable and mostly consistent with psychology writing conventions, and paragraphing is typically topic-focused. However, there are noticeable APA/layout and formatting problems (e.g., reference/layout irregularities, table issues, and duplicated fragments), plus several grammar/wording issues that reduce polish. Some redundancy and occasional ambiguous phrasing appear in crucial parts, which limits the “near faultless” clarity expected for the top rubric bands.
final_grade_justification: Overall, the paper shows a generally coherent experimental question and a mostly complete, feasible method with clear operationalization of the main outcome (LSAS-SR) and a straightforward mixed-design ANOVA. However, scientific reasoning and argumentation are weakened by some unclear/incorrect statements (e.g., role of Pavlovian extinction, interpretation of interaction, and presentation of “confirmatory analyses” without clarity) and by incomplete or inconsistent reporting in key places (tables/SEs, outlier handling rationale, and missing numerical details needed to evaluate the claims). Results and discussion largely align with a plausible narrative that both conditions improve, but the statistical reporting and interpretation are not fully rigorous or consistent, reducing confidence in the conclusions. Language/APA quality is variable and sometimes undermines clarity (e.g., formatting issues and repetition), keeping the paper in the mid-range band rather than “Good/Excellent.”

intro_rubric_feedback: The Introduction provides a mostly coherent line of reasoning from SAD severity and treatment barriers to the need to compare therapist-guided and self-guided VRET, culminating in a relevant RQ/hypothesis. The scientific relevance of some claims (e.g., comparisons of effectiveness and the Pavlovian explanation) is present but not always tightly argued or clearly linked to the specific design. Several references are used responsibly, but the rationale would benefit from clearer justification of why the expected direction at post-treatment is theoretically and empirically grounded.
methods_rubric_feedback: The Methods section is largely complete and the design matches the stated comparison, including diagnosis procedure, random assignment, materials, and measurement time points. Participants, exclusion criteria, and session structure are described in a way that is mostly comprehensible, and the LSAS-SR description supports replication. Still, details that are important for scientific reasoning and internal validity (e.g., specifics of VR exposure content and delivery standardization, therapist training/consistency, adherence/engagement monitoring in the self-guided arm, and analytic plan alignment with the RQ) would need more precision to reach the top rubric bands.
results_rubric_feedback: The Results include an appropriate broad analysis strategy (mixed-design ANOVA) and report several key test statistics and post-hoc directions. Nevertheless, the reporting is not fully reliable: there are inconsistencies in the table presentation, potential issues with how outliers were handled/justified, and the degrees of freedom and interaction statement do not clearly align with the stated 3-level within-subject factor. Because the evidence depends on correct statistical reasoning, these problems prevent the results from fitting into the upper rubric bands.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The Discussion is generally coherent: it restates the main findings, offers theoretically motivated explanations (e.g., self-efficacy), and connects claims to prior VRET literature. It also provides relevant critical reflection on measurement context and generalisability, with concrete suggestions for future research. However, the argument would be stronger if it more explicitly addressed how the results/statistical anomalies (as reported) affect the confidence in the interpretation, and if alternative explanations were considered with tighter linkage to the actual analyses.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is mostly understandable and uses scientific terminology appropriately, with a generally clear paragraph structure and topical focus. There are some grammar and clarity issues (e.g., missing/incorrect punctuation, occasional awkward phrasing, and several run-on sentences) that slightly reduce readability. APA layout/formatting is partially inconsistent (e.g., reference formatting and some table/text artifacts), which keeps it from the highest language/style rubric band.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows an overall coherent research question and a plausible hypothesis comparing therapist-guided versus self-guided VRET, with generally responsible use of sources in the Introduction. However, the Results section contains major methodological/analytic inconsistencies and reporting issues (e.g., incorrect/missing table details, outlier handling without a clear impact on analyses, and questionable ANOVA degrees of freedom and interaction reporting relative to the stated three time points), which undermines confidence in the statistical conclusions. The Discussion provides relevant limitations and plausible explanations, but it does not fully reconcile the analysis/reporting problems with the interpretation. Overall, it fits around the “developing”/“adequate” midpoint, with scientific reasoning partially strong but execution of evidence-based argumentation weakened by the results reporting.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly coherent rationale culminating in a clear comparison between therapist-guided and self-guided VRET, and it frames the study as addressing an identifiable gap. The scientific relevance is generally appropriate, and references are used with some responsibility, but the argumentation sometimes becomes imprecise (e.g., causal statements about therapists and extinction/context) and the hypothesis section is not explicitly tied back to the strongest evidence reviewed. The line to the RQ/hypothesis would be stronger with tighter mapping between prior findings and the expected pattern across time (post vs follow-up).
methods_rubric_feedback: The methods section is largely complete: participants (diagnostic approach and exclusions), materials (VR setup and therapist support distinction), and procedure (randomization, session structure, measurement time points) are described in a way that is mostly replicable. Randomization is specified and the measurement instrument is adequately characterized. Clarity could improve by specifying the exact structure/content of the exposure exercises, therapist training/standardization, and what “built-in virtual therapist guidance” entails in concrete terms (e.g., scripts, feedback rules).
results_rubric_feedback: The statistical approach is generally appropriate for the design (mixed ANOVA across time with a between-subject condition) and results are reported with effect sizes and post-hoc comparisons. However, the handling/reporting is not fully rigorous: the description of the outliers is inconsistent with the time factor structure and outliers are retained without criteria or sensitivity analyses, and there are inconsistencies/ambiguities around the reported table contents. Additionally, some degrees of freedom and interaction interpretation are not described with enough precision to fully validate the inferential claims.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion reaches a conclusion consistent with the reported ANOVA pattern (both improve; therapist-guided more immediately, convergence by follow-up) and it provides relevant critical reflections on internal validity (response/demand context) and external validity (student sample). The limitation-to-effect explanations are sometimes speculative (e.g., self-efficacy mechanism) and would benefit from more careful alignment with the observed time course and alternative explanations. Future research suggestions are constructive, though they could more directly target the most consequential threats identified.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Language is understandable and mostly maintains a scientific register, with generally structured paragraphs in each section. There are, however, noticeable APA/layout and consistency issues (e.g., spacing, punctuation, minor typos/inconsistencies in condition naming, and incomplete/garbled table presentation). Some wording is vague in crucial interpretive passages, and redundancy appears in places (especially around summarizing similar findings across time), which reduces clarity.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows a generally coherent scientific line from SAD and VRET rationale to a clear comparative research question and postulated effect direction. Methods are mostly complete and aligned with the aim (participants, random assignment, measures, and exposure schedule are described), and Results contain an appropriate mixed-design ANOVA with interpretable effects and post-hoc follow-up. However, scientific reasoning is weakened by an incomplete/unclear handling of the stated interaction and by discrepancies/ambiguities in reporting (e.g., outliers handling without formal sensitivity analysis; some condition/table inconsistencies; keeping outliers based on an ANOVA decision without justification grounded in criteria). The Discussion provides relevant limitations tied to internal validity and generalizability, but some explanations are speculative and the “context/extinction” argument is asserted more than demonstrated. Overall, the work fits the “Adequate–Good” bands, with the main deductions coming from shortcomings in analytic/reporting precision and the robustness of the argumentation.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a reasonable line of reasoning from SAD prevalence and treatment barriers to the rationale for comparing therapist-guided versus self-guided VRET, culminating in a comparison-based research question/hypothesis. The scientific relevance is generally clear, but the path from prior evidence to the specific form/expectation of the post-treatment hypothesis could be argued more precisely (e.g., specifying why the guided advantage should be larger specifically at post-test). The references are mostly used responsibly, though there is some imprecision in how claims are attributed and connected to the cited sources.
methods_rubric_feedback: The method section is largely coherent and includes key components: participants, diagnostic procedure, materials, treatment structure, and outcome measurement at baseline/post/follow-up. Still, key scientific-reasoning details that strengthen replicability and internal validity are not fully specified (e.g., adherence/compliance monitoring for home use, therapist standardization and potential therapist expectancy effects, and how outliers were identified/handled statistically beyond “kept”). Overall, the design matches the stated RQ/hypothesis at a broad level, but reporting completeness is not at the “Good/Excellent” band for methodological rigor.
results_rubric_feedback: Results reporting is mostly coherent: a mixed-design ANOVA is described, with main effects and an interaction followed by post-hoc comparisons. However, the statistical reporting contains internal inconsistencies (notably the ANOVA degrees of freedom relative to the described number of time points) and the treatment of outliers is stated without sufficient detail about impact/sensitivity analyses. Effect sizes are reported, which is a strength, but the overall correctness/completeness cannot reach the “Good/Excellent” band due to these ambiguities.
discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion provides a mostly correct reasoning chain from results to interpretation, and it includes critical reflection on response bias, generalizability, and context effects that are relevant to the design. The explanation for the self-guided follow-up improvement (self-efficacy) is plausible and connects to theory, but could be more tightly evaluated against alternative explanations (e.g., regression to the mean, measurement timing effects, or differential engagement). References are used responsibly, and limitations and future directions are generally convincing, aligning with the mid rubric bands.
lang_style_rubric_feedback: Scientific language is generally clear and the structure is readable, with topical paragraphs and understandable transitions. There are noticeable APA/layout issues in the reference list and some minor grammatical/redundancy problems, plus some vague phrasing in places where precision would help (e.g., around statistical conclusions and mechanism). Overall comprehension is not hindered, but the style/editing does not reach the near-faultless standard of the highest rubric band.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows an overall coherent study aim with a clear comparison between therapist-guided and self-guided VRET, and the Discussion provides mostly plausible reasoning and relevant limitations. However, scientific argumentation is weakened by a lack of a precise hypothesis tied to an explicitly defined analytic model and by inconsistencies/oddities in the Methods–Results reporting (e.g., time factor degrees of freedom/number of levels and outlier handling) that reduce confidence in the statistical conclusions. Methods are largely complete and appropriate for an RCT, but several procedural details relevant to internal validity (e.g., therapist effects, adherence/compliance in home use, blinding, and handling of outliers beyond stating they are kept) are missing. Language is generally understandable with moderate APA/reference/layout issues. Overall, the work fits the “Adequate” to “Developing” bands for Methods/Results, and the “Adequate” band for Introduction/Discussion, yielding a mid-range holistic grade.

intro_rubric_feedback: The introduction provides a mostly clear line of reasoning toward a relevant RQ by moving from SAD impact to VRET and the therapist-guidance question, ending with a testable hypothesis. methods_rubric_feedback: The method is well organized and includes key details: diagnostic procedure, sample characteristics, randomization, VR apparatus/software, therapist vs self-guided difference, and measurement timing. The procedure is largely complete and understandable, and it includes some attempt at standardization (session length/number; exposure progression). However, several important methodological elements for scientific rigor are missing or unclear (e.g., therapist training/role specifics, adherence/compliance monitoring for self-guided home use, exact VR exercise content/protocol, and analytic handling of the stated outliers beyond “kept”).
results_rubric_feedback: Results reporting includes an appropriate overall model choice (mixed-design ANOVA) and descriptive statistics by time/condition, plus an attempt at follow-up tests with Bonferroni correction. discussion_rubric_feedback: The discussion connects the findings back to the hypothesis and compares them to prior work, and it offers several relevant limitations that could plausibly affect interpretation (context of post-test, generalizability, exposure context). Some sentences are vague or redundant, and there are a few unclear phrasing points that make parts of the argument/statistical interpretation harder to follow.
final_grade_justification: The paper shows an overall coherent, testable research aim and compares the two conditions directly, but the introduction and hypothesis are sometimes under-specified in terms of theoretical precision and expected effect magnitude. The methods section is largely complete and internally consistent (participants, randomization, measures, session structure), supporting Adequate-to-Good scientific execution. Results reporting is mostly plausible but has major coherence/statistical issues (e.g., time factor degrees of freedom inconsistent with three timepoints; post-hoc p-value pattern not fully aligned with the stated interaction; an unexplained and potentially unaddressed outlier handling decision), preventing an “Excellent” rating.
